metadata.title: Topology Knowledge Graph Dataset
metadata.description: Topology content nodes with enriched statements/explanations and first-class directed dependency/provenance edges, updated from topology_.json.

fields.id: Stable slug identifier for the node.
fields.kind: Mathematical content type such as theorem, definition, lemma, proposition, corollary, example, exercise, or remark.
fields.number: Displayed item number from the source text.
fields.title: Human-readable title.
fields.chapter: Source chapter label; numeric chapters and single-letter appendix labels are supported.
fields.section: Source section label; numeric sections and single-letter appendix labels are supported.
fields.originalText: Cleaned source text, with Unicode math converted where possible to MathJax/LaTeX.
fields.statementDependencies: IDs of nodes needed to understand the statement.
fields.proofDependencies: IDs of nodes used in the proof or justification.
fields.edge.label: Human-readable edge relation.
fields.qualityFlags: Review or quality annotations for a node.
fields.mergedFrom: Source fragment(s) merged into this node during cleanup.
fields.edge.rationale: Human-readable reason for the directed edge.
fields.edge.review: Review flags and human-review status for the edge.
fields.edge.semanticRelation: Mathematical/provenance relation within the app-compatible relation enum.
fields.mathematicalFormula: Optional concise KaTeX-ready formula string, stored without outer math delimiters.

nodes.title: Brouwer Fixed Point Theorem
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐷𝑛 → 𝐷𝑛 be a continuous map from the (unit) disk in ℝ𝑛 to itself. Then 𝑓 has a fixed point, i.e., there is some point 𝑥 ∈ 𝐷𝑛 such that 𝑓 (𝑥) = 𝑥.

For 𝑛 = 1 this is a well-known result from calculus: The graph of any continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [0, 1] → [0, 1] must cross the diagonal 𝑦 = 𝑥 for some 𝑥∗ ∈ [0, 1]. Hence, 𝑓 (𝑥∗ ) = 𝑥∗ . See Figure 1.4. The second theorem is the fundamental theorem of algebra.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $f : D^n \to D^n$ be a continuous map from the unit disk in $\mathbb{R}^n$ to itself. Then there exists $x \in D^n$ such that $f(x)=x$.
nodes.explanation: The theorem asserts that every continuous self-map of the closed unit disk has a fixed point. In the introduction it is used as a motivating example: in dimension one, the graph of a continuous map $[0,1]\to[0,1]$ must cross the diagonal.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:D^n\to D^n\text{ continuous }\Longrightarrow \exists x\in D^n:\ f(x)=x

nodes.title: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
nodes.originalText:
A polynomial equation

𝑧𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 = 0

of degree 𝑛 > 0 with complex coefficients has at least one complex root.

Chapter 1. Introduction 7

𝑦 (1, 1) ∙

𝑦=𝑥 𝑦 = 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑥

𝑥∗ 𝑥

Figure 1.4: The graph of any continuous map from [0, 1] to [0, 1] must cross the diagonal.

To prove it we will use the fact that the fundamental group of the circle is isomorphic to the group of integers. The fundamental theorem of algebra may be proved in many different ways, including using only algebraic techniques and analysis. However, the proof we will provide (based on [4, §56]) is a fairly simple corollary of the computation of the fundamental group of the circle.

2. Continuous maps

2.1 Metric spaces From calculus we know what to mean by a continuous map from ℝ𝑛 to ℝ𝑚 : a map 𝑓 ∶ ℝ𝑛 → ℝ𝑚 is continuous at 𝑝 ∈ ℝ𝑛 if for all 𝜖 > 0 there exists a 𝛿 > 0 such that if ‖𝑝 − 𝑞‖ℝ𝑛 < 𝛿, then ‖𝑓 (𝑝) − 𝑓 (𝑞)‖ℝ𝑚 < 𝜖. Here ‖ ⋅ ‖ℝ𝑛 denotes the Euclidean norm in ℝ𝑛 . Similarly, ‖ ⋅ ‖ℝ𝑚 denotes the Euclidean norm in ℝ𝑚 . Topological spaces provide the most general setting for which the concept of continuity makes sense. Before we get to the concept of a topological space, let us consider metric spaces. Metric spaces allow us to speak of distance between elements. Using the notion of distance between elements we can make sense of continuity of maps between metric spaces.
nodes.formalStatement: Every nonconstant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one complex root. Equivalently, every polynomial equation $z^n+a_{n-1}z^{n-1}+\cdots+a_1z+a_0=0$ with $n>0$ has a solution in $\mathbb{C}$.
nodes.explanation: This introductory theorem is presented as a later application of topology: the text notes that it will be proved using the computation of the fundamental group of the circle.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: p(z)=z^n+a_{n-1}z^{n-1}+\cdots+a_1z+a_0,\ n>0\Longrightarrow \exists z\in\mathbb{C}:p(z)=0

nodes.title: Metric spaces
nodes.originalText:
A metric space (𝑋, 𝑑) is a non-empty set 𝑋 together with a map 𝑑 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝑋 → ℝ called a metric such that the following properties hold:

M1 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) ⩾ 0 for all 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋, and 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = 0 if and only if 𝑥 = 𝑦;

M2 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑑(𝑦, 𝑥) for all 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋;

M3 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑧) ⩽ 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) + 𝑑(𝑦, 𝑧) for all 𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑧 ∈ 𝑋.

The first condition says that the distance between two elements is always positive, and equal to zero if and only if the two elements are the same. The second condition says that distance is symmetric. The third condition says that the triangle inequality holds. The metric 𝑑 is sometimes also referred to as a distance function.

2.2 (ℝ𝑛 seen as a metric space) Let 𝑋 = ℝ and 𝑑 be the map defined by 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = Example √ |𝑥 − 𝑦|(= (𝑥 − 𝑦)2 ). The first two requirements for 𝑑 are clearly satisfied, and the third follows from the usual triangle inequality for real numbers,

𝑑(𝑥, 𝑧) = |𝑥 − 𝑧| = |(𝑥 − 𝑦) + (𝑦 − 𝑧)| ⩽ |𝑥 − 𝑦| + |𝑦 − 𝑧| = 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) + 𝑑(𝑦, 𝑧).

For 𝑋 = ℝ𝑛 with 𝑛 > 0 an integer, √ let 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = ‖𝑥 − 𝑦‖ where ‖ ⋅ ‖ is the Euclidean norm, e.g., for 𝑛 = 2, 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = ‖𝑥 − 𝑦‖ = (𝑥1 − 𝑦1 )2 + (𝑥2 − 𝑦2 )2 . Again, the first two requirements for 𝑑 are clearly satisfied. The third requirement follows from the triangle inequality for vectors in ℝ𝑛 .

We may equip ℝ𝑛 with other metrics than the one described in Example 2.2. For instance, for 𝑋 = ℝ2 , let 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = |𝑥1 − 𝑦1 | + |𝑥2 − 𝑦2 |.

This is known as the taxi cab metric.

Chapter 2. Continuous maps 9

We say that two metrics 𝑑1 and 𝑑2 on the same set 𝑋 are equivalent if there are constants 𝐿 and 𝑀 such that 𝑑1 (𝑥, 𝑦) ⩽ 𝐿𝑑2 (𝑥, 𝑦) and 𝑑2 (𝑥, 𝑦) ⩽ 𝑀𝑑1 (𝑥, 𝑦)

for all 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: A metric space is a pair $(X,d)$ where $X$ is a nonempty set and $d:X\times X\to\mathbb{R}$ satisfies, for all $x,y,z\in X$: $d(x,y)\geq 0$ with equality if and only if $x=y$; $d(x,y)=d(y,x)$; and $d(x,z)\leq d(x,y)+d(y,z)$.
nodes.explanation: A metric space is a set equipped with a distance function. The three axioms give positivity, symmetry, and the triangle inequality, making metric spaces the starting point for continuity and open-set definitions.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: d:X\times X\to\mathbb{R},\quad d(x,y)\ge 0,\quad d(x,y)=0\iff x=y,\quad d(x,y)=d(y,x),\quad d(x,z)\le d(x,y)+d(y,z)

nodes.title: Discrete metric spaces
nodes.originalText: For any set 𝑋, let 𝑑 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝑋 → ℝ be the map given by { 1 𝑥 ≠ 𝑦, 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = 0 𝑥 = 𝑦. We call 𝑑 the discrete metric on 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: For any set $X$, define $d:X\times X\to\mathbb{R}$ by $d(x,y)=0$ if $x=y$ and $d(x,y)=1$ if $x\neq y$. This is the discrete metric on $X$.
nodes.explanation: The discrete metric gives every distinct pair of points distance one. It is the simplest example of a metric space and later makes open balls especially easy to compute.

nodes.title: C[a,b] with the Sup Metric
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 = 𝐶[𝑎, 𝑏], i.e., the set of continuous maps from the interval 𝐼 = [𝑎, 𝑏] ⊆ ℝ to ℝ, and let 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = max |𝑥(𝑖) − 𝑦(𝑖)|. 𝑖∈𝐼
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X=C[a,b]$ be the set of continuous maps from $[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{R}$. The function $d(x,y)=\max_{i\in[a,b]} |x(i)-y(i)|$ defines a metric on $X$.
nodes.explanation: This example puts a metric on a function space using maximum pointwise distance. It shows that metric spaces need not consist of points in Euclidean space.

nodes.title: Restricted Metric on a Subset
nodes.originalText:
If 𝑑 is a metric on a set 𝑋, and 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑋 is any subset of 𝑋, then 𝑑 is also a metric on 𝐴.

2.2 Continuous maps between metric spaces The definition of continuity of maps between metric spaces is completely analogous to the situation that we have from calculus.
nodes.formalStatement: If $d$ is a metric on a set $X$ and $A\subseteq X$, then the restriction of $d$ to $A\times A$ is a metric on $A$.
nodes.explanation: A metric can be restricted to any subset of its space. This is the metric-space precursor to later subspace constructions.

nodes.title: Continuous maps between metric spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑𝑋 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑑𝑌 ) be two metric spaces. A map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous at 𝑝 ∈ 𝑋 if for all 𝜖 > 0 there is a 𝛿 > 0 such that if 𝑑𝑋 (𝑝, 𝑞) < 𝛿 then 𝑑𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑝), 𝑓 (𝑞)) < 𝜖. If 𝑓 is continuous at every 𝑝 ∈ 𝑋, we say that 𝑓 is continuous.

To get us to the setting of topological spaces we will need the concept of open and closed sets.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces. A map $f:X\to Y$ is continuous at $p\in X$ if for every $\epsilon>0$ there exists $\delta>0$ such that $d_X(p,q)<\delta$ implies $d_Y(f(p),f(q))<\epsilon$. The map $f$ is continuous if it is continuous at every point of $X$.
nodes.explanation: Continuity between metric spaces is the epsilon-delta condition expressed using the two metrics. This definition is the metric version that will later be reformulated using open sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f\text{ continuous at }p\iff \forall \varepsilon>0\ \exists \delta>0\ \forall q\in X,\ d_X(p,q)<\delta\Rightarrow d_Y(f(p),f(q))<\varepsilon

nodes.title: Open and closed balls
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space, and let 𝑎 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝑟 > 0 be real number. The open ball centered at 𝑎 with radius 𝑟 is the subset

𝖡(𝑎; 𝑟) = {𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑎) < 𝑟}

of 𝑋. The closed ball centered at 𝑎 with radius 𝑟 is the subset

𝖡(𝑎; 𝑟) = {𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑎) ⩽ 𝑟}

of 𝑋.

In Euclidean space with the usual metric (induced from Euclidean norm), a ball (as defined above) is precisely what we think of as a ball in everyday language. Open balls are sometimes referred to as simply balls, and closed balls are sometimes referred to as discs, e.g. Theorem 1.1.

10 2.2. Continuous maps between metric spaces
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $a\in X$, and let $r>0$. The open ball centered at $a$ with radius $r$ is $B(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)<r\}$, and the closed ball centered at $a$ with radius $r$ is $\overline{B}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)\leq r\}$.
nodes.explanation: Open and closed balls are the basic local neighborhoods determined by a metric. They are used to define open sets and to express continuity at a point.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: B(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)<r\},\qquad \overline{B}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)\le r\}

nodes.title: Open balls in discrete metric spaces
nodes.originalText: Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be the metric space defined in Example 2.3. Then 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟1 ) = {𝑥} and 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟2 ) = 𝑋 for all 0 < 𝑟1 ⩽ 1 and all 𝑟2 > 1.
nodes.formalStatement: In a discrete metric space $(X,d)$, $B(x;r)=\{x\}$ for $0<r\leq 1$, while $B(x;r)=X$ for $r>1$.
nodes.explanation: This example computes metric balls in the discrete metric. It shows how the radius controls whether an open ball isolates one point or contains the whole space.

nodes.title: Open and closed sets
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space. A subset 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑋 is open in 𝑋 if for every point 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴, there is an open ball 𝖡(𝑎; 𝑟) about 𝑎 contained in 𝐴. We say that 𝐴 is closed in 𝑋 if the complement 𝐴𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝐴 = {𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑥 ∉ 𝐴} is open.

Most subsets are neither open nor closed. Subsets that are both open and closed are sometimes referred to as clopen. In particular, both ∅ and 𝑋 are clopen in 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space. A subset $A\subseteq X$ is open in $X$ if for every $a\in A$ there exists an open ball $B(a;r)$ with $B(a;r)\subseteq A$. The subset $A$ is closed in $X$ if its complement $A^c=X\setminus A$ is open.
nodes.explanation: Open sets are subsets containing a metric ball around each of their points. Closed sets are defined by open complements, and this language prepares the transition from metric spaces to topological spaces.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: A\text{ open}\iff \forall a\in A\ \exists r>0\text{ such that }B(a;r)\subseteq A,\qquad A\text{ closed}\iff X\setminus A\text{ is open}

nodes.title: Metric Balls Are Open or Closed
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space, 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝑟 > 0 a real number. Then the open ball 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝑋 is open in 𝑋, and the closed ball 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝑋 is closed in 𝑋.

Proof. We prove the statement about open balls. The statement about closed balls follows from a similar argument. Assume that 𝑦 ∈ 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟). We need to prove that there is an open ball 𝖡(𝑦; 𝜖) about 𝑦 that is contained in 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟). Let 𝜖 = 𝑟 − 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦). By the triangle inequality of the metric 𝑑, M3, we have that for 𝑧 ∈ 𝖡(𝑦; 𝜖),

𝑑(𝑥, 𝑧) ⩽ 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) + 𝑑(𝑦, 𝑧) < 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) + 𝜖 = 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) + 𝑟 − 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑟.

Hence, 𝖡(𝑦; 𝜖) ⊆ 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟).

For a metric space (𝑋, 𝑑), a subset 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑋 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, we say that: (i) 𝑥 is an interior point of 𝐴 if there is an open ball 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) about 𝑥 which is contained in 𝐴, (ii) 𝑥 is an exterior point of 𝐴 if there is an open ball 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) which is contained in 𝐴𝑐 and (iii) 𝑥 is a boundary point if all open balls about 𝑥 contains points in 𝐴 and in 𝐴𝑐 . Hence, 𝐴 is open in 𝑋 if and only if 𝐴 only consists of its interior points. An interior point will always belong to 𝐴. An exterior point will never belong to 𝐴. A boundary point will some times belong to 𝐴, and some times to 𝐴𝑐 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $x\in X$, and let $r>0$. Then the open ball $B(x;r)$ is open in $X$, and the closed ball $\overline{B}(x;r)$ is closed in $X$.
nodes.explanation: This lemma verifies that the basic metric balls behave as their names suggest. The proof for open balls uses the triangle inequality to fit a smaller ball around any chosen point.

nodes.title: Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText: Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space, 𝐴 a subset of 𝑋 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is a neighborhood of 𝑥 if there is an open ball about 𝑥 that is contained in 𝐴. We say that 𝐴 is an open neighborhood (of 𝑥) if 𝐴 itself is open.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $A\subseteq X$, and let $x\in X$. The set $A$ is a neighborhood of $x$ if there is an open ball about $x$ contained in $A$. It is an open neighborhood of $x$ if $A$ itself is open.
nodes.explanation: A neighborhood records the idea that a set contains some open ball around a point. This gives a pointwise way to discuss local behavior in metric spaces.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: A\text{ is a neighborhood of }x\iff \exists r>0\text{ such that }B(x;r)\subseteq A

nodes.title: Continuity at a point
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑𝑋 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑑𝑌 ) be two metric spaces, and let 𝑝 ∈ 𝑋. A map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous at 𝑝 if and only if for all neighborhoods 𝐵 of 𝑓 (𝑝), there is a neighborhood 𝐴 of 𝑝 such that 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝐵.

Chapter 2. Continuous maps 11

Proof. Assume that 𝑓 is continuous at 𝑝. If 𝐵 is a neighborhood of 𝑓 (𝑝), then, by definition, there is an open ball 𝖡𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑝); 𝜖) about 𝑓 (𝑝) that is contained in 𝐵. Since 𝑓 is continuous at 𝑝, there is a 𝛿 > 0 such that if 𝑑𝑋 (𝑝, 𝑞) < 𝛿, then 𝑑𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑝), 𝑓 (𝑞)) < 𝜖. Hence, 𝑓 (𝖡𝑋 (𝑝; 𝛿)) ⊆ 𝖡𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑝); 𝜖) ⊆ 𝐵. That is, if we let 𝐴 = 𝖡𝑋 (𝑝, 𝛿), then for all neighborhoods 𝐵 of 𝑓 (𝑝), we have that 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝐵 where 𝐴 is a neighborhood of 𝑝. Assume that for all neighborhoods 𝐵 of 𝑓 (𝑝), there is a neighborhood 𝐴 of 𝑝 such that 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝐵. We need to prove that for all 𝜖 > 0, there is a 𝛿 > 0 such that if 𝑑𝑋 (𝑝, 𝑞) < 𝛿, then 𝑑𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑝), 𝑓 (𝑞)) < 𝜖. By utilizing the fact that 𝐵 = 𝖡𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑝); 𝜖) is a neighborhood of 𝑓 (𝑝), then, by assumption, there must be a neighborhood 𝐴 of 𝑝 such that 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝐵. Since 𝐴 is a neighborhood of 𝑝, there is an open ball 𝖡𝑋 (𝑝; 𝛿) about 𝑝 that is contained in 𝐴. Now assume that 𝑑𝑋 (𝑝, 𝑝′ ) < 𝛿. Then 𝑝′ ∈ 𝖡𝑋 (𝑝; 𝛿) ⊆ 𝐴. Thus 𝑓 (𝑝′ ) ∈ 𝐵 = 𝖡𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑝); 𝜖), and hence, 𝑑𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑝), 𝑓 (𝑝′ )) < 𝜖. Thus 𝑓 is continuous at 𝑝.

The following theorem gives an alternative description of continuous maps between metric spaces.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces, let $p\in X$, and let $f:X\to Y$. Then $f$ is continuous at $p$ if and only if for every neighborhood $B$ of $f(p)$ there exists a neighborhood $A$ of $p$ such that $f(A)\subseteq B$.
nodes.explanation: This theorem recasts epsilon-delta continuity using neighborhoods. The proof translates neighborhoods into open balls and then applies the metric definition of continuity.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f\text{ continuous at }p\iff \forall B\in\mathcal{N}(f(p)),\ \exists A\in\mathcal{N}(p):\ f(A)\subseteq B

nodes.title: Preimage Criterion for Metric Continuity
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑𝑋 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑑𝑌 ) be two metric spaces. A map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous if and only if for every subset 𝐵 ⊆ 𝑌 open in 𝑌 , the preimage of 𝐵 under 𝑓 ,

𝑓 −1 (𝐵) = {𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑓 (𝑥) ∈ 𝐵} ⊆ 𝑋,

is open in 𝑋.

Proof. Assume that 𝑓 is continuous. For 𝐵 ⊆ 𝑌 open in 𝑌 , we have to prove that 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) ⊆ 𝑋 is open in 𝑋. Let 𝑎 ∈ 𝑓 −1 (𝐵). We want to prove that there is an open ball about 𝑎 in 𝑋 that is contained in 𝑓 −1 (𝐵). By assumption, 𝐵 ⊆ 𝑌 is open in 𝑌 . Hence, there is an 𝜖 > 0 such that 𝖡𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑎); 𝜖) ⊆ 𝐵. From the assumption that 𝑓 is continuous there is a 𝛿 > 0 such that 𝖡𝑋 (𝑎; 𝛿) ⊆ 𝑓 −1 (𝖡𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑎); 𝜖)) ⊆ 𝑓 −1 (𝐵). We now prove the opposite implication. Assume that for every subset 𝐵 ⊆ 𝑌 open in 𝑌 , the preimage 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) of 𝐵 under 𝑓 is open in 𝑋. Let 𝑎 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝜖 > 0 be a real number. From the first assumption it follows that 𝑓 −1 (𝖡𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑎); 𝜖)) ⊆ 𝑋 is open in 𝑋. As 𝑓 −1 (𝖡𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑎); 𝜖)) is open and contains 𝑎, there is a 𝛿 > 0 such that 𝖡𝑋 (𝑎; 𝛿) ⊆ 𝑓 −1 (𝖡𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑎); 𝜖)). Thus 𝑥 ∈ 𝖡𝑋 (𝑎; 𝛿) implies that 𝑓 (𝑥) ∈ 𝖡𝑌 (𝑓 (𝑎); 𝜖). Hence, 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous.

Let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be sets, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝐵. Then 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) will always exist even if there is no inverse map. In the cases where 𝑓 has an inverse there is no ambiguity. If 𝑈 and 𝑉 are both subsets of 𝐵 then

𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∪ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∩ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ),

and furthermore, if 𝑈 ⊆ 𝑉 then 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ⊆ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ). Let 𝑈 ⊆ 𝐴 and 𝑉 ⊆ 𝐵, then ( ) 𝑈 ⊆ 𝑓 −1 (𝑓 (𝑈 )) and 𝑓 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) ⊆ 𝑉 .

We also note that if 𝑈 is a subset of 𝐵 then

𝑓 −1 (𝐵 ⧵ 𝑈 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 𝑐 ) = (𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ))𝑐 = 𝐴 ⧵ 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ).

12 2.3. Exercises

2.3 Exercises

Exercise 2.1 Does 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = (𝑥 − 𝑦)2 define a metric on 𝑋 = ℝ?

Exercise 2.2 Show that ℝ2 equipped with the taxi cab metric is a metric space.

Exercise 2.3 Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space. Show that the map 𝑑 ′ ∶ 𝑋 × 𝑋 → ℝ given by

𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) 𝑑 ′ (𝑥, 𝑦) = 1 + 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) is also a metric on 𝑋.

Exercise 2.4 Draw a picture of the open ball 𝖡((0, 0); 1) in the metric space (ℝ2 , 𝑑) with

(a) 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑑1 (𝑥, 𝑦) = |𝑥1 − 𝑦1 | + |𝑥2 − 𝑦2 |; √ (b) 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑑2 (𝑥, 𝑦) = (𝑥1 − 𝑦1 )2 + (𝑥2 − 𝑦2 )2 ;

(c) 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑑∞ (𝑥, 𝑦) = max{|𝑥1 − 𝑦1 |, |𝑥2 − 𝑦2 |}.

Exercise 2.5 Show that 𝑑1 , 𝑑2 and 𝑑∞ (as defined in Exercise 2.4) are equivalent on 𝑋 = ℝ2 .

Exercise 2.6 Show that in a discrete metric space (𝑋, 𝑑), cf. Example 2.3, every subset is both open and closed in 𝑋.

Exercise 2.7 Show that for equivalent metrics 𝑑 and 𝑑 ′ on the set 𝑋, the open sets are the same.

Exercise 2.8 Let (𝑋, 𝑑𝑋 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑑𝑌 ) be metric spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a map. Show that 𝑓 is continuous if and only if for every subset 𝐵 ⊆ 𝑌 closed in 𝑌 , the preimage 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) is closed in 𝑋.

3. Topological spaces

3.1 Definition and examples Topological spaces are spaces constructed to support continuous maps. The definition is as follows.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces. A map $f:X\to Y$ is continuous if and only if for every subset $B\subseteq Y$ that is open in $Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(B)=\{x\in X\mid f(x)\in B\}$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: This theorem identifies continuity with preservation of open sets under preimage. It is the bridge from metric continuity to the topological definition of continuity.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y\text{ continuous}\iff \forall B\subseteq Y,\ B\text{ open}\Rightarrow f^{-1}(B)\text{ open in }X

nodes.title: Topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
A topological space is a set 𝑋 together with a collection  of subsets of 𝑋 that are called open in 𝑋, such that the following properties hold.

T1 The subsets ∅ and 𝑋 are in  .

T2 The union of the elements of any subcollection of  is in  .

T3 The intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of  is in  .

A topological space is strictly speaking an ordered pair (𝑋,  ). We refer to  as the topology on 𝑋. We will often omit specific mention of  if no confusion will arise.

The following theorem states that every metric space (𝑋, 𝑑) is a topological space with the metric topology 𝑑 on 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: A topological space is a pair $(X,\mathcal{T})$ where $X$ is a set and $\mathcal{T}$ is a collection of subsets of $X$ such that $\emptyset,X\in\mathcal{T}$, arbitrary unions of elements of $\mathcal{T}$ lie in $\mathcal{T}$, and finite intersections of elements of $\mathcal{T}$ lie in $\mathcal{T}$. The elements of $\mathcal{T}$ are called open sets.
nodes.explanation: A topology abstracts the open-set behavior seen in metric spaces. The three axioms make open sets stable under the union and finite-intersection operations used throughout the subject.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}\subseteq\mathcal{P}(X),\quad \emptyset,X\in\mathcal{T},\quad \{U_i\}_{i\in I}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\Rightarrow\bigcup_{i\in I}U_i\in\mathcal{T},\quad U_1,\ldots,U_n\in\mathcal{T}\Rightarrow\bigcap_{k=1}^n U_k\in\mathcal{T}

nodes.title: Metric spaces are topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space. Let 𝑑 be the collection of subsets 𝑈 ⊆ 𝑋 with the property that 𝑈 ∈ 𝑑 if and only if for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 there is an 𝑟 > 0 such that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝑈 . Then 𝑑 defines a topology on 𝑋.

Proof. Clearly, ∅ ∈ 𝑑 . To show that 𝑋 ∈ 𝑑 , note that for any 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, 𝖡(𝑥; 1) ⊆ 𝑋. Hence, 𝑋 ∈ 𝑑 . Thus T1 is satisfied. ⋃ Let {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be any subcollection of 𝑑 . We need to prove that 𝑉 = 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 ∈ 𝑑 . Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑉 . ⋃ From 𝑉 = 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 there is 𝜆 ∈ Λ such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝜆 . By the property of 𝑈𝜆 satisfied by the 𝑈𝜆 in 𝑑 there is an 𝑟 > 0 such that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝑈𝜆 . Hence, 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝑉 . Thus 𝑉 ∈ 𝑑 , and T2 is satisfied. We prove that the intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of 𝑑 is also an element of 𝑑 . Let 𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 ∈ 𝑑 . We need to prove that 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ∈ 𝑑 . Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 . Since 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ⊆ 𝑈𝑖 , we have that 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2, … , 𝑛. By the defining property of 𝑑 there is an 𝑟𝑖 > 0 such that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟𝑖 ) ⊆ 𝑈𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2, … , 𝑛. Let 𝑟 = min{𝑟1 , 𝑟2 , … , 𝑟𝑛 }. Then 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟𝑖 ) ⊆ 𝑈𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2, … , 𝑛. Thus 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝑈1 ∩𝑈2 ∩⋯∩𝑈𝑛 , and so 𝑈1 ∩𝑈2 ∩⋯∩𝑈𝑛 ∈ 𝑑 . Hence, T3 is satisfied.

The following theorem relates the metric topologies for two equivalent metrics.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space. Let $\mathcal{T}_d$ be the collection of subsets $U\subseteq X$ such that for each $x\in U$ there exists $r>0$ with $B(x;r)\subseteq U$. Then $\mathcal{T}_d$ is a topology on $X$.
nodes.explanation: Every metric determines a topology by declaring the metric-open subsets to be open. The proof checks the topology axioms using open balls and the metric definition of open sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}_d=\{U\subseteq X\mid \forall x\in U,\ \exists r>0:\ B(x;r)\subseteq U\}\text{ is a topology on }X

nodes.title: Equivalent Metrics Induce the Same Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be any set, and let 𝑑1 and 𝑑2 be two equivalent metrics on 𝑋. Then 𝑑1 = 𝑑2 .

14 3.1. Definition and examples

This follows from Exercise 2.7.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a set and let $d_1$ and $d_2$ be equivalent metrics on $X$. Then the metric topologies agree: $\mathcal{T}_{d_1}=\mathcal{T}_{d_2}$.
nodes.explanation: Equivalent metrics have the same open sets, so they determine the same topology even if their numerical distances differ.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: d_1\sim d_2\Longrightarrow \mathcal{T}_{d_1}=\mathcal{T}_{d_2}

nodes.title: Discrete topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be any set. The collection  of all subsets of 𝑋, i.e. the power set (𝑋) of 𝑋, is a topology on 𝑋. We refer to this topology as the discrete topology. A set 𝑋 equipped with the discrete topology is referred to as a discrete topological space.

The discrete topology is the unique topology where the singletons are open. We can think of a discrete topological space as a space of separate, isolated points, with no close interaction between different points. For any set 𝑋, the discrete topology is the largest topology we may equip 𝑋 with. The smallest topology is called the indiscrete topology.
nodes.formalStatement: For any set $X$, the power set $\mathcal{P}(X)$ is a topology on $X$. This topology is called the discrete topology.
nodes.explanation: The discrete topology makes every subset open. It is the largest topology on a fixed set and models spaces whose points are completely separated.

nodes.title: Indiscrete topology
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be any set. The collection  consisting of ∅ and 𝑋 is a topology on 𝑋, referred to as the indiscrete topology on 𝑋. A set 𝑋 equipped with the indiscrete topology is referred to as an indiscrete topological space.
nodes.formalStatement: For any set $X$, the collection $\{\emptyset,X\}$ is a topology on $X$. This topology is called the indiscrete topology.
nodes.explanation: The indiscrete topology has only the two open sets required by the topology axioms. It is the smallest topology on a fixed set.

nodes.title: Topologies on a Three-Point Set
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}. The following collections all define a topology on 𝑋. (1) 1 = ind = {∅, 𝑋}
nodes.explanation: Illustrates topologies on a three-point set in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Comparable topologies
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be any set and suppose that 1 and 2 are two topologies on 𝑋. If 1 ⊆ 2 , we say that 1 is coarser than 2 and that 2 is finer than 1 . We say that 1 and 2 are comparable if either 1 ⊆ 2 or 2 ⊆ 1 .

Clearly, for any set 𝑋, the discrete topology disc contains the indiscrete topology ind : disc ⊇ ind . Hence, the discrete topology is finer than the indiscrete topology and the indiscrete topology is coarser than the discrete topology.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a set and let $\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2$ be topologies on $X$. If $\mathcal{T}_1\subseteq\mathcal{T}_2$, then $\mathcal{T}_1$ is coarser than $\mathcal{T}_2$ and $\mathcal{T}_2$ is finer than $\mathcal{T}_1$. The topologies are comparable if either one is contained in the other.
nodes.explanation: This definition orders topologies on a fixed set by inclusion of open sets. A finer topology has more open sets; a coarser topology has fewer.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}_1\preceq\mathcal{T}_2\iff \mathcal{T}_1\subseteq\mathcal{T}_2,\qquad \mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2\text{ comparable}\iff \mathcal{T}_1\subseteq\mathcal{T}_2\text{ or }\mathcal{T}_2\subseteq\mathcal{T}_1

nodes.title: Cofinite topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be any set. The collection  of subsets of 𝑋 consisting of subsets 𝑈 ⊆ 𝑋 such that 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 is either finite or all of 𝑋 is a topology on 𝑋. We refer to this topology as the cofinite topology on 𝑋.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 15

If 𝑋 is a finite set, the cofinite topology is equal to the discrete topology. If 𝑋 is an infinite set, the cofinite topology is strictly coarser than the discrete topology, in the sense that the cofinite topology is properly contained in the discrete topology. We end this section with a theorem that we can use to prove that some set is open. To state the theorem we need the following definition.
nodes.formalStatement: For a set $X$, the collection of subsets $U\subseteq X$ such that $X\setminus U$ is finite or equal to all of $X$ is a topology on $X$, called the cofinite topology.
nodes.explanation: The cofinite topology is a standard example between the indiscrete and discrete topologies on an infinite set.

nodes.title: Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, 𝑈 a subset of 𝑋 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. We say that 𝑈 is a neighborhood of 𝑥 if 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 and 𝑈 is open in 𝑋.

A neighborhood in the sense of the previous definition is sometimes referred to as an open neigh- borhood, cf. Definition 2.11.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a topological space, let $U\subseteq X$, and let $x\in X$. The set $U$ is a neighborhood of $x$ if $x\in U$ and $U$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: In a topological space, a neighborhood is simply an open set containing the point. This is the topological analogue of metric neighborhoods.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: U\text{ is a neighborhood of }x\iff x\in U\in\mathcal{T}

nodes.title: Open Sets via Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. A subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 is open in 𝑋 if and only if for every 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 there is a neighborhood 𝑈𝑥 of 𝑥 such that 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 .

Proof. Assume that 𝑈 is open in 𝑋. Then for every 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 , 𝑈 is a neighborhood of 𝑥 that is contained in 𝑈 . We prove the other implication. Assume that for every 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 there is a 𝑈𝑥 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 , i.e., that 𝑈𝑥 is a neighborhood of 𝑥 such that 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 . To prove that 𝑈 ∈  , we will ⋃ ⋃ prove that 𝑈 = 𝑥∈𝑈 𝑈𝑥 . Assume that 𝑥′ ∈ 𝑈𝑥′ . Then 𝑥′ ∈ 𝑈𝑥′ ⊆ 𝑥∈𝑈 𝑈𝑥 . Furthermore, any ⋃ point in 𝑥∈𝑈 𝑈𝑥 is in 𝑈𝑥 for some 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 so by assumption, 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 . Hence, ⋃ 𝑈 = 𝑥∈𝑈 𝑈𝑥 . As 𝑈 is the union of open sets it must be an open set as well by T2.

3.2 Continuous maps We know from Theorem 2.13 that a map between metric spaces is continuous if and only if the preimage of an open set is open. This motivates the following definition.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a topological space. A subset $U\subseteq X$ is open in $X$ if and only if for every $x\in U$ there exists a neighborhood $U_x$ of $x$ such that $U_x\subseteq U$.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: U\subseteq X\text{ open}\iff \forall x\in U,\ \exists U_x\in\mathcal{T}:\ x\in U_x\subseteq U
nodes.explanation: This theorem characterizes open sets pointwise by neighborhoods. The proof uses the fact that an arbitrary union of open neighborhoods is open.

nodes.title: Continuous maps between topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. A map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is said to be continuous if preimages of open sets are open, i.e., if 𝑉 is an open set in 𝑌 then the preimage 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) of 𝑉 under 𝑓 is open in 𝑋.

Hence, all continuous maps between metric spaces (𝑋, 𝑑𝑋 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑑𝑌 ) are also continuous maps between the corresponding topological spaces 𝑋 and 𝑌 with the metric topologies 𝑑𝑋 and 𝑑𝑌 , re- spectively.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ and $Y$ be topological spaces. A map $f:X\to Y$ is continuous if for every open set $V\subseteq Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: Topological continuity is defined by the preimage of open sets. This definition is motivated by the metric preimage criterion and no longer requires distances.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y\text{ is continuous}\iff \forall V\in\mathcal{T}_Y,\ f^{-1}(V)\in\mathcal{T}_X

nodes.title: Constant Maps Are Continuous
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Then all constant maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 are continuous: the preimages are either empty or the entire space, and these are always open, cf. T1.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Then all constant maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 are continuous: the preimages are either empty or the entire space, and these are always open, cf. T1.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates constant maps are continuous in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Maps from Discrete Spaces Are Continuous
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a discrete topological space and 𝑌 a topological space. Then all maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 are continuous.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a discrete topological space and 𝑌 a topological space. Then all maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 are continuous.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates maps from discrete spaces are continuous in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Maps into Indiscrete Spaces Are Continuous
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be any topological space and 𝑌 be an indiscrete topological space. Then all maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 are continuous.

16 3.2. Continuous maps
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be any topological space and 𝑌 be an indiscrete topological space. Then all maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 are continuous.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates maps into indiscrete spaces are continuous in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Identity Maps Between Discrete and Standard Real Lines
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝdisc be the discrete topological space consisting of the real numbers with the discrete topology, and let ℝ be the topological space consisting of the the real numbers with the usual (Euclidean) metric topology. Then the identity map id ℝdisc ←←←←→ ℝ

is continuous by Example 3.13, while the identity map id ℝ ←←←←→ ℝdisc

is not continuous: singletons are open in the discrete topology but not in the (Euclidean) metric topology.

The following theorem says that the composition of two continuous maps is a continuous map.
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝdisc be the discrete topological space consisting of the real numbers with the discrete topology, and let ℝ be the topological space consisting of the the real numbers with the usual (Euclidean) metric topology. Then the identity map id ℝdisc ←←←←→ ℝ is continuous by Example 3.13, while the identity map id ℝ ←←←←→ ℝdisc
nodes.explanation: Illustrates identity maps between discrete and standard real lines in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Composition of Continuous Maps
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋, 𝑌 and 𝑍 be topological spaces. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑍 are continuous maps, then the composite 𝑔◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑍 is continuous.

Proof. Let 𝑊 ⊆ 𝑍 be open in 𝑍. We need to prove that (𝑔◦𝑓 )−1 (𝑊 ) is open in 𝑋. Since

(𝑔◦𝑓 )−1 (𝑊 ) = {𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑔(𝑓 (𝑥)) ∈ 𝑊 } = {𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑓 (𝑥) ∈ 𝑔 −1 (𝑊 )} = {𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑓 −1 (𝑔 −1 (𝑊 ))} = 𝑓 −1 (𝑔 −1 (𝑊 ))

and that 𝑔 −1 (𝑊 ) is open in 𝑌 and 𝑓 −1 (𝑔 −1 (𝑊 )) is open in 𝑋 (by continuity of 𝑔 and 𝑓 ), it follows that (𝑔◦𝑓 )−1 (𝑊 ) is open in 𝑋. Hence, 𝑔◦𝑓 is continuous.

We can express continuity at a point for maps between topological spaces using neighborhoods. (See Theorem 2.12 for the case of metric spaces.)
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ be topological spaces. If $f:X\to Y$ and $g:Y\to Z$ are continuous maps, then the composite $g\circ f:X\to Z$ is continuous.
nodes.explanation: Continuous maps are closed under composition. The proof uses the identity $(g\circ f)^{-1}(W)=f^{-1}(g^{-1}(W))$ for an open set $W\subseteq Z$.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y,\ g:Y\to Z\text{ continuous}\Longrightarrow g\circ f:X\to Z\text{ continuous}

nodes.title: Continuity at a point
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. A map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous at 𝑥 if for all neighborhoods 𝑉 of 𝑓 (𝑥) there is a neighborhood 𝑈 of 𝑥 such that 𝑓 (𝑈 ) ⊆ 𝑉 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. A map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous at 𝑥 if for all neighborhoods 𝑉 of 𝑓 (𝑥) there is a neighborhood 𝑈 of 𝑥 such that 𝑓 (𝑈 ) ⊆ 𝑉 .
nodes.explanation: Defines continuity at a point and fixes terminology used later in continuity, maps, topology.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f\text{ is continuous at }x\iff \forall V\ni f(x)\text{ open},\ \exists U\ni x\text{ open such that }f(U)\subseteq V

nodes.title: Continuity Is Pointwise Continuity
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. A map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous if and only if it is continuous at each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋.

Proof. Assume that 𝑓 is continuous, and let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝑉 be a neighborhood of 𝑓 (𝑥). Then the set 𝑈 = 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) is a neighborhood of 𝑥 such that 𝑓 (𝑈 ) ⊆ 𝑉 . Assume that 𝑓 is continuous at each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Let 𝑉 ⊆ 𝑌 be open in 𝑌 . Choose 𝑥 ∈ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ). Since 𝑓 is continuous at 𝑥 there is neighborhood 𝑈𝑥 of 𝑥 such that 𝑓 (𝑈𝑥 ) ⊆ 𝑉 . Hence, 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ). It follows that 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) can be written as the union of the open sets 𝑈𝑥 , and hence, it is open in 𝑋. Thus 𝑓 is continuous.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 17

3.3 Homeomorphisms We now introduce the notion of topological equivalence, also known as homeomorphism.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. A map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous if and only if it is continuous at each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about continuity is pointwise continuity; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y\text{ continuous}\iff \forall x\in X,\ f\text{ is continuous at }x

nodes.title: Homeomorphisms
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. A bijective map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 with the property that both 𝑓 and 𝑓 −1 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 are continuous, is called a homeo- morphism. If there exists a homeomorphism 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 , we say that 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomor- phic and write 𝑋 ≅ 𝑌 .

A homeomorphism 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 gives a one-to-one correspondence between open sets in 𝑋 and 𝑌 . As a result, any property of a topological space that can be expressed in terms of its elements and its open subsets is preserved by homeomorphisms. Such a property is called a topological property.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. A bijective map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 with the property that both 𝑓 and 𝑓 −1 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 are continuous, is called a homeomorphism. If there exists a homeomorphism 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 , we say that 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic and write 𝑋 ≅ 𝑌 . A homeomorphism 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 gives a one-to-one correspondence between open sets in 𝑋 and 𝑌 . As a result, any property of a topological space that can be expressed in terms of its elements and its open subsets is preserved by homeomorphisms. Such a property is called a topological property.
nodes.explanation: Defines homeomorphisms and fixes terminology used later in continuity, homeomorphism, maps.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\cong Y\iff \exists f:X\to Y\text{ bijective such that }f\text{ and }f^{-1}\text{ are continuous}

nodes.title: Affine Homeomorphism of the Real Line
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. The map 𝑓 ∶ ℝ → ℝ given by

𝑓 (𝑥) = 2𝑥 − 1

is a homeomorphism. Let 𝑔 ∶ ℝ → ℝ be given by

𝑔(𝑦) = (𝑦 + 1) then, clearly, 𝑔(𝑓 (𝑥)) = 𝑥 and 𝑓 (𝑔(𝑦)) = 𝑦 for all real numbers 𝑥 and 𝑦. Thus 𝑓 is a bijection and 𝑓 −1 = 𝑔. From calculus we know that 𝑓 and 𝑔 are continuous. Hence, 𝑓 is a homeomorphism.
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. The map 𝑓 ∶ ℝ → ℝ given by 𝑓 (𝑥) = 2𝑥 − 1
nodes.explanation: Illustrates affine homeomorphism of the real line in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Continuous Bijection Between Nonidentical Two-Point Topologies
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏}, and let 1 = {∅, {𝑎}, 𝑋} and 2 = {∅, {𝑏}, 𝑋} be two topolo- gies on 𝑋. The map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 given by 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑏 and 𝑓 (𝑏) = 𝑎 is clearly a continuous bijection (with the domain given 1 as topology, and the codomain given 2 as topology). Also, 𝑓 is its own inverse: 𝑓 = 𝑓 −1 . Hence, 𝑓 is a homeomorphism and (𝑋, 1 ) ≅ (𝑋, 2 ).

Homeomorphisms are continuous bijections, but the converse is not true.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏}, and let 1 = {∅, {𝑎}, 𝑋} and 2 = {∅, {𝑏}, 𝑋} be two topologies on 𝑋. The map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 given by 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑏 and 𝑓 (𝑏) = 𝑎 is clearly a continuous bijection (with the domain given 1 as topology, and the codomain given 2 as topology). Also, 𝑓 is its own inverse: 𝑓 = 𝑓 −1 . Hence, 𝑓 is a homeomorphism and (𝑋, 1 ) ≅ (𝑋, 2 ). Homeomorphisms are continuous bijections, but the converse is not true.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates continuous bijection between nonidentical two-point topologies in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Continuous Bijection Need Not Be a Homeomorphism
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏}. The identity map id ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 where the domain is given the discrete topology and the codomain is given the indiscrete topology is a continuous bijection but not a homeomorphism: the inverse map is not continuous.

The following theorem says that being homeomorphic is an equivalence relation on any set of topological spaces.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏}. The identity map id ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 where the domain is given the discrete topology and the codomain is given the indiscrete topology is a continuous bijection but not a homeomorphism: the inverse map is not continuous.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates continuous bijection need not be a homeomorphism in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Homeomorphism Is an Equivalence Relation
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋, 𝑌 and 𝑍 be topological spaces.

Reflexivity The identity map id ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 (where the domain and the codomain are equipped with the same topology), given by id(𝑥) = 𝑥 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, is a homeomorphism.

Symmetry If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a homeomorphism, then 𝑓 −1 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is also a homeomor- phism.

Transitivity If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑍 are homeomorphisms, then 𝑔◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑍 is also

18 3.4. Closed sets

a homeomorphism.

Proof. The identity map id ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 (where the domain and the codomain are equipped with the same topology) is clearly continuous and bijective. As the identity map is its own inverse, then it is also a homeomorphism. Hence, 𝑋 ≅ 𝑋 and so ≅ satisfies the reflexivity condition for an equivalence relation. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a homeomorphism, then 𝑓 −1 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is also a homeomorphism: 𝑓 −1 is a continuous bijection with continuous inverse (𝑓 −1 )−1 = 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 . Hence, 𝑋 ≅ 𝑌 if and only if 𝑌 ≅ 𝑋. Thus ≅ satisfies the symmetry condition for an equivalence relation.

Theorem 3.16 tells us that the composition of two homeomorphisms 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑍 is continuous. The composition of two bijective maps is always bijective. Hence, 𝑔◦𝑓 is a continuous bijection. We need to prove that its inverse, (𝑔◦𝑓 )−1 , is continuous. Since

(𝑔◦𝑓 )−1 = 𝑓 −1 ◦𝑔 −1

is a composition of continuous maps, then by Theorem 3.16 so is (𝑔◦𝑓 )−1 . Thus 𝑔◦𝑓 is a homeomorphism. Hence, if 𝑋 ≅ 𝑌 and 𝑌 ≅ 𝑍, then 𝑋 ≅ 𝑍. Thus ≅ satisfies the transitivity condition for an equivalence relation.
nodes.formalStatement: For topological spaces $X$, $Y$, and $Z$, being homeomorphic is an equivalence relation: $X\cong X$; if $X\cong Y$ then $Y\cong X$; and if $X\cong Y$ and $Y\cong Z$ then $X\cong Z$.
nodes.explanation: This theorem formalizes the idea that homeomorphic spaces are topologically the same. The proof checks reflexivity by the identity map, symmetry by taking inverses, and transitivity by composing homeomorphisms.

mergedFrom.reason: This was extracted as a duplicate theorem 3.16, but its text is the transitivity proof continuation for Theorem 3.23.
mergedFrom.cleanText:
Theorem 3.16 tells us that the composition of two homeomorphisms 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑍 is continuous. The composition of two bijective maps is always bijective. Hence, 𝑔◦𝑓 is a continuous bijection. We need to prove that its inverse, (𝑔◦𝑓 )−1 , is continuous. Since

(𝑔◦𝑓 )−1 = 𝑓 −1 ◦𝑔 −1

is a composition of continuous maps, then by Theorem 3.16 so is (𝑔◦𝑓 )−1 . Thus 𝑔◦𝑓 is a homeomorphism. Hence, if 𝑋 ≅ 𝑌 and 𝑌 ≅ 𝑍, then 𝑋 ≅ 𝑍. Thus ≅ satisfies the transitivity condition for an equivalence relation.

nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\cong X,\quad X\cong Y\Rightarrow Y\cong X,\quad (X\cong Y\land Y\cong Z)\Rightarrow X\cong Z

nodes.title: Closed subsets
nodes.originalText:
A subset 𝐾 of a topological space 𝑋 is closed in 𝑋 if and only if the complement 𝐾 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝐾 is open in 𝑋.

This is completely analogous to how we defined closed subsets in metric spaces, cf. Definition 2.9.
nodes.formalStatement: A subset $K$ of a topological space $X$ is closed in $X$ if and only if its complement $X\setminus K$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: Defines closed subsets and fixes terminology used later in closed-sets, metric, open-sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: K\subseteq X\text{ is closed}\iff X\setminus K\in\mathcal{T}

nodes.title: Closed Sets in a Discrete Space
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a discrete topological space. Since every subset of 𝑋 is open in 𝑋, it follows that every subset of 𝑋 is also closed in 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a discrete topological space. Since every subset of 𝑋 is open in 𝑋, it follows that every subset of 𝑋 is also closed in 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates closed sets in a discrete space in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Closed Sets in an Indiscrete Space
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete topological space. The only subsets of 𝑋 that are closed in 𝑋 are ∅ and 𝑋 (which are also the only subsets that are open in 𝑋).

Recall that in a discrete topological space, all the singletons are open sets. This is usually not the case.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete topological space. The only subsets of 𝑋 that are closed in 𝑋 are ∅ and 𝑋 (which are also the only subsets that are open in 𝑋). Recall that in a discrete topological space, all the singletons are open sets. This is usually not the case.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates closed sets in an indiscrete space in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Closed Intervals in the Real Line
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Then every subset [𝑎, 𝑏] = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ 𝑎 ⩽ 𝑥 ⩽ 𝑏} ⊆ ℝ is closed in ℝ: the complement [𝑎, 𝑏]𝑐 = ℝ ⧵ [𝑎, 𝑏] = (−∞, 𝑎) ∪ (𝑏, ∞) is a union of open sets in ℝ, and hence, is open in ℝ. Furthermore, all the singletons are closed: the complement {𝑎}𝑐 = ℝ ⧵ {𝑎} = (−∞, 𝑎) ∪ (𝑎, ∞) is a union of open sets in ℝ, and hence, is open in ℝ.

By passing to complements we get the following theorem.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 19
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Then every subset [𝑎, 𝑏] = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ 𝑎 ⩽ 𝑥 ⩽ 𝑏} ⊆ ℝ is closed in ℝ: the complement [𝑎, 𝑏]𝑐 = ℝ ⧵ [𝑎, 𝑏] = (−∞, 𝑎) ∪ (𝑏, ∞) is a union of open sets in ℝ, and hence, is open in ℝ. Furthermore, all the singletons are closed: the complement {𝑎}𝑐 = ℝ ⧵ {𝑎} = (−∞, 𝑎) ∪ (𝑎, ∞) is a union of open sets in ℝ, and hence, is open in ℝ. By passing to complements we get
nodes.explanation: Illustrates closed intervals in the real line in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Closure Properties of Closed Sets
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space.

(1) Both ∅ and 𝑋 are closed (as subsets) in 𝑋.

(2) The intersection of any subcollection of closed sets in 𝑋 is closed in 𝑋.

(3) The union of any finite subcollection of closed sets in 𝑋 is closed in 𝑋.

It follows that we could have defined a topological space 𝑋 by specifying a collection of subsets of 𝑋 satisfying the three statements in Theorem 3.28 where we would say that a subset of 𝑋 is open in 𝑋 if its complement is closed in 𝑋. We end this section with a theorem describing the connection between continuous maps and closed sets. We will need the following definition.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. (1) Both ∅ and 𝑋 are closed (as subsets) in 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about closure properties of closed sets; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \emptyset,X\text{ closed};\quad \{C_\lambda\}_{\lambda\in\Lambda}\text{ closed}\Rightarrow \bigcap_{\lambda\in\Lambda}C_\lambda\text{ closed};\quad C_1,\dots,C_n\text{ closed}\Rightarrow \bigcup_{i=1}^n C_i\text{ closed}

nodes.title: Closure
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. The closure of 𝐴, written 𝐴, is the intersection of all subsets of 𝑋 that contain 𝐴 and which are closed in 𝑋.

From the definition it follows that 𝐴 is the smallest subset of 𝑋 that contains 𝐴 and which is closed in 𝑋. Furthermore, if 𝐴 is closed in 𝑋, then 𝐴 = 𝐴. There is an analogous definition for open sets where we take union instead of intersection. We can define the interior of 𝐴, written Int(𝐴), to be the union of all subsets of 𝑋 that are contained in 𝐴 and which are open in 𝑋. It follows that Int(𝐴) is the largest subset of 𝑋 that is contained in 𝐴 and which is open in 𝑋. Furthermore, Int(𝐴) ⊆ 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐴.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. The closure of 𝐴, written 𝐴, is the intersection of all subsets of 𝑋 that contain 𝐴 and which are closed in 𝑋. From the definition it follows that 𝐴 is the smallest subset of 𝑋 that contains 𝐴 and which is closed in 𝑋. Furthermore, if 𝐴 is closed in 𝑋, then 𝐴 = 𝐴. There is an analogous definition for open sets where we take union instead of intersection. We can define the interior of 𝐴, written Int(𝐴), to be the union of all subsets of 𝑋 that are contained in 𝐴 and which are open in 𝑋. It follows that Int(𝐴) is the largest subset of 𝑋 that is contained in 𝐴 and which is open in 𝑋. Furthermore, Int(𝐴) ⊆ 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐴.
nodes.explanation: Defines closure and fixes terminology used later in closed-sets, sets, topology.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \overline{A}=\bigcap\{K\subseteq X\mid A\subseteq K\text{ and }K\text{ is closed}\},\qquad \operatorname{Int}(A)=\bigcup\{U\subseteq A\mid U\text{ is open}\}

nodes.title: Closure in a Finite Topological Space
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a topological space consisting of the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐} and the topology  = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, 𝑋}. Then the closed subsets in 𝑋 are ∅, {𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐} and 𝑋. Thus the intersection of all of the closed subsets that contain {𝑏} is simply {𝑏, 𝑐}∩𝑋 = {𝑏, 𝑐}, and hence, {𝑏} = {𝑏, 𝑐}.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space consisting of the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐} and the topology  = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, 𝑋}. Then the closed subsets in 𝑋 are ∅, {𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐} and 𝑋. Thus the intersection of all of the closed subsets that contain {𝑏} is simply {𝑏, 𝑐}∩𝑋 = {𝑏, 𝑐}, and hence, {𝑏} = {𝑏, 𝑐}.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates closure in a finite topological space in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Closure and Interior of a Half-Open Interval
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Assume that 𝑎 < 𝑏 are real numbers. Then (𝑎, 𝑏] = [𝑎, 𝑏] and Int((𝑎, 𝑏]) = (𝑎, 𝑏).

Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space. If we consider 𝑋 as a topological space with the metric topology 𝑑 , the closure 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) of an open ball 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) about 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 is, in general, not the same as the closed ball 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟). If 𝑑 is the discrete metric and 𝑋 has at least two elements, then 𝖡(𝑥; 1) = {𝑥} while 𝖡(𝑥; 1) = 𝑋. It is always the case that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟).
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Assume that 𝑎 < 𝑏 are real numbers. Then (𝑎, 𝑏] = [𝑎, 𝑏] and Int((𝑎, 𝑏]) = (𝑎, 𝑏). Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space. If we consider 𝑋 as a topological space with the metric topology 𝑑 , the closure 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) of an open ball 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) about 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 is, in general, not the same as the closed ball 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟). If 𝑑 is the discrete metric and 𝑋 has at least two elements, then 𝖡(𝑥; 1) = {𝑥} while 𝖡(𝑥; 1) = 𝑋. It is always the case that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟).
nodes.explanation: Illustrates closure and interior of a half-open interval in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Dense
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is dense in 𝑋 if 𝐴 = 𝑋.

From the definition it follows that 𝐴 is dense in 𝑋 if and only if 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ≠ ∅ for every nonempty subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 which is open in 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is dense in 𝑋 if 𝐴 = 𝑋. From the definition it follows that 𝐴 is dense in 𝑋 if and only if 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ≠ ∅ for every nonempty subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 which is open in 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Defines dense and fixes terminology used later in open-sets, sets, topology.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: A\text{ is dense in }X\iff \overline{A}=X\iff \forall U\in\mathcal{T},\ U\ne\emptyset\Rightarrow A\cap U\ne\emptyset

nodes.title: The Rational Numbers Are Dense in the Real Line
nodes.originalText: Let ℝ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Then the subset ℚ of rational numbers is dense in ℝ: ℚ = ℝ.
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Then the subset ℚ of rational numbers is dense in ℝ: ℚ = ℝ.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates the rational numbers are dense in the real line in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Dense Subsets in Discrete Spaces
nodes.originalText:
For any topological space 𝑋, the subset 𝑋 is dense in 𝑋. If 𝑋 is a discrete topological space, then the subset 𝑋 is the only dense subset in 𝑋.

20 3.5. Exercises
nodes.formalStatement: For any topological space 𝑋, the subset 𝑋 is dense in 𝑋. If 𝑋 is a discrete topological space, then the subset 𝑋 is the only dense subset in 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates dense subsets in discrete spaces in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Equivalent Characterizations of Continuity via Closure and Closed Sets
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a map between topological spaces. Then the following are equivalent:

(1) 𝑓 is continuous;

(2) for every subset 𝐴 of 𝑋, we have 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝑓 (𝐴);

(3) for every closed subset 𝐵 of 𝑌 , the preimage 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) of 𝐵 under 𝑓 is closed in 𝑋.

Proof. By passing to complements, it follows readily that (1) and (3) are equivalent. We will prove that (2) is equivalent to (3). Assume (2). Let 𝐵 be a subset of 𝑌 that is closed in 𝑌 , and let 𝐴 = 𝑓 −1 (𝐵). We must show that 𝐴 is closed. We have 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝐵. If 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴, then 𝑓 (𝑥) ∈ 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝐵. Hence, 𝑥 ∈ 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) = 𝐴. In other words, 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐴. Thus 𝐴 = 𝐴, and hence, 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) is closed in 𝑋. Now assume (3). Let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We must show that 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝑓 (𝐴). Since 𝑓 (𝐴) is closed in 𝑌 , it follows by assumption that 𝑓 −1 (𝑓 (𝐴)) is closed in 𝑋. Furthermore, 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑓 −1 (𝑓 (𝐴)) ⊆ 𝑓 −1 (𝑓 (𝐴)). Since 𝑓 −1 (𝑓 (𝐴)) is closed in 𝑋, it follows that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑓 −1 (𝑓 (𝐴)). Hence, 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝑓 (𝐴).

3.5 Exercises

Exercise 3.1 Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑}. Show that  = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑}, 𝑋} is not a topology on 𝑋. Find a topology  ′ (different from the discrete topology) on 𝑋 such that  ⊆  ′.

Exercise 3.2 Let 𝑋 be a non-empty set, and let 𝑥0 be an element of 𝑋. Show that

 = {𝑈 ⊆ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑥0 ∉ 𝑈 or 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 is finite}

is a topology on 𝑋.

Exercise 3.3 Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Define the coarsest topology on 𝑋 such that 𝐴 is open in 𝑋.

Exercise 3.4 Show that the discrete topology disc is finer than the cofinite topology cof on any set 𝑋.

Exercise 3.5 Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑}. Find two topologies 1 and 2 with 1 ≠ 2 such that a bijection 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is a homeomorphism (where the domain is given 1 as topology and the codomain is given 2 as topology).

Exercise 3.6 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be subsets of 𝑋.

(a) Assume that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵. Show that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵.

(b) Show that 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵 = 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 21

We say that a set 𝐴 intersects or meets a set 𝐵 if 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ≠ ∅.

Exercise 3.7 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Show that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 if and only if every neighborhood of 𝑥 intersects 𝐴.

Exercise 3.8 Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, and let

 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑏, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋}

be a topology on 𝑋. Is the subset {𝑎, 𝑏} dense in 𝑋?

4. Generating topologies

4.1 Generating topologies from subsets The following theorem tells us how we may extract a third topology from two other topologies on the same set.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a map between topological spaces. Then the following are equivalent: (1) 𝑓 is continuous;
nodes.explanation: States a main result about equivalent characterizations of continuity via closure and closed sets; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f\text{ continuous}\iff \forall C\subseteq Y,\ C\text{ closed}\Rightarrow f^{-1}(C)\text{ closed}\iff \forall A\subseteq X,\ f(\overline{A})\subseteq \overline{f(A)}

nodes.title: The intersection of two topologies is a topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 1 and 2 be two topologies on 𝑋. Then 1 ∩ 2 is also a topology on 𝑋.

Proof. Clearly, ∅ and 𝑋 are in 1 ∩ 2 , so T1 is satisfied. Let {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of sets such that 𝑈𝜆 ∈ 1 ∩ 2 for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ where Λ is some ⋃ index set. Then, for 𝑖 = 1, 2, 𝑈𝜆 ∈ 𝑖 for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. Thus 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 ∈ 𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2. Hence, ⋃ 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 ∈ 1 ∩ 2 , and so, T2 is satisfied. Let {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 } be a finite subcollection of  . We need to prove that T3 is satisfied, i.e., 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ∈  . Note that if 𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 ∈ 𝑖 then 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ∈ 𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2. Hence, 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ∈ 1 ∩ 2 . Thus T3 is satisfied.

Theorem ⋂ 4.1 may be extended to hold for a family of topologies: if {𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ is a family of topologies on 𝑋, then 𝜆∈Λ 𝜆 is also a topology on 𝑋. If we follow the convention that for subsets 𝑆 of a fixed (large) set 𝑈 , ⋂ 𝑆 = 𝑈, 𝑆∈∅

then the extended version of Theorem 4.1 may also include an empty family {𝜆 }𝜆∈∅ of topologies with ⋂ 𝜆 = (𝑋), 𝜆∈∅

i.e., the discrete topology on 𝑋 (with our fixed (large) set 𝑈 being equal to (𝑋)). However, not all mathemati- cians follow this convention. Thus we will in general not define the intersection of an empty family.

The union of two topologies is not necessarily a topology.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 1 and 2 be two topologies on 𝑋. Then 1 ∩ 2 is also a topology on 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about the intersection of two topologies is a topology; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2\text{ topologies on }X\Longrightarrow \mathcal{T}_1\cap\mathcal{T}_2\text{ is a topology on }X

nodes.title: Intersection and Union of Topologies
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, and let 1 = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, 𝑋} and 2 = {∅, {𝑐}, 𝑋} be two topologies on 𝑋. Then 1 ∩ 2 = {∅, 𝑋} is the indiscrete topology on 𝑋 while

1 ∪ 2 = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑐}, 𝑋}

is not a topology on 𝑋: 1 ∪ 2 does not satisfy T2.

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 23

Recall from Section 3.1 that for any set 𝑋 the discrete topology disc is the largest topology we may equip 𝑋 with, and the indiscrete topology ind is the smallest topology we may equip 𝑋 with. For any topology  on 𝑋 we have ind ⊆  ⊆ disc .

That is, we have partially ordered topologies on 𝑋 by inclusion. Let 𝑋 be a set. We often want to have a collection of subsets  of 𝑋 to be the open subsets of a topology on 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, and let 1 = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, 𝑋} and 2 = {∅, {𝑐}, 𝑋} be two topologies on 𝑋. Then 1 ∩ 2 = {∅, 𝑋} is the indiscrete topology on 𝑋 while 1 ∪ 2 = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑐}, 𝑋}
nodes.explanation: Illustrates intersection and union of topologies in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Topology generated by a collection of subsets
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a collection of subsets of 𝑋. The topology generated by  is the topology ⋂ ⟨⟩ =   topology ⊆

on 𝑋.

In other words, ⟨⟩ contains  and for any other topology  ′ containing , we have ⟨⟩ ⊆  ′ . Thus ⟨⟩ is unique.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a collection of subsets of 𝑋. The topology generated by  is the topology ⋂ ⟨⟩ =   topology ⊆ on 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Defines topology generated by a collection of subsets and fixes terminology used later in sets, topology.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \langle\mathcal{S}\rangle=\bigcap\{\mathcal{T}\mid \mathcal{T}\text{ is a topology on }X\text{ and }\mathcal{S}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\}

nodes.title: Empty Subbasis Generates the Indiscrete Topology
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  = ∅. Then ⟨⟩ is the same as the indiscrete topology on 𝑋, i.e., ⟨⟩ = ind = {∅, 𝑋}.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  = ∅. Then ⟨⟩ is the same as the indiscrete topology on 𝑋, i.e., ⟨⟩ = ind = {∅, 𝑋}.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates empty subbasis generates the indiscrete topology in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Singleton Subbasis Generates the Discrete Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be the collection of all the singletons of 𝑋, i.e.,  = {{𝑥} ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋}. Then ⟨⟩ is the same as the discrete topology on 𝑋, i.e.,

⟨⟩ = disc = (𝑋).

4.2 Basis for a topology It is often convenient to define a topology  on a set 𝑋 by only specifying a subcollection  of  satisfying certain properties. The open subsets of 𝑋 are then precisely the unions of subcollections of . In this way, we say the basis determines, or generates, the topology.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be the collection of all the singletons of 𝑋, i.e.,  = {{𝑥} ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋}. Then ⟨⟩ is the same as the discrete topology on 𝑋, i.e., ⟨⟩ = disc = (𝑋).
nodes.explanation: Illustrates singleton subbasis generates the discrete topology in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Basis
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set. A basis for a topology on 𝑋 is a collection  of subsets of 𝑋 such that

B1 for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, there is a 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵;

B2 if 𝐵1 , 𝐵2 ∈  and 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 , then there is a 𝐵3 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵3 ⊆ 𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 .

The elements of  are sometimes referred to as basis elements.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set. A basis for a topology on 𝑋 is a collection  of subsets of 𝑋 such that B1 for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, there is a 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵;
nodes.explanation: Defines basis and fixes terminology used later in basis, sets, topology.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{B}\text{ is a basis}\iff \bigcup_{B\in\mathcal{B}}B=X\text{ and }\forall B_1,B_2\in\mathcal{B}\ \forall x\in B_1\cap B_2\ \exists B_3\in\mathcal{B}:x\in B_3\subseteq B_1\cap B_2

nodes.title: Singletons Form a Basis for the Discrete Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be the collection of all the singletons of 𝑋. Then  is a basis for the discrete topology on 𝑋.

24 4.2. Basis for a topology
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be the collection of all the singletons of 𝑋. Then  is a basis for the discrete topology on 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates singletons form a basis for the discrete topology in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Open Balls Form a Basis for the Metric Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space. Then the collection of (open) 𝜖-balls

 = {𝖡(𝑥; 𝜖) ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, 𝜖 > 0}

is a basis for the metric topology 𝑑 , as defined in Theorem 3.2, on 𝑋.

The following theorem describes a topology generated by a basis.
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space. Then the collection of (open) 𝜖-balls  = {𝖡(𝑥; 𝜖) ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, 𝜖 > 0}
nodes.explanation: Illustrates open balls form a basis for the metric topology in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Topology Generated by a Basis
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be basis for a topology on 𝑋. The collection  generated by  of subsets 𝑈 of 𝑋 with the property that for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 there is a basis element 𝐵 ∈  with 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝑈 is a topology on 𝑋.

Proof. Clearly, ∅ and 𝑋 are both in  . Hence, T1 is satisfied. ⋃ Let {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a subcollection of  . Let 𝑉 = 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 . We need to prove that 𝑉 ∈  . Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑉 . Then there is a 𝜆 ∈ Λ such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝜆 . Since 𝑈𝜆 ∈  , there is a basis element 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝑈𝜆 . As 𝑈𝜆 ⊆ 𝑉 , it follows that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝑉 . Hence, 𝑉 ∈  , and so, T2 is satisfied. We need to prove that T3 is satisfied, that is, for a finite subcollection {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 } of  , we have 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ∈  . We will prove the case for 𝑛 = 2. The general result then follows by an induction argument. Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 . Since 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ⊆ 𝑈𝑖 we have 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝑖 , and thus there is a basis element 𝐵𝑖 ∈  with 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵𝑖 ⊆ 𝑈𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2. Hence, 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 ⊆ 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 . By B2 there is a basis element 𝐵3 ∈  with 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵3 ⊆ 𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 . Thus 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵3 ⊆ 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 , and so, 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∈  .

The topology generated by a basis may also be described using the following theorem.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be basis for a topology on 𝑋. The collection  generated by  of subsets 𝑈 of 𝑋 with the property that for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 there is a basis element 𝐵 ∈  with 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝑈 is a topology on 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about topology generated by a basis; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}=\{U\subseteq X\mid \forall x\in U,\ \exists B\in\mathcal{B}:\ x\in B\subseteq U\}\text{ is a topology on }X

nodes.title: Basis Elements Generate All Opens by Unions
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a basis for a topology  on 𝑋. Then  is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of .

Proof. Let 𝐵 ∈  be any basis element. Then for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵 we obviously have 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵 and 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐵. Thus 𝐵 ∈  . It follows that any union of basis elements is a union of elements of  , and hence, is in . Conversely, let 𝑈 ∈  . For each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 there is a 𝐵𝑥 ∈  with 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵𝑥 and 𝐵𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 . Then ⋃ 𝑈 = 𝑥∈𝑈 𝐵𝑥 , and thus, 𝑈 is the union of elements of .

We end this section with a theorem describing a criterion for whether one topology is finer than another when both topologies are described using bases.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a basis for a topology  on 𝑋. Then  is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of .
nodes.explanation: States a main result about basis elements generate all opens by unions; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}=\left\{\bigcup_{\lambda\in\Lambda}B_\lambda\mid B_\lambda\in\mathcal{B}\right\}

nodes.title: Comparing Topologies via Bases
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 1 and 2 be bases for topologies 1 and 2 , respec- tively, on 𝑋. Then the following are equivalent:

(1) 2 is finer than 1 , i.e., 1 ⊆ 2 .

(2) For each 𝐵1 ∈ 1 and each 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵1 there is a 𝐵2 ∈ 2 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵2 ⊆ 𝐵1 .

Proof. Assume (1). Let 𝐵1 ∈ 1 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵1 . Since 𝐵1 ∈ 1 and 1 ⊆ 2 we have 𝐵1 ∈ 2 . Furthermore, as 2 is the topology generated by 2 there is a 𝐵2 ∈ 2 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵2 where 𝐵2 ⊆ 𝐵1 . Hence, (2) is satisfied.

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 25

Now assume (2). Let 𝑈 ∈ 1 . We must prove that 𝑈 ∈ 2 . Since 1 generates 1 , then for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 there is a 𝐵1 ∈ 1 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵1 ⊆ 𝑈 . By assumption there is a 𝐵2 ∈ 2 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵2 ⊆ 𝐵1 . Hence, 𝐵2 ⊆ 𝑈 , and so, 𝑈 ∈ 2 . Thus (1) is satisfied.

In order to have 1 ⊆ 2 it is not necessary to have 1 ⊆ 2 , i.e., each basis element in 1 need not be a basis element in 2 . However, for each basis element 𝐵1 ∈ 1 and each point 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵1 there should be some (possibly) smaller basis element 𝐵2 ∈ 2 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵2 ⊆ 𝐵1 .

4.3 Subbasis for a topology Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a collection of subsets of 𝑋. We can form a basis  for a topology by simply taking all finite intersections 𝑛 ⋂ 𝐵= 𝑆𝑖 𝑖=1 of elements of . Thus the open sets in the topology generated by this basis are all unions of such basis elements 𝐵, cf. Theorem 4.10. Hence, the open sets are all unions of all finite intersections of elements of . The collection  is then referred to as a subbasis. We will impose the restriction that the union of the elements of  is equal to 𝑋 to avoid ambiguity about the intersection of an empty collection.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 1 and 2 be bases for topologies 1 and 2 , respectively, on 𝑋. Then the following are equivalent: (1) 2 is finer than 1 , i.e., 1 ⊆ 2 .
nodes.explanation: States a main result about comparing topologies via bases; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}_1\subseteq\mathcal{T}_2\iff \forall x\in X,\ \forall B_1\in\mathcal{B}_1\ (x\in B_1\Rightarrow \exists B_2\in\mathcal{B}_2:\ x\in B_2\subseteq B_1)

nodes.title: Subbasis
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a set. A subbasis for a topology on 𝑋 is a collection  of subsets of 𝑋 whose union equals 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set. A subbasis for a topology on 𝑋 is a collection  of subsets of 𝑋 whose union equals 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Defines subbasis and fixes terminology used later in basis, sets, topology.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{S}\text{ is a subbasis on }X\iff \bigcup_{S\in\mathcal{S}}S=X

nodes.title: Finite Intersections of a Subbasis Form a Basis
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a subbasis for a topology on 𝑋. The collection  consisting of all finite intersections of elements of  is a basis for a topology on 𝑋 and is called the basis associated to .

Proof. Each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 must lie in some 𝑆 ∈ . Hence, 𝑥 ∈ 𝑆. Thus 𝑥 is an element of the basis element 𝑆 in , and so, B1 is satisfied. ⋂ ⋂𝑛 ′ Let 𝐵1 = 𝑚 𝑖=1 𝑆𝑖 and 𝐵2 = 𝑖=1 𝑆𝑖 be two basis elements of , and let 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 . We must prove that there is a basis element 𝐵3 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵3 ⊆ 𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 . Let (𝑚 ) ( 𝑛 ) ⋂ ⋂ ′ 𝐵3 = 𝑆𝑖 ∩ 𝑆𝑖 . 𝑖=1 𝑖=1

Then 𝐵3 is also a finite intersection of elements of , and hence, 𝐵3 ∈  with 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵3 . Thus B2 is satisfied.

By combining the previous lemma with Theorem 4.10, we get the following lemma.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a subbasis for a topology on 𝑋. The collection  consisting of all finite intersections of elements of  is a basis for a topology on 𝑋 and is called the basis associated to .
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about finite intersections of a subbasis form a basis that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Topology Generated by a Subbasis
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a subbasis for a topology on 𝑋. The collection  generated by  consisting of all unions of all basis elements of the associated basis  is a topology on 𝑋.

When referring to the topology  generated by the subbasis  we mean the topology generated by the associated basis . We have  ⊆  ⊆  .

26 4.4. Exercises

The following theorem provides an explicit description of the topology generated by a collection of subsets  of a set 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a subbasis for a topology on 𝑋. The collection  generated by  consisting of all unions of all basis elements of the associated basis  is a topology on 𝑋. When referring to the topology  generated by the subbasis  we mean the topology generated by the associated basis . We have  ⊆  ⊆  .
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about topology generated by a subbasis that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Universal Property of the Topology Generated by a Subbasis
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a subbasis for a topology on 𝑋. Then there is a unique topology ⟨⟩ generated by  which is coarser than any other topology containing , where { } ⋃⋂ 𝑛𝜆 | | ⟨⟩ = 𝑆𝜆,𝑖 | 𝑆𝜆,𝑖 ∈  . | 𝜆∈Λ 𝑖=1 |

In other words, ⟨⟩ is the topology for which  is a subbasis. Proof. Since the discrete topology disc = (𝑋), there is at least one topology on 𝑋 that contains . We know from Theorem 4.1 that taking the intersection of all topologies that contain  is again a topology which clearly still contains . By construction, ⟨⟩ is then contained in any other topology containing . Thus ⟨⟩ is the unique topology with this property. Let { } ⋃⋂ 𝑛𝜆 | |  = 𝑆𝜆,𝑖 | 𝑆𝜆,𝑖 ∈  . | 𝜆∈Λ 𝑖=1 | Then  ⊆ ⟨⟩. We need to prove that they are equal. To do this we will prove that  is a topology on 𝑋 that contains . Hence, by the first part ⟨⟩ =  . Since  is a subbasis for a topology on 𝑋, by Lemma 4.14 we know that the topology generated by  is equal to the collection of all unions of basis elements of the associated  to . Hence,  is a topology on 𝑋. We end this section with a theorem about continuity and (sub)basis.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be a subbasis for a topology on 𝑋. Then there is a unique topology ⟨⟩ generated by  which is coarser than any other topology containing , where { } ⋃⋂ 𝑛𝜆 | | ⟨⟩ = 𝑆𝜆,𝑖 | 𝑆𝜆,𝑖 ∈  . | 𝜆∈Λ 𝑖=1 | In other words, ⟨⟩ is the topology for which  is a subbasis.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about universal property of the topology generated by a subbasis; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \langle\mathcal{S}\rangle=\left\{\bigcup_{\lambda\in\Lambda}\bigcap_{i=1}^{n_\lambda}S_{\lambda,i}\mid S_{\lambda,i}\in\mathcal{S}\right\}

nodes.title: Continuity Tested on a Basis or Subbasis
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let  (resp., ) be a basis (resp., subbasis) for the topology on 𝑌 . Then a map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous if and only if for each 𝐵 ∈  (resp. 𝑆 ∈ ) the preimage 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) (resp., 𝑓 −1 (𝑆)) is open in 𝑋.

Proof. We prove the statement about basis. Assume that 𝑓 is continuous. Since each basis element 𝐵 ∈  is open in 𝑌 , then by continuity 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) is open in 𝑋. Assume that for each 𝐵 ∈  the preimage 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) is open in 𝑋. Let 𝑌 be the topology on 𝑌 . ⋃ Since every 𝑉 ∈ 𝑌 is a union 𝑉 = 𝜆∈Λ 𝐵𝜆 of basis elements 𝐵𝜆 ∈ , we have ⋃ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝐵𝜆 ). 𝜆∈Λ

Thus if each 𝑓 −1 (𝐵𝜆 ) is open in 𝑋, so is 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ).

4.4 Exercises

Exercise 4.1 Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, and let

 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋}

be a topology on 𝑋. Show that  = {{𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}} is a subbasis for  . Is  ′ = {{𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑑, 𝑒}} a subbasis for  ?

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 27

Exercise 4.2 Let  be the collection of all open intervals (𝑎, 𝑏) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ 𝑎 < 𝑥 < 𝑏} in ℝ.

(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℝ. The topology generated by  is called the standard topology on ℝ denoted by std .

(b) Show that std = 𝑑 where 𝑑 is the metric topology obtained from the metric 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) = |𝑥 − 𝑦|.

Exercise 4.3 Show that

 = {(𝑎, ∞) ∣ 𝑎 ∈ ℝ} ∪ {(−∞, 𝑏) ∣ 𝑏 ∈ ℝ}

is a subbasis for the standard topology on ℝ.

Exercise 4.4 Let ℚ deonte the set of rational numbers, and let ℝ denote the set of real numbers. Show that  = {(𝑎, 𝑏) ∣ 𝑎 < 𝑏, 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ ℚ} is a basis for the standard topology on ℝ where (𝑎, 𝑏) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ 𝑎 < 𝑥 < 𝑏}.

Exercise 4.5 Let  be the collection of all half-open intervals of the form [𝑎, 𝑏) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ 𝑎 ⩽ 𝑥 < 𝑏} in ℝ.

(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℝ. The topology generated by  is called the lower limit topology on ℝ.

(b) Find the closure of the subset (0, 1) of ℝ given the lower limit topology.

Exercise 4.6 For each 𝑛 ∈ ℤ, let { {𝑛} if 𝑛 is odd, 𝐵(𝑛) = {𝑛 − 1, 𝑛, 𝑛 + 1} if 𝑛 is even.

Show that the collection  = {𝐵(𝑛) ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ} is a basis for a topology on ℤ. The topology gen- erated by  is known as the digital line topology on ℤ. See [1, pp. 44–46] for some applications of this topology.

Exercise 4.7 Let  be the collection of all subsets of the form 𝐴𝑎,𝑏 = {𝑎𝑧 + 𝑏 ∣ 𝑧 ∈ ℤ} of ℤ, where 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ ℤ and 𝑎 ≠ 0. (The set 𝐴𝑎,𝑏 is known as an arithmetic progression.)

(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℤ.

(b) Show that there are infinitely many primes by using the topology generated by . (This topology is known as the arithmetic progression topology on ℤ and it was used originally by Furstenberg [3] to show that there are infinitely many primes.)

28 4.4. Exercises

Exercise 4.8 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. Show that a subset 𝐴 of 𝑋 is dense in 𝑋 if and only if every non-empty basis element in  intersects 𝐴. (Recall that a set 𝑈 intersects a set 𝑉 if 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 ≠ ∅.)

5. Constructing topological spaces

5.1 Subspaces Let 𝐴 be a subset of a topological space 𝑋. There is a natural way to define a topology on 𝐴 that is based on the topology on 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let  (resp., ) be a basis (resp., subbasis) for the topology on 𝑌 . Then a map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous if and only if for each 𝐵 ∈  (resp. 𝑆 ∈ ) the preimage 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) (resp., 𝑓 −1 (𝑆)) is open in 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about continuity tested on a basis or subbasis; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y\text{ continuous}\iff \forall B\in\mathcal{B},\ f^{-1}(B)\text{ is open in }X\iff \forall S\in\mathcal{S},\ f^{-1}(S)\text{ is open in }X

nodes.title: Subspace topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. The collection 𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋} of subsets of 𝐴 is called the subspace topology on 𝐴.

The subspace topology is indeed a topology.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. The collection 𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋} of subsets of 𝐴 is called the subspace topology on 𝐴. The subspace topology is indeed a topology.
nodes.explanation: Defines subspace topology and fixes terminology used later in open-sets, sets, subspace.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}_A=\{A\cap U\mid U\in\mathcal{T}_X\}

nodes.title: Subspace Topology Is a Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Then the collection 𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋} is a topology on 𝐴.

Proof. Let  denote the topology on 𝑋. Since ∅, 𝑋 ∈  , ∅ = 𝐴 ∩ ∅ and 𝐴 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑋, then, clearly, ∅, 𝐴 ∈ 𝐴 . Hence, T1 is satisfied. Let {𝑉𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of subsets of 𝐴 who are open in 𝐴, i.e., 𝑉𝜆 ∈ 𝐴 . We must show that ⋃ 𝜆∈Λ 𝑉𝜆 ∈ 𝐴 . For each 𝜆 ∈ Λ there is a 𝑈𝜆 ∈  such that 𝑉𝜆 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈𝜆 . Thus ⋃ ⋃( ) ⋃ 𝑉𝜆 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈𝜆 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈𝜆 . 𝜆∈Λ 𝜆∈Λ 𝜆∈Λ ⋃ ⋃ Since 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 ∈  , it follows that 𝜆∈Λ 𝑉𝜆 ∈ 𝐴 . Hence, T2 is satisfied. Let 𝑉1 , 𝑉2 , … , 𝑉𝑛 be subsets of 𝐴 that are open in 𝐴, i.e., 𝑉𝑖 ∈ 𝐴 for 𝑖 = 1, 2, … , 𝑛. We must ⋂ show that 𝑛𝑖=1 𝑉𝑖 ∈ 𝐴 . For each 𝑖 ∈ {1, 2, … , 𝑛} there is a 𝑈𝑖 ∈  such that 𝑉𝑖 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈𝑖 . Thus

𝑛 𝑛 𝑛 ⋂ ⋂ ( ) ⋂ 𝑉𝑖 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈𝑖 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈𝑖 . 𝑖=1 𝑖=1 𝑖=1

⋂𝑛 ⋂𝑛 Since 𝑖=1 𝑈𝑖 ∈  , it follows that 𝑖=1 𝑉𝑖 ∈ 𝐴 . Hence, T3 is satisfied.

Let 𝑋 be a topological space (with topology  ), and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. If 𝑉 is a subset of 𝐴, there are two possible meanings to the statement “𝑉 is open.” We can either take 𝑉 to be open in 𝑋, i.e., 𝑉 ∈  , or we can take 𝑉 to be open in 𝐴, i.e., 𝑉 ∈ 𝐴 . In general, these do not mean the same thing. We say that 𝑉 is closed in 𝐴 if 𝑉 is closed in the subspace topology of 𝐴. In other words, if 𝑉 is closed in 𝐴, then 𝐴 ⧵ 𝑉 is open in 𝐴.

30 5.1. Subspaces
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Then the collection 𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋} is a topology on 𝐴.
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about subspace topology is a topology that supports later statements or proofs.
