nodes.title: Closed Sets in a Subspace
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Then a subset 𝐾 of 𝐴 is closed in 𝐴 if and only if there is a closed subset 𝐿 of 𝑋 with 𝐾 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝐿.

Proof. Assume that 𝐾 is a closed subset of 𝐴. Then 𝑉 = 𝐴 ⧵ 𝐾 is open in 𝐴, i.e., there is an open subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 with 𝑉 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 . Moreover, 𝐿 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 is a closed subset of 𝑋 and

𝐴 ∩ 𝐿 = 𝐴 ∩ (𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 ) = 𝐴 ⧵ (𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ) = 𝐴 ⧵ 𝑉 = 𝐾.

See Figure 5.1.

𝑋

𝐴 𝑈

𝐾

𝑉

Figure 5.1: 𝐾 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝐿

Now assume that 𝐿 is a closed subset of 𝑋 with 𝐾 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝐿. Then 𝑈 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝐿 is an open subset of 𝑋, and so, 𝑉 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 is an open subset of 𝐴. Furthermore,

𝐴 ⧵ 𝐾 = 𝐴 ⧵ (𝐴 ∩ 𝐿) = 𝐴 ∩ (𝑋 ⧵ 𝐿) = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 = 𝑉 .

See Figure 5.2. Hence, 𝐾 is a closed subset of 𝐴.

𝑋 𝐿 𝐴

𝑉

𝐾

Figure 5.2: 𝑉 = 𝐴 ⧵ 𝐾

The next example illustrates the fact that we may have subsets of a topological space 𝑋 that are open in the subspace 𝐴 but which are not open in 𝑋.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 31
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Then a subset 𝐾 of 𝐴 is closed in 𝐴 if and only if there is a closed subset 𝐿 of 𝑋 with 𝐾 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝐿.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about closed sets in a subspace; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: K\subseteq A\text{ closed in }A\iff \exists L\subseteq X\text{ closed in }X:\ K=A\cap L

nodes.title: Half-Open Intervals as Open Sets in a Subspace
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf. Exercise 4.2, and let 𝐼 = [0, 1] be a subspace of ℝ. Then sets of the form [0, 𝑎) and (𝑎, 1] with 0 < 𝑎 < 1 are open in 𝐼 but not in ℝ.

The following theorem describes how we may extract a basis for the subspace topology on 𝐴 from the basis of a topology on 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf. Exercise 4.2, and let 𝐼 = [0, 1] be a subspace of ℝ. Then sets of the form [0, 𝑎) and (𝑎, 1] with 0 < 𝑎 < 1 are open in 𝐼 but not in ℝ.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates half-open intervals as open sets in a subspace in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Bases for Subspace Topologies
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. If 𝐴 is a subset of 𝑋, then the collection

𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ∣ 𝐵 ∈ }

is a basis for the subspace topology on 𝐴.

Proof. We need to prove that 𝐴 is a basis for the subspace topology on 𝐴. We first prove that 𝐴 is a basis for a topology on 𝐴, and then that the topology generated by 𝐴 equals the subspace topology on 𝐴. First note that each 𝐵 ∈  is open in 𝑋, and so, each 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ∈ 𝐴 is open in 𝐴. For each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 there is a basis element 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵. Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴. Since 𝐴 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑋, there must be a basis element 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵. Hence, B1 holds. Now let 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ (𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 ) where 𝐵1 and 𝐵2 are basis elements of . Since  is a basis for the topology on 𝑋, it follows that there is a basis element 𝐵3 of  such that 𝐵3 ⊆ 𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵3 ⊆ 𝐴 ∩ (𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 ). Hence, B2 is satisfied. Thus 𝐴 is a basis for a topology on 𝐴. Let 𝐴 be the subspace topology on 𝐴. We want to prove that the topology  ′ generated by 𝐴 is equal to 𝐴 . If 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 , then, using the fact that  is a basis for the topology on 𝑋, we have 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 . Thus 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∈  ′ , cf. Theorem 4.9. By Theorem 4.10 we know that  ′ is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of 𝐴 . Hence, if 𝑊 ∈  ′ then 𝑊 equals a union of elements of 𝐴 . Since each element of 𝐴 belongs to 𝐴 and 𝐴 is a topology, 𝑊 also belongs to 𝐴 .

We end this section with an alternative description of the subspace topology. Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑇 be a set. There do exist topologies on 𝑇 that make 𝑓 ∶ 𝑇 → 𝑋 continuous, e.g., the discrete topology. If 𝑓 is the intersection of all topologies on 𝑇 such that 𝑓 is continuous, then 𝑓 is the coarsest topology for which 𝑓 is continuous and

𝑓 = {𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋}.

From this we may define the subspace topology as follows: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be subset of 𝑋. The subspace topology on 𝐴 is then the coarsest topology on 𝐴 for which the inclusion 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋, given by 𝑖(𝑥) = 𝑥 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴, is continuous. This coincides with our previous definition as 𝑖−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 for any subset 𝑈 of 𝑋. Thus

𝑖 = {𝑖−1 (𝑈 ) ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋} = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋},

and hence, 𝑖 = 𝐴 . The following theorem describes a universal property for the subspace topology.

32 5.1. Subspaces
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. If 𝐴 is a subset of 𝑋, then the collection 𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ∣ 𝐵 ∈ }
nodes.explanation: States a main result about bases for subspace topologies; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{B}_A=\{A\cap B\mid B\in\mathcal{B}\}\text{ is a basis for }A

nodes.title: Universal Property of the Subspace Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Then the subspace topology on 𝐴 is the only topology on 𝐴 with the following universal property: for every topo- logical space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝐴, 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑖◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is continuous where 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map given by 𝑖(𝑥) = 𝑥 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴.

𝑋 𝑖◦𝑓 𝑖

𝑌 𝐴 𝑓

Proof. We will first prove that the subspace topology 𝐴 has the universal property that for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝐴, 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑖◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is continuous. Then we will prove that 𝐴 is the only topology on 𝐴 with this property. Consider 𝐴 as a subspace of 𝑋. Assume that 𝑓 is continuous. Since the inclusion map 𝑖 is con- tinuous (with 𝐴 given the subspace topology), and the composition of two continuous maps is again continuous, cf. Theorem 3.16, it follows that 𝑖◦𝑓 is continuous. Now assume that 𝑖◦𝑓 is continuous. Let 𝑉 be an open set in 𝐴, i.e., 𝑉 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 for some open set 𝑈 in 𝑋. Since

𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑖−1 (𝑈 )) = (𝑖◦𝑓 )−1 (𝑈 )

is open in 𝑌 by continuity of 𝑖◦𝑓 , it follows that 𝑓 is continuous. Thus the subspace topology 𝐴 has the desired property. Let  ′ be a topology on 𝐴 with the universal property that for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝐴, 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑖◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is continuous. We must show that 𝐴 =  ′ . Let  be the topology on 𝑋, and let 𝐴 be given the topology  ′ . First let 𝑌 = 𝐴 with the subspace topology. Then for 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝐴, 𝐴 ) → (𝐴,  ′ ), we have 𝑖◦ id = 𝑖 ∶ (𝐴, 𝐴 ) → (𝑋,  ) which is continuous. Hence, by the universal property id is continuous.

(𝑋,  ) 𝑖◦ id 𝑖

(𝐴, 𝐴 ) (𝐴,  ′ ) id

Thus any 𝑉 ∈  ′ must also be an element of 𝐴 , and so,  ′ ⊆ 𝐴 . Secondly let 𝑌 = 𝐴 with  ′ as its topology. Then, clearly, 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝐴,  ′ ) → (𝐴,  ′ ) is continu- ous. Thus by the universal property it follows that 𝑖◦ id = 𝑖 ∶ (𝐴,  ′ ) → (𝑋,  ) is continuous.

(𝑋,  ) 𝑖◦ id 𝑖

(𝐴,  ′ ) (𝐴,  ′ ) id

Thus for any 𝑈 ∈  , we have 𝑖−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∈  ′ . That is, 𝐴 ⊆  ′ . Hence, 𝐴 =  ′ .

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 33

5.2 Products Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. If we want to give the product 𝑋 × 𝑌 a topology, a first approach might be to take the collection  = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 } as the topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 . However,  is not a topology. The union of two elements of  is not necessarily of the form 𝑈 × 𝑉 for some 𝑈 open in 𝑋 and some 𝑉 open in 𝑌 . See Figure 5.3.

𝑌

𝑉1 𝑈1 × 𝑉1

𝑉2 𝑈2 × 𝑉2

𝑋 𝑈1 𝑈2

Figure 5.3: The collection  of all products of open sets in 𝑋 and in 𝑌 is not a topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .

We can remedy the situation by taking  as a basis instead. The topology generated from this basis is what we will take to be the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Then the subspace topology on 𝐴 is the only topology on 𝐴 with the following universal property: for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝐴, 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑖◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is continuous where 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map given by 𝑖(𝑥) = 𝑥 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴. 𝑋 𝑖◦𝑓 𝑖
nodes.explanation: States a main result about universal property of the subspace topology; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:Y\to A\text{ continuous}\iff i\circ f:Y\to X\text{ continuous}

nodes.title: Product topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. The product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 is the topology generated by the basis

 = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 }.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. The product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 is the topology generated by the basis  = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 }.
nodes.explanation: Defines product topology and fixes terminology used later in basis, open-sets, product.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}_{X\times Y}=\left\langle\{U\times V\mid U\in\mathcal{T}_X,\ V\in\mathcal{T}_Y\}\right\rangle

nodes.title: Rectangles Form a Basis for the Product Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Then the collection

 = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 }

is a basis for a topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .

Proof. Let (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 . We need to show that there is a basis element 𝑈 × 𝑉 ∈  such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑈 × 𝑉 ⊆ 𝑋 × 𝑌 . Since 𝑋 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑌 is open in 𝑌 , we simply take 𝑈 = 𝑋 and 𝑉 = 𝑌 . Thus B1 is satisfied. Now let (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ (𝑈1 × 𝑉1 ) ∩ (𝑈2 × 𝑉2 ) where 𝑈1 × 𝑉1 , 𝑈2 × 𝑉2 ∈ . Since (𝑈1 × 𝑉1 ) ∩ (𝑈2 × 𝑉2 ) = (𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ) × (𝑉1 ∩ 𝑉2 ), and 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 and 𝑉1 ∩ 𝑉2 are open in 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively, it follows by letting 𝑈3 = 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 and 𝑉3 = 𝑉1 ∩𝑉2 that there is a basis element 𝑈3 ×𝑉3 ∈  such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑈3 ×𝑉3 ⊆ (𝑈1 ×𝑉1 )∩(𝑈2 ×𝑉2 ). Hence, B2 holds. Thus  is a basis for a topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .

34 5.2. Products

The basis described in Lemma 5.8 is relatively large; it consists of pairs of every open set 𝑈 in 𝑋 and every open set 𝑉 in 𝑌 . The following theorem describes a smaller basis for the product topology based on bases rather than whole topologies.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Then the collection  = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 }
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about rectangles form a basis for the product topology that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Product Basis from Bases on Factors
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. If 𝑋 is a basis for the topology on 𝑋 and 𝑌 is a basis for the topology on 𝑌 , then the collection

𝑋×𝑌 = {𝐵𝑋 × 𝐵𝑌 ∣ 𝐵𝑋 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝐵𝑌 ∈ 𝑌 }

is a basis for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proof of Theorem 5.5 and adapt them to our current setting. First note that each 𝐵𝑋 × 𝐵𝑌 ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 is open in 𝑋 × 𝑌 as each 𝐵𝑋 is open in 𝑋 and each 𝐵𝑌 is open in 𝑌 . Let (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 . Using the fact that 𝑋 and 𝑌 are bases for topologies on 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively, there are basis elements 𝐵𝑋 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝐵𝑌 ∈ 𝑌 such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝐵𝑋 × 𝐵𝑌 ⊆ 𝑋 × 𝑌 . Thus B1 is satisfied. Now let (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ (𝐵𝑋,1 × 𝐵𝑌 ,1 ) ∩ (𝐵𝑋,2 × 𝐵𝑌 ,2 ) where 𝐵𝑋,1 , 𝐵𝑋,2 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝐵𝑌 ,1 , 𝐵𝑌 ,2 ∈ 𝑌 . Since (𝐵𝑋,1 × 𝐵𝑌 ,1 ) ∩ (𝐵𝑋,2 × 𝐵𝑌 ,2 ) = (𝐵𝑋,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑋,2 ) × (𝐵𝑌 ,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑌 ,2 ) and 𝑋 and 𝑌 are bases for the topologies on 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively, it follows that there are basis elements 𝐵𝑋,3 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝐵𝑌 ,3 ∈ 𝑌 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵𝑋,3 ⊆ 𝐵𝑋,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑋,2 , 𝑦 ∈ 𝐵𝑌 ,3 ⊆ 𝐵𝑌 ,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑌 ,2 and (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝐵𝑋,3 × 𝐵𝑌 ,3 ⊆ (𝐵𝑋,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑋,2 ) × (𝐵𝑌 ,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑌 ,2 ). Thus B2 holds. Hence, 𝑋×𝑌 is a basis for a topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 . Let 𝑋×𝑌 be the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 . We want to prove that the topology  ′ generated by 𝑋×𝑌 is equal to 𝑋×𝑌 . Let 𝑊 ∈ 𝑋×𝑌 , and let (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑊 . Then there is an open set 𝑈 in 𝑋 and an open set 𝑉 in 𝑌 such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑈 × 𝑉 ⊆ 𝑊 , cf. Theorem 4.9. Since 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑋 is a basis for the topology on 𝑋, it follows that there is a basis element 𝐵𝑋 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 . Likewise, there is a basis element 𝐵𝑌 ∈ 𝑌 such that 𝑦 ∈ 𝐵𝑌 ⊆ 𝑉 . Thus (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝐵𝑋 × 𝐵𝑌 ⊆ 𝑊 , and so, 𝑊 ∈  ′ , cf. Theorem 4.9. By Theorem 4.10, we know that  ′ is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of 𝑋×𝑌 . Hence, if 𝑊 ∈  ′ then 𝑊 equals a union of elements of 𝑋×𝑌 . Since each element of 𝑋×𝑌 belongs to 𝑋×𝑌 and 𝑋×𝑌 is a topology, 𝑊 also belongs to 𝑋×𝑌 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. If 𝑋 is a basis for the topology on 𝑋 and 𝑌 is a basis for the topology on 𝑌 , then the collection 𝑋×𝑌 = {𝐵𝑋 × 𝐵𝑌 ∣ 𝐵𝑋 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝐵𝑌 ∈ 𝑌 }
nodes.explanation: States a main result about product basis from bases on factors; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{B}_{X\times Y}=\{B_X\times B_Y\mid B_X\in\mathcal{B}_X,\ B_Y\in\mathcal{B}_Y\}

nodes.title: Computing a Product Topology on Finite Spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} and 𝑌 = {1, 2, 3} with topologies 𝑋 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} and 𝑌 = {∅, {1}, {1, 2}, 𝑌 }, respectively. Then the collection { 𝑋×𝑌 = {𝑎, 𝑏} × {1}, {𝑏} × {1}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × {1}, {𝑎, 𝑏} × {1, 2}, {𝑏} × {1, 2}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × {1, 2}, } {𝑎, 𝑏} × 𝑌 , {𝑏} × 𝑌 , {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × 𝑌

is a basis for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} and 𝑌 = {1, 2, 3} with topologies 𝑋 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} and 𝑌 = {∅, {1}, {1, 2}, 𝑌 }, respectively. Then the collection { 𝑋×𝑌 = {𝑎, 𝑏} × {1}, {𝑏} × {1}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × {1}, {𝑎, 𝑏} × {1, 2}, {𝑏} × {1, 2}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × {1, 2}, } {𝑎, 𝑏} × 𝑌 , {𝑏} × 𝑌 , {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × 𝑌 is a basis for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .
nodes.explanation: Illustrates computing a product topology on finite spaces in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Open Rectangles in the Euclidean Plane
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf. Exercise 4.2. Then the collection ℝ2 = {(𝑎, 𝑏) × (𝑐, 𝑑) ∣ 𝑎 < 𝑏, 𝑐 < 𝑑} of open rectangular regions in ℝ2 is a basis for the product topology on ℝ2 = ℝ × ℝ, as a basis for the standard topology of ℝ is the collection of open intervals of the form (𝑎, 𝑏) where 𝑎 < 𝑏.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 35
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf. Exercise 4.2. Then the collection ℝ2 = {(𝑎, 𝑏) × (𝑐, 𝑑) ∣ 𝑎 < 𝑏, 𝑐 < 𝑑} of open rectangular regions in ℝ2 is a basis for the product topology on ℝ2 = ℝ × ℝ, as a basis for the standard topology of ℝ is the collection of open intervals of the form (𝑎, 𝑏) where 𝑎 < 𝑏.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates open rectangles in the euclidean plane in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: The Circle as a Subspace of the Plane
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑆 1 denote the circle {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ ℝ2 ∣ 𝑥2 + 𝑦2 = 1} seen as a subspace of ℝ2 (with the standard topology), i.e., 𝑆 1 is given the subspace topology. A basis element 𝐵 for the product topology on the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 is illustrated in Figure 5.4. Note that the surface depicted in Figure 5.4 is homeomorphic to 𝑇 2 ; it is the surface of revolution generated by revolving a circle, say of radius 1 in the 𝑥𝑧-plane with center (2, 0, 0), about an axis, e.g., the 𝑧-axis.

Figure 5.4: An illustration of a basis element 𝐵 for the product topology on 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 .

We end this section with an alternative description of the product topology. The map pr 1 ∶ 𝑋×𝑌 → 𝑋 given by pr 1 (𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑥 for (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 is called the projection of 𝑋 × 𝑌 onto 𝑋. Similarly, the map pr 2 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝑌 → 𝑌 given by pr 2 (𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑦 for (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 is called the projection of 𝑋 × 𝑌 onto 𝑌 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑆 1 denote the circle {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ ℝ2 ∣ 𝑥2 + 𝑦2 = 1} seen as a subspace of ℝ2 (with the standard topology), i.e., 𝑆 1 is given the subspace topology. A basis element 𝐵 for the product topology on the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 is illustrated in Figure 5.4. Note that the surface depicted in Figure 5.4 is homeomorphic to 𝑇 2 ; it is the surface of revolution generated by revolving a circle, say of radius 1 in the 𝑥𝑧-plane with center (2, 0, 0), about an axis, e.g., the 𝑧-axis. Figure 5.4: An illustration of a basis element 𝐵 for the product topology on 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 .
nodes.explanation: Illustrates the circle as a subspace of the plane in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Universal Property of the Product Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Let pr 1 ∶ 𝑋×𝑌 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ∶ 𝑋×𝑌 → 𝑌 be the projections of 𝑋 × 𝑌 onto its first and second factors, respectively. The product topology is the only topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 with the following universal property: for every topological space 𝑍 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if pr 1 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑌 are continuous.

𝑋×𝑌 𝑋×𝑌 𝑓 𝑓 pr 1 pr 2

𝑍 𝑋 𝑍 𝑌 pr 1 ◦𝑓 pr 2 ◦𝑓

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proof of Theorem 5.6 and adapt them to our current setting. We first prove that the product topology 𝑋×𝑌 has the universal property that for every topolog- ical space 𝑍 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if pr 1 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑌 are continuous. Let 𝑋 × 𝑌 be given the product topology, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 be continuous. Since pr −1 1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑈 × 𝑌 for an open set 𝑈 in 𝑋 and 𝑌 is open in 𝑌 , it follows that pr 1 is continuous. Likewise, pr 2 ∶ 𝑋 ×

36 5.2. Products

𝑌 → 𝑌 is continuous. Thus by Theorem 3.16 both pr 1 ◦𝑓 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 are continuous. Now assume that pr 1 ◦𝑓 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 are continuous. Let 𝑈 × 𝑉 be a subset of 𝑋 × 𝑌 where 𝑈 is an open set in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is an open set in 𝑌 . Since 𝑈 × 𝑉 = pr −1 (𝑈 ) ∩ pr −1 (𝑉 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 × 𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (pr −1 (𝑈 ) ∩ pr −1 (𝑉 )) = 𝑓 −1 (pr −1 (𝑈 )) ∩ 𝑓 −1 (pr −1 (𝑉 )) = (pr 1 ◦𝑓 )−1 (𝑈 ) ∩ (pr 2 ◦𝑓 )−1 (𝑉 ) is open in 𝑍 by continuity of pr 1 ◦𝑓 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 , and subsets of the form 𝑈 × 𝑉 form a basis for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 , cf. Lemma 5.8, it follows that 𝑓 is continuous. Let  be a topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 with the universal property that for every topological space 𝑍 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if pr 1 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑌 are continuous. We must show that 𝑋×𝑌 =  . Let 𝑋 be the topology on 𝑋 and 𝑌 be the topology on 𝑌 , and let 𝑋 × 𝑌 be given the topology  . First let 𝑍 = 𝑋 × 𝑌 with the product topology. Then for 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑋×𝑌 ) → (𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ), we have pr 1 ◦ id = pr 1 ∶ (𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑋×𝑌 ) → (𝑋, 𝑋 ) and pr 2 ◦ id = pr 2 ∶ (𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑋×𝑌 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑌 ) which are both continuous. Thus by the universal property id is continuous.

(𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) (𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) id id pr 1 pr 2

(𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑋×𝑌 ) (𝑋, 𝑋 ) (𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑋×𝑌 ) (𝑌 , 𝑌 ) pr 1 ◦ id pr 2 ◦ id

Hence, any 𝑊 ∈  must also be an element of 𝑋×𝑌 , and so,  ⊆ 𝑋×𝑌 . Secondly let 𝑍 = 𝑋 ×𝑌 with  as its topology. Then, clearly, 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝑋 ×𝑌 ,  ) → (𝑋 ×𝑌 ,  ) is continuous. Thus by the universal property it follows that both pr 1 ◦ id = pr 1 ∶ (𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) → (𝑋, 𝑋 ) and pr 2 ◦ id = pr 2 ∶ (𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) → (𝑌 , 𝑌 ) are continuous.

(𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) (𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) id id pr 1 pr 2

(𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) (𝑋, 𝑋 ) (𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) (𝑌 , 𝑌 ) pr 1 ◦ id pr 2 ◦ id

Thus for each 𝑈 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝑉 ∈ 𝑌 , we have pr −1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑈 × 𝑌 ∈  and pr −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑋 × 𝑉 ∈  , and so, 𝑈 × 𝑉 = pr −1 (𝑈 ) ∩ pr −1 (𝑉 ) ∈  . Hence, 𝑋×𝑌 ⊆  . Thus 𝑋×𝑌 =  .

We can extend our discussion of the product topology from 𝑋 × 𝑌 to 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 where each 𝑋𝑖 ∏ is a topological space. If we are to extend to the product 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 , which we can think of as the set of maps ⋃ 𝑓 ∶ Λ → 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 where 𝑓 (𝜆) ∈ 𝑋𝜆 for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ, of an indexed family {𝑋𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ of topological spaces we ∏ ∏ may proceed in two ways. We may equip 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 with the topology generated by the basis 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 where ∏ 𝑈𝜆 is open in 𝑋𝜆 for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. This is known as the box topology. We may also equip 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 with the ⋃ topology generated by the subbasis  = 𝜇∈Λ {pr −1 𝜇 (𝑈𝜇 ) ∣ 𝑈𝜇 is open in 𝑋𝜇 }. This is known as the product ∏𝑛 topology. For finite products 𝑖=1 𝑋𝑖 the two topologies are the same. Also, the box topology is, in general, finer than the product topology. Finally, several results regarding finite products may be extended to arbitrary products when using the product topology but not the box topology.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 37

5.3 Quotient spaces Let 𝑋 be a topological space. In Section 5.1, we discussed how to define the coarsest possible topology on a subset 𝐴 of 𝑋 such that the inclusion map 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is continuous. This is known as the subspace topology. If we let 𝐴 be a set which is not necessarily a subset of 𝑋 and we consider a surjective map 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴, the quotient topology is the finest topology on 𝐴 such that 𝜋 is continuous. The torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 (see Figure 5.4) can be constructed by taking a rectangle and “gluing” its edges together in an appropriate way as shown in Figure 5.5. Such a construction involves the concept of quotient topology.

nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Let pr 1 ∶ 𝑋×𝑌 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ∶ 𝑋×𝑌 → 𝑌 be the projections of 𝑋 × 𝑌 onto its first and second factors, respectively. The product topology is the only topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 with the following universal property: for every topological space 𝑍 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if pr 1 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑌 are continuous. 𝑋×𝑌 𝑋×𝑌 𝑓 𝑓 pr 1 pr 2
nodes.explanation: States a main result about universal property of the product topology; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:Z\to X\times Y\text{ continuous}\iff \operatorname{pr}_1\circ f\text{ and }\operatorname{pr}_2\circ f\text{ are continuous}

nodes.title: Equivalence classes
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let ∼ be an equivalence relation on 𝑋. The equivalence class of 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 is the subset

[𝑥] = {𝑦 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑥 ∼ 𝑦}

of 𝑋. Let 𝑋∕∼ = {[𝑥] ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋} be the set of equivalence classes.

By definition, 𝑥 ∈ [𝑥] for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and [𝑥] = [𝑦] if and only if 𝑥 ∼ 𝑦. Moreover, two equivalence classes [𝑥1 ] and [𝑥2 ] are either disjoint or equal. Finally, the union of all equivalence classes equal 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let ∼ be an equivalence relation on 𝑋. The equivalence class of 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 is the subset [𝑥] = {𝑦 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑥 ∼ 𝑦}
nodes.explanation: Defines equivalence classes and fixes terminology used later in sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: [x]=\{y\in X\mid x\sim y\}

nodes.title: Quotient Topology Is a Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝐴 be sets, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a surjective map. Then the map

𝜑 ∶ 𝑋∕∼ → 𝐴

given by 𝜑([𝑥]) = 𝜋(𝑥), where 𝑥1 ∼ 𝑥2 if and only if 𝜋(𝑥1 ) = 𝜋(𝑥2 ), is a bijection.

Proof. The map is well-defined since [𝑥1 ] = [𝑥2 ] only if 𝑥1 ∼ 𝑥2 , and so, 𝜋(𝑥1 ) = 𝜋(𝑥2 ) by definition of the equivalence relation. It is injective since 𝜑([𝑥1 ]) = 𝜑([𝑥2 ]) implies 𝜋(𝑥1 ) = 𝜋(𝑥2 ), and so, 𝑥1 ∼ 𝑥2 , i.e., [𝑥1 ] = [𝑥2 ]. Finally, it is surjective since 𝜋 is; every element of 𝐴 is of the form 𝜋(𝑥) = 𝜑([𝑥]) for some 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋.

Thus by Lemma 5.15 we can, up to a bijection, go back and forth between equivalence relations on 𝑋 and surjective maps 𝑋 → 𝐴.

38 5.3. Quotient spaces
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝐴 be sets, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a surjective map. Then the map 𝜑 ∶ 𝑋∕∼ → 𝐴
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about quotient topology is a topology that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Quotient space
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, let 𝐴 be a set, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a surjective map. The quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋 is the collection of subsets 𝑈 of 𝐴 such that 𝜋 −1 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑋. We say that 𝜋 is a quotient map if 𝐴 is given the quotient topology, and we call 𝐴 the quotient space.

In other words, 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 is quotient map if it is surjective and a subset 𝑈 of 𝐴 is open in 𝐴 if and only if 𝜋 −1 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑋. Equivalently, 𝜋 is a quotient map if it is surjective and 𝑈 is closed in 𝐴 if and only if 𝜋 −1 (𝑈 ) is closed in 𝑋. Clearly, a quotient map is continuous.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a topological space, let $A$ be a set, and let $\pi:X\to A$ be surjective. The quotient topology on $A$ induced by $\pi$ consists of those subsets $U\subseteq A$ for which $\pi^{-1}(U)$ is open in $X$; with this topology, $A$ is called a quotient space.
nodes.explanation: A quotient space is built by declaring a subset of the target open exactly when its full preimage under the quotient map is open.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}_A=\{U\subseteq A\mid \pi^{-1}(U)\in\mathcal{T}_X\}

nodes.title: Universal Property of the Quotient Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, let 𝐴 be a set, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a surjective map. Then the quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋 is a topology and it is the finest topology on 𝐴 such that 𝜋 is continuous.

Proof. Since 𝜋 −1 (∅) = ∅ and 𝜋 −1 (𝐴) = 𝑋, and both ∅ and 𝑋 are open in 𝑋, it follows that ∅ and 𝐴 are open in 𝐴. Thus T1 holds. Let {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of subsets of 𝐴 that are open in 𝐴. Then 𝜋 −1 (𝑈𝜆 ) is open in 𝑋 for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. Since ( ) ⋃ ⋃ −1 𝜋 𝑈𝜆 = 𝜋 −1 (𝑈𝜆 ) 𝜆∈Λ 𝜆∈Λ ⋃ is a union of open sets in 𝑋, it must be open in 𝑋. Hence, 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 is open in 𝐴. Thus T2 is satisfied. We prove that the intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of the quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋 is also an element of the quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋. Let 𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 be subsets of 𝐴 that are open in 𝐴. Then 𝜋 −1 (𝑈𝑖 ) is open in 𝑋 for 𝑖 = 1, 2, … , 𝑛. Since ( 𝑛 ) 𝑛 ⋂ ⋂ 𝜋 −1 𝑈𝑖 = 𝜋 −1 (𝑈𝑖 ) 𝑖=1 𝑖=1

is a (finite) intersection of open sets in 𝑋, it must be open in 𝑋. Hence, 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 is open in 𝐴, and so, T3 is satisfied. Thus the quotient topology is a topology. Let  be a topology on 𝐴 such that 𝜋 is continuous. We must show that  is coarser than the quotient topology. Since 𝜋 is continuous when 𝐴 is given  as its topology, we have for each 𝑉 ∈  that 𝜋 −1 (𝑉 ) is open in 𝑋, and so, 𝑉 is in the quotient topology. Hence,  is coarser than the quotient topology.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, let 𝐴 be a set, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a surjective map. Then the quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋 is a topology and it is the finest topology on 𝐴 such that 𝜋 is continuous.
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about universal property of the quotient topology that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Quotient Map Identifying a Subset of the Real Line
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, let 𝐴 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, and let 𝜋∶ ℝ → 𝐴 be the map given by ⎧𝑎 𝑥 = 0, ⎪ 𝜋(𝑥) = ⎨𝑏 𝑥 < 0, ⎪𝑐 𝑥 > 0. ⎩ Then the quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋 is the collection {∅, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐}, 𝐴} of subsets of 𝐴.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 39
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, let 𝐴 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, and let 𝜋∶ ℝ → 𝐴 be the map given by ⎧𝑎 𝑥 = 0, ⎪ 𝜋(𝑥) = ⎨𝑏 𝑥 < 0, ⎪𝑐 𝑥 > 0. ⎩ Then the quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋 is the collection {∅, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐}, 𝐴} of subsets of 𝐴.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates quotient map identifying a subset of the real line in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Open and closed maps
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a continuous map. We say that 𝑓 is an open map if for each subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 that is open in 𝑋 the image 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑌 . Likewise, we say that 𝑓 is a closed map if for each subset 𝑉 of 𝑋 that is closed in 𝑋 the image 𝑓 (𝑉 ) is closed in 𝑌 .

If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is an open (closed) map and 𝐴 is a subset of 𝑋 then it is not true in general that 𝑓 |𝐴 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑓 (𝐴) is an open (closed) map. However, if we also assume that 𝐴 is an open (closed) subset of 𝑋, then 𝑓 |𝐴 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑓 (𝐴) is an open (closed) map. Consider the case where 𝐴 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑓 is an open map. Let 𝑈 be a subset of 𝐴 that is open in 𝐴, i.e., 𝑈 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑉 for some open subset 𝑉 of 𝑋. Then 𝑈 is also open in 𝑋, and hence, 𝑓 |𝐴 (𝑈 ) = 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is an open subset of 𝑌 . Furthermore, since 𝑓 (𝐴) is open in 𝑌 and 𝑓 (𝑈 ) ⊆ 𝑓 (𝐴), 𝑓 (𝑈 ) must be open in 𝑓 (𝐴).
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a continuous map. We say that 𝑓 is an open map if for each subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 that is open in 𝑋 the image 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑌 . Likewise, we say that 𝑓 is a closed map if for each subset 𝑉 of 𝑋 that is closed in 𝑋 the image 𝑓 (𝑉 ) is closed in 𝑌 . If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is an open (closed) map and 𝐴 is a subset of 𝑋 then it is not true in general that 𝑓 |𝐴 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑓 (𝐴) is an open (closed) map. However, if we also assume that 𝐴 is an open (closed) subset of 𝑋, then 𝑓 |𝐴 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑓 (𝐴) is an open (closed) map. Consider the case where 𝐴 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑓 is an open map. Let 𝑈 be a subset of 𝐴 that is open in 𝐴, i.e., 𝑈 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑉 for some open subset 𝑉 of 𝑋. Then 𝑈 is also open in 𝑋, and hence, 𝑓 |𝐴 (𝑈 ) = 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is an open subset of 𝑌 . Furthermore, since 𝑓 (𝐴) is open in 𝑌 and 𝑓 (𝑈 ) ⊆ 𝑓 (𝐴), 𝑓 (𝑈 ) must be open in 𝑓 (𝐴).
nodes.explanation: Defines open and closed maps and fixes terminology used later in closed-sets, continuity, maps.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f\text{ open}\iff U\in\mathcal{T}_X\Rightarrow f(U)\in\mathcal{T}_Y,\qquad f\text{ closed}\iff K\subseteq X\text{ closed}\Rightarrow f(K)\subseteq Y\text{ closed}

nodes.title: Open and Closed Maps Need Not Be Homeomorphisms
nodes.originalText: Any homeomorphism is both open and closed. However, the converse is, in general, not true. Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let ∗ be the space consisting of a single element. Then ℝ →∗ is both an open and a closed map but it is clearly not a homeomorphism.
nodes.formalStatement: Any homeomorphism is both open and closed. However, the converse is, in general, not true. Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let ∗ be the space consisting of a single element. Then ℝ →∗ is both an open and a closed map but it is clearly not a homeomorphism.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates open and closed maps need not be homeomorphisms in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Quotient Maps from Bijective Maps
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a bijective continuous map. Then the following are equivalent:

(1) 𝑓 is a homeomorphism;

(2) 𝑓 is an open map;

(3) 𝑓 is a closed map.

Proof. We will show that (1) is equivalent to (2) and that (2) is equivalent to (3). Since 𝑓 is a bijection, we have ( −1 )−1 𝑓 (𝑈 ) = 𝑓 (𝑈 )

for any subset 𝑈 of 𝑋. In particular, if 𝑈 is an open subset of 𝑋 and if we assume that 𝑓 is a homeo- morphism then 𝑓 (𝑈 ) must be open in 𝑌 as 𝑓 −1 is continuous. Hence, 𝑓 is an open map. Furthermore, if we assume that 𝑓 is open instead of being a homeomorphism then 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑌 for 𝑈 an open subset of 𝑋. Hence, 𝑓 −1 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is continuous. Thus 𝑓 is a homeomorphism. Hence, (1) is equivalent to (2). Assume that 𝑓 is an open map. Since 𝑓 is a bijection, we have

𝑓 (𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 ) = 𝑓 (𝑈 𝑐 ) = 𝑓 (𝑈 )𝑐 = 𝑌 ⧵ 𝑓 (𝑈 ).

Thus 𝑓 is closed: 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 is closed and 𝑌 ⧵ 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is closed. The opposite implication follows by a completely analogous argument. Hence, (2) is equivalent to (3). This completes the proof.

The following lemma establishes a relation between open and closed maps, and quotient maps.

nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a bijective continuous map. Then the following are equivalent: (1) 𝑓 is a homeomorphism;
nodes.explanation: States a main result about quotient maps from bijective maps; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y\text{ bijective continuous}\Longrightarrow \bigl(f\text{ homeomorphism}\iff f\text{ quotient map}\bigr)

nodes.title: Open or Closed Surjections Are Quotient Maps
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective contin- uous map.

(1) If 𝜋 is in addition open then it is a quotient map.

(2) If 𝜋 is in addition closed then it is a quotient map.

Proof. Assume that 𝜋 is in addition open. Let 𝑉 be a subset of 𝑌 . If 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 then 𝜋 −1 (𝑉 ) is open in 𝑋 by assumption of continuity of 𝜋. If 𝜋 −1 (𝑉 ) is open in 𝑋 then since 𝜋 is surjective, we have

𝜋(𝜋 −1 (𝑉 )) = 𝑉

which is open in 𝑌 since we have assumed that 𝜋 is an open map. Hence, (1) holds. Now assume that 𝜋 is also closed in addition to being a surjective continuous map. Let 𝑊 be a subset of 𝑌 . If 𝑊 is closed in 𝑌 then 𝜋 −1 (𝑊 ) is closed in 𝑋 by assumption of continuity of 𝜋. If 𝜋 −1 (𝑊 ) is closed in 𝑋 then since 𝜋 is surjective, we have

𝜋(𝜋 −1 (𝑊 )) = 𝑊

which is closed in 𝑌 since we have assumed that 𝜋 is a closed map. Hence, (2) holds.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. (1) If 𝜋 is in addition open then it is a quotient map.
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about open or closed surjections are quotient maps that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Circle as a Quotient of the Unit Interval
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Consider [0, 1] as a subspace of ℝ and 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is also given the standard topology. Let 𝜋 ∶ [0, 1] → 𝑆 1 be the map given by 𝜋(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)). Then, clearly, 𝜋 is a surjective continuous map. We can show that 𝜋 is also closed (to do this it helps to have defined compactness). Thus by Lemma 5.22 𝜋 is a quotient map. Note that 𝜋 is not open as 𝜋([0, 𝑡)) is not open for 0 < 𝑡 < 1 (where we are using the fact that [0, 𝑡) is open in [0, 1] for 0 < 𝑡 < 1). Let ∼ be the equivalence relation on [0, 1] given by 𝑠 ∼ 𝑡 if and only if 𝜋(𝑠) = 𝜋(𝑡), and let

𝑝 ∶ [0, 1] → [0, 1]∕∼

be the map given by 𝑝(𝑡) = [𝑡]. Then, clearly, 𝑝 is a surjective continuous map. The induced bijective map

𝜑 ∶ [0, 1]∕∼ → 𝑆 1

given by 𝜑([𝑡]) = 𝜋(𝑡) is then a homeomorphism from [0, 1]∕∼ with the quotient topology induced by 𝑝 to 𝑆 1 with the quotient topology induced by 𝜋. See Figure 5.6.

Figure 5.6: Constructing the circle 𝑆 1 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Consider [0, 1] as a subspace of ℝ and 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is also given the standard topology. Let 𝜋 ∶ [0, 1] → 𝑆 1 be the map given by 𝜋(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)). Then, clearly, 𝜋 is a surjective continuous map. We can show that 𝜋 is also closed (to do this it helps to have defined compactness). Thus by Lemma 5.22 𝜋 is a quotient map. Note that 𝜋 is not open as 𝜋([0, 𝑡)) is not open for 0 < 𝑡 < 1 (where we are using the fact that [0, 𝑡) is open in [0, 1] for 0 < 𝑡 < 1). Let ∼ be the equivalence relation on [0, 1] given by 𝑠 ∼ 𝑡 if and only if 𝜋(𝑠) = 𝜋(𝑡), and let 𝑝 ∶ [0, 1] → [0, 1]∕∼
nodes.explanation: Illustrates circle as a quotient of the unit interval in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Quotient Description of a Cylinder
nodes.originalText:
Consider [0, 1] × [0, 1] as a subspace of ℝ × ℝ = ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology, and 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 × ℝ2 = ℝ4 where ℝ4 is also given the standard topology. Let 𝜋 ∶ [0, 1] × [0, 1] → 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 be the map given by

𝜋(𝑠, 𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑠), sin(2𝜋𝑠), cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)).

Clearly, 𝜋 is a surjective continuous function. As in the previous example, we can show that 𝜋 is closed, and so, by Lemma 5.22 it is a quotient map. Let ∼ be the equivalence relation on [0, 1] × [0, 1] given by

(𝑠, 0) ∼ (𝑠, 1) and (0, 𝑡) ∼ (1, 𝑡),

and let 𝑝 ∶ [0, 1] × [0, 1] → ([0, 1] × [0, 1])∕∼ be the map given by 𝑝(𝑠, 𝑡) = [𝑠, 𝑡]. Then, clearly, 𝑝 is a surjective continuous map. The induced bijective map

𝜑 ∶ ([0, 1] × [0, 1])∕∼ → 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1

given by 𝜑([𝑠, 𝑡]) = 𝜋(𝑠, 𝑡) is then a homeomorphism from ([0, 1] × [0, 1])∕∼ with the quotient topology induced by 𝑝 to 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 with the quotient topology induced by 𝜋. The equivalence classes are then the sets

{(0, 0), (0, 1), (1, 0), (1, 1)}

(the four vertices of the square are identified),

{(𝑠, 0), (𝑠, 1)} and {(0, 𝑡), (1, 𝑡)}

(opposing edges of the square are identified), and

{(𝑠, 𝑡)}

for 𝑠, 𝑡 ∈ (0, 1). See Figure 5.5.

42 5.3. Quotient spaces
nodes.formalStatement: Consider [0, 1] × [0, 1] as a subspace of ℝ × ℝ = ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology, and 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 × ℝ2 = ℝ4 where ℝ4 is also given the standard topology. Let 𝜋 ∶ [0, 1] × [0, 1] → 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 be the map given by 𝜋(𝑠, 𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑠), sin(2𝜋𝑠), cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)).
nodes.explanation: Illustrates quotient description of a cylinder in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Real Projective Space as a Quotient Space
nodes.originalText:
The real projective space is the quotient space

ℝP𝑛 = 𝑆 𝑛 ∕∼

where 𝑆 𝑛 is the 𝑛-sphere and ∼ is the equivalence relation given by 𝑥 ∼ 𝑦 if and only if 𝑥 = ±𝑦, i.e., [𝑥] = {𝑥, −𝑥}. We say that 𝑥 and −𝑥 are antipodal points. The topology on ℝP𝑛 is defined by the quotient map 𝜋 ∶ 𝑆 𝑛 → ℝP𝑛 given by 𝜋(𝑥) = [𝑥].

We end this section with an alternative description of the quotient topology.
nodes.formalStatement: The real projective space is the quotient space ℝP𝑛 = 𝑆 𝑛 ∕∼
nodes.explanation: Illustrates real projective space as a quotient space in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Quotient Topology via Saturated Open Sets
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, let 𝐴 be a set, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a surjective map. The quotient topology is the only topology on 𝐴 with the following universal property: for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑓 ◦𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous.

𝑋 𝑓 ◦𝜋 𝜋

𝐴 𝑌 𝑓

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proofs of Theorem 5.6 and Theorem 5.13 and adapt them to our current setting. We first prove that the quotient topology  𝜋 has the universal property that for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑓 ◦𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous. Let 𝐴 be given the quotient topology induced by 𝜋, and assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 is continuous. By definition of the quotient topology 𝜋 −1 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑋 if and only if 𝑈 is open in 𝐴. Hence, 𝜋 is continuous. Thus by Theorem 3.16 𝑓 ◦𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous. Now assume that 𝑓 ◦𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous. Let 𝑉 be an open set in 𝑌 . Since 𝜋 is a quotient map and

(𝑓 ◦𝜋)−1 (𝑉 ) = 𝜋 −1 (𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ))

is open in 𝑋 by assumption of continuity of 𝑓 ◦𝜋, it follows that 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) is open in 𝐴. Thus 𝑓 is continuous. Let  ′ be a topology on 𝐴 with the universal property that for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑓 ◦𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous. We must show that  ′ =  𝜋. Let  be the topology on 𝑋, and let 𝐴 be given the topology  ′ . First let 𝑌 = 𝐴 with the quotient topology induced by 𝑓 ◦𝜋. Then for 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝐴,  ′ ) → (𝐴,  𝜋 ), we have

id ◦𝜋 = 𝜋 ∶ (𝑋,  ) → (𝐴,  𝜋 )

which is continuous. Hence, by the universal property id is continuous.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 43

(𝑋,  ) id ◦𝜋 𝜋

(𝐴,  ′ ) (𝐴,  𝜋 ) id

Thus any 𝑉 ∈  𝜋 must also be an element of  ′ , and so,  𝜋 ⊆  ′ . Secondly let 𝑌 = 𝐴 with  ′ as its topology. Then, clearly, 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝐴,  ′ ) → (𝐴,  ′ ) is continu- ous. Thus by the universal property it follows that

id ◦𝜋 = 𝜋 ∶ (𝑋,  ) → (𝐴,  ′ )

is continuous. (𝑋,  ) id ◦𝜋 𝜋

(𝐴,  ′ ) (𝐴,  ′ ) id

Since the quotient topology induced by 𝜋 is the finest topology on 𝐴 such that 𝜋 is a continuous map, cf. Lemma 5.17, it follows that  ′ ⊆  𝜋 . Hence,  ′ =  𝜋 .

5.4 Exercises

Exercise 5.1 Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Show that any subspace of the form (𝑎, 𝑏), i.e., an open interval, is homeomorphic to ℝ.

Exercise 5.2 Let 𝑋 be a topological space and let 𝑌 be a subspace of 𝑋. If 𝐴 is a subset of 𝑌 , show that the subspace topology on 𝐴 inherited from 𝑌 is equal to the subspace topology on 𝐴 inherited from 𝑋.

Exercise 5.3Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and consider the set of rational numbers ℚ as a subspace of ℝ. Show that the subset { √ } | √ 𝐴= 𝑥∈ℚ|− 5<𝑥< 5 | of ℚ is both open and closed in ℚ.

Exercise 5.4 Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑} be given the topology 𝑋 = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑐, 𝑑}, {𝑐, 𝑑}, 𝑋}, and let 𝑌 = {1, 2, 3} be given the topology 𝑌 = {∅, {1}, {1, 3}, 𝑌 }. Find a basis for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .

Exercise 5.5 Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be subsets of 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively. Show that the topology on 𝐴 × 𝐵 as a subspace of the product 𝑋 × 𝑌 is equal to the product topology on 𝐴 × 𝐵 where 𝐴 and 𝐵 are given the subspace topology inherited from 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively.

44 5.4. Exercises

Exercise 5.6 Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Show that the product topology is the coarsest topology on 𝑋 ×𝑌 for which both of the projection maps pr 1 ∶ 𝑋 ×𝑌 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ∶ 𝑋 ×𝑌 → 𝑌 are continuous.

Exercise 5.7 Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑋 × 𝑌 be given the product topology. Show that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a continuous map, the subspace

𝐺 = {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 ∣ 𝑦 = 𝑓 (𝑥)}

of 𝑋 × 𝑌 , is homeomorphic to 𝑋.

Exercise 5.8 Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Let

𝜋∶ ℝ → ℤ

be the map given by { 𝑥 𝑥∈ℤ 𝜋(𝑥) = 𝑛 𝑥 ∈ (𝑛 − 1, 𝑛 + 1), and 𝑛 is an odd integer.

Show that the quotient topology on ℤ induced by 𝜋 is equal to the digital line topology, cf. Exercise 4.6.

6. Topological properties

6.1 Connected spaces One of the fundamental results of calculus is the intermediate value theorem. The theorem says that for a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → ℝ and for a real number 𝑟 between 𝑓 (𝑎) and 𝑓 (𝑏) there is a real number 𝑐 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟. See Figure 6.1. From this result we can deduce that the graph of a continuous map (in this setting) is connected.

𝑦

𝑓 (𝑏)

𝑦 = 𝑓 (𝑥)

𝑟

𝑓 (𝑎) 𝑥 𝑎 𝑐 𝑏

Figure 6.1: The intermediate value theorem.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a topological space, let $A$ be a set, and let $\pi:X\to A$ be surjective. The quotient topology on $A$ is characterized by the universal property that, for every topological space $Y$ and every map $f:A\to Y$, the map $f$ is continuous if and only if $f\circ\pi:X\to Y$ is continuous.
nodes.explanation: This universal property is the main practical test for maps out of a quotient space: continuity can be checked after composing with the quotient map.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:A\to Y\text{ continuous}\iff f\circ\pi:X\to Y\text{ continuous}

nodes.title: Connected space
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. A separation of 𝑋 is a pair of non-empty subsets 𝑈 and 𝑉 that are open in 𝑋, disjoint and whose union equal 𝑋. We say that 𝑋 is connected if there are no separations of 𝑋. Otherwise it is disconnected.

The property of being connected is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of open subsets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is connected then so is 𝑌 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. A separation of 𝑋 is a pair of non-empty subsets 𝑈 and 𝑉 that are open in 𝑋, disjoint and whose union equal 𝑋. We say that 𝑋 is connected if there are no separations of 𝑋. Otherwise it is disconnected. The property of being connected is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of open subsets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is connected then so is 𝑌 .
nodes.explanation: Defines connected space and fixes terminology used later in connectedness, open-sets, sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ connected}\iff \nexists\ U,V\in\mathcal{T}\text{ with }U,V\ne\emptyset,\ U\cap V=\emptyset,\ U\cup V=X

nodes.title: Connectedness in a Finite Topological Space
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}. If we equip 𝑋 with the topology 1 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} then it is disconnected; the pair 𝑈 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐} and 𝑉 = {𝑑, 𝑒} is a separation of 𝑋 in this topology. However, if we equip 𝑋 with the topology 2 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐}, {𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} then it is connected; there are no separations of 𝑋 in this topology.

46 6.1. Connected spaces
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}. If we equip 𝑋 with the topology 1 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} then it is disconnected; the pair 𝑈 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐} and 𝑉 = {𝑑, 𝑒} is a separation of 𝑋 in this topology. However, if we equip 𝑋 with the topology 2 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐}, {𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} then it is connected; there are no separations of 𝑋 in this topology.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates connectedness in a finite topological space in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Indiscrete Spaces Are Connected
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete space. Then 𝑋 is connected as there are no separations of 𝑋, i.e., there are no non-empty open subsets of 𝑋 who are disjoint and whose union equal 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete space. Then 𝑋 is connected as there are no separations of 𝑋, i.e., there are no non-empty open subsets of 𝑋 who are disjoint and whose union equal 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates indiscrete spaces are connected in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Discrete Spaces with Multiple Points Are Disconnected
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a discrete space containing two or more points. Then 𝑋 is disconnected. Let 𝑝 ∈ 𝑋 and let 𝑈 = {𝑝} and 𝑉 = 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ {𝑝}. Then the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a discrete space containing two or more points. Then 𝑋 is disconnected. Let 𝑝 ∈ 𝑋 and let 𝑈 = {𝑝} and 𝑉 = 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ {𝑝}. Then the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates discrete spaces with multiple points are disconnected in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: The Real Line Is Connected
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let 𝑋 = [0, 1) ∪ (1, 2) be a subspace of ℝ. Since 𝑈 = [0, 1) is open in 𝑋 (but not in ℝ) and 𝑉 = (1, 2) is open 𝑋, 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋, they form a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is disconnected.

In the above examples the spaces that are connected all share the property that the only subsets that are both open and closed in 𝑋 are ∅ and 𝑋. Likewise, the disconnected spaces all share the property that there are non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let 𝑋 = [0, 1) ∪ (1, 2) be a subspace of ℝ. Since 𝑈 = [0, 1) is open in 𝑋 (but not in ℝ) and 𝑉 = (1, 2) is open 𝑋, 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋, they form a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is disconnected. In the above examples the spaces that are connected all share the property that the only subsets that are both open and closed in 𝑋 are ∅ and 𝑋. Likewise, the disconnected spaces all share the property that there are non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates the real line is connected in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Connectedness via Clopen Subsets
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if the are no non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that 𝑋 is disconnected if and only if there are non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋. Assume 𝑋 is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of 𝑋. Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑈 is open in 𝑋. Since 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋, we have 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 = 𝑉 . Thus 𝑈 𝑐 is open in 𝑋, and so, 𝑈 is closed in 𝑋. Hence, 𝑈 is both open and closed in 𝑋. Likewise, 𝑉 is both open and closed in 𝑋. Assume that the non-empty proper subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 is both open and closed in 𝑋. Let 𝑉 = 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 . Then 𝑉 is open in 𝑋, 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋. Hence, the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is disconnected.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if the are no non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about connectedness via clopen subsets; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ connected}\iff \nexists A\subsetneq X:\ A\neq\emptyset\text{ and }A\text{ is clopen}

nodes.title: Continuous Images of Connected Spaces Are Connected
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a connected space, 𝑌 be a topological space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is connected.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a surjective continuous map and 𝑌 is disconnected then 𝑋 is disconnected. Assume that 𝑌 is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of 𝑌 . Let the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑌 . Then 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) are non-empty subsets of 𝑋 which are open in 𝑋 as 𝑓 is a surjective continuous map. Furthermore,

𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∩ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 ) = ∅ and 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∪ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 ) = 𝑋.

Hence, the pair 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) is a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is disconnected.

A subset 𝐴 of a topological space 𝑍 is connected if 𝐴 is connected in the subspace topology. Thus the theorem can be extended to saying that the continuous image of a connected space is connected, i.e., assuming 𝑋 is a connected space and 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a continuous map then 𝑓 (𝑋) is connected in 𝑌 .

Chapter 6. Topological properties 47

We may also describe connectedness by way of the following theorem.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a connected space, 𝑌 be a topological space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is connected.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about continuous images of connected spaces are connected; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ connected},\ f:X\twoheadrightarrow Y\text{ continuous}\Longrightarrow Y\text{ connected}

nodes.title: Connectedness via Continuous Maps to a Two-Point Discrete Space
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if every contin- uous map form 𝑋 to a discrete space, with at least two points, is constant.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that 𝑋 is disconnected if and only if there is a non-constant continuous map from 𝑋 to a discrete space (with at least two points). Assume that 𝑋 is disconnected. Let 𝑈 be a non-empty proper subset of 𝑋 that is both open and closed in 𝑋. Let 𝑌 = {𝑎, 𝑏} be given the discrete topology. The map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 that sends 𝑈 to 𝑎 and 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 to 𝑏 is continuous and not constant. Let 𝑌 be a discrete space with at least two points. Assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a non-constant continuous map. Then for each 𝑦 ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋), i.e., 𝑦 = 𝑓 (𝑥) for some 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, 𝑓 −1 ({𝑦}) is a non-empty proper subset of 𝑋 that is both open and closed in 𝑋. Thus by Theorem 6.6, 𝑋 is disconnected.

We will prove that the (finite) product of connected spaces is again connected. To prove this we will need the following two results.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if every continuous map form 𝑋 to a discrete space, with at least two points, is constant.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about connectedness via continuous maps to a two-point discrete space; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ connected}\iff \text{every continuous }f:X\to D\text{ to a discrete space }|D|\ge2\text{ is constant}

nodes.title: Separation Elements Give Distinct Images under a Map to a Two-Point Space
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a disconnected space with separation 𝑈 and 𝑉 , and let 𝐴 be a connected subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑈 or 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑉 .

Proof. Since 𝑈 and 𝑉 are open in 𝑋, the intersections 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 and 𝐴 ∩ 𝑉 are both open in 𝐴 (in the subspace topology). Furthermore, the complement of 𝐴∩𝑈 in 𝐴 is equal to 𝐴∩𝑉 as 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵𝑈 = 𝑉 . Hence, 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 is also closed in 𝐴. Thus by Theorem 6.6, 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 is either empty or all of 𝐴 as 𝐴 is connected. If 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 = ∅ then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑉 . If 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 = 𝐴 then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑈 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a disconnected space with separation 𝑈 and 𝑉 , and let 𝐴 be a connected subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑈 or 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑉 .
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about separation elements give distinct images under a map to a two-point space that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Unions of Connected Subspaces with Common Point Are Connected
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let {𝐴𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of connected ⋂ ⋃ subspaces of 𝑋 such that 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is non-empty. Then 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is connected.

⋃ Proof. Let 𝑌 = 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 . Suppose that 𝑌 is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of 𝑌 . Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑌 . We will show that this leads to a contradiction, and so, 𝑌 must be connected. ⋂ Let 𝑝 ∈ 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 . Then either 𝑝 ∈ 𝑈 or 𝑝 ∈ 𝑉 . Assume without loss of generality that 𝑝 ∈ 𝑈 . By Lemma 6.9 it follows that for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ either 𝐴𝜆 ⊆ 𝑈 or 𝐴𝜆 ⊆ 𝑉 . Since we have assumed that 𝑝 ∈ 𝑈 we must have 𝐴𝜆 ⊆ 𝑈 for all 𝜆 ∈ Λ. Thus 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 . But this implies that 𝑉 is empty, and hence, contradicts that 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of 𝑌 . Thus 𝑌 is connected.

We can prove that the (finite) product of connected spaces is again connected.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let {𝐴𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of connected ⋂ ⋃ subspaces of 𝑋 such that 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is non-empty. Then 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is connected. ⋃
nodes.explanation: States a main result about unions of connected subspaces with common point are connected; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \{A_\lambda\}_{\lambda\in\Lambda}\text{ connected},\ \bigcap_{\lambda\in\Lambda}A_\lambda\neq\emptyset\Longrightarrow \bigcup_{\lambda\in\Lambda}A_\lambda\text{ connected}

nodes.title: Finite Products of Connected Spaces Are Connected
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be connected spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is connected.

Proof. We prove the statement for the product of two connected spaces. The general result then follows by an induction argument. Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two connected spaces. We must prove that 𝑋 × 𝑌 is connected. Since for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the subspace {𝑥} × 𝑌 of 𝑋 × 𝑌 is homeomorphic to 𝑌 , it follows that {𝑥} × 𝑌 is connected. Similarly, for each 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 the subspace 𝑋 × {𝑦} is homeomorphic to 𝑋, and hence, 𝑋 × {𝑦} is

48 6.1. Connected spaces

connected. Thus by Theorem 6.10 it follows that for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and each 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 the subspace ({𝑥} × 𝑌 ) ∪ (𝑋 × {𝑦}) is connected as it is the union of two connected spaces whose intersection is ({𝑥} × 𝑌 ) ∩ (𝑋 × {𝑦}) = {(𝑥, 𝑦)} ≠ ∅. Fix 𝑥0 ∈ 𝑋 and let 𝐴𝑦 = ({𝑥0 } × 𝑌 ) ∪ (𝑋 × {𝑦}). Then for each 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 the subspace 𝐴𝑦 of 𝑋 × 𝑌 is connected as it is the union of two connected spaces whose intersection is equal to {(𝑥0 , 𝑦)}. Hence, ⋃ by Theorem 6.10 it follows that 𝑦∈𝑌 𝐴𝑦 is connected as it is the union of connected spaces whose intersection in non-empty. Since ⋃ 𝐴𝑦 = 𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑦∈𝑌

it follows that 𝑋 × 𝑌 is connected.

∏ The theorem can be extended to hold for arbitrary products, 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 , if we equip the product with the ∏ product topology. If we equip 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 with the box topology the statement is no longer true.

An important example of a connected space is the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be connected spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is connected.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about finite products of connected spaces are connected; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X_1,\dots,X_n\text{ connected}\Longrightarrow X_1\times\cdots\times X_n\text{ connected}

nodes.title: The real numbers are connected
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then ℝ is connected.

We will use the fact that the real numbers satisfy the following two properties.

(1) Every subset of ℝ that is bounded above has a least upper bound. This is known as the least upper bound property.

(2) If 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ ℝ with 𝑥 < 𝑦 then there is a real number 𝑧 such that 𝑥 < 𝑧 < 𝑦.

Proof. Assume that ℝ is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of ℝ. Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of ℝ, and choose 𝑎 ∈ 𝑈 and 𝑏 ∈ 𝑉 . We may assume without loss of generality that 𝑎 < 𝑏. Let 𝐴 = [𝑎, 𝑏] ∩ 𝑈 and 𝐵 = [𝑎, 𝑏] ∩ 𝑉 . Then the pair 𝐴 and 𝐵 is a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏], with 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵. Also note that 𝐴 is bounded above by 𝑏. Hence, by the least upper bound property 𝐴 has a least upper bound; 𝑐 = sup 𝐴. Thus 𝑎 ⩽ 𝑐 ⩽ 𝑏. We will show that 𝑐 belongs neither to 𝐴 nor to 𝐵 thus contradicting the fact that 𝐴 and 𝐵 is a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏]. Assume that 𝑐 ∈ 𝐵. Since 𝑎 ∉ 𝐵 and 𝐵 is open in [𝑎, 𝑏], it follows that there is a real number 𝑑 such that 𝑎 < 𝑑 < 𝑐 and (𝑑, 𝑐] ⊆ 𝐵. This implies that 𝑑 is an upper bound of 𝐴 and that 𝑑 is less than the least upper bound 𝑐. That is a contradiction, and so, 𝑐 ∉ 𝐵. Now assume that 𝑐 ∈ 𝐴. Since 𝐴 is open in [𝑎, 𝑏] and 𝑏 ∉ 𝐴, there is a real number 𝑑 such that [𝑐, 𝑑) ⊆ 𝐴. For any 𝑒 ∈ (𝑐, 𝑑) it follows that 𝑒 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑒 > 𝑐. That is a contradiction to the fact that 𝑐 is an upper bound of 𝐴. Thus 𝑐 ∉ 𝐴. Hence, 𝑐 ∉ 𝐴 and 𝑐 ∉ 𝐵. This is a contradiction to the fact that 𝑐 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] and that 𝐴 and 𝐵 is a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏]. Thus ℝ must be connected.

As an immediate consequence of Theorem 6.12, we get that open intervals of the form (𝑎, 𝑏), (−∞, 𝑏) and (𝑎, ∞) are all connected as they are all homeomorphic to ℝ (with the standard topology). We can also show that every closed interval [𝑎, 𝑏] is connected. Furthermore, by Theorem 6.11 and Theorem 6.12 ℝ𝑛 is a connected space.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 49
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then ℝ is connected. We will use the fact that the real numbers satisfy the following two properties.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about the real numbers are connected; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathbb{R}\text{ with the standard topology is connected}

nodes.title: Generalized intermediate value theorem
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a connected space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. If 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ 𝑋 and if 𝑟 is a real number that lies between 𝑓 (𝑎) and 𝑓 (𝑏), there is a 𝑐 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟.

Proof. Assume that 𝑟 ∉ 𝑓 (𝑋). We will show that this contradicts the assumption that 𝑋 is connected. By the assumption that 𝑟 ∉ 𝑓 (𝑋), i.e., 𝑓 (𝑋) ⊆ ℝ ⧵ {𝑟} = (−∞, 𝑟) ∪ (𝑟, ∞), we have a separation of 𝑋: 𝑈 = 𝑓 −1 ((−∞, 𝑟)) and 𝑉 = 𝑓 −1 ((𝑟, ∞))

are disjoint non-empty open subsets of 𝑋 whose union equals 𝑋. Thus they are a separation of 𝑋. This contradicts the assumption that 𝑋 is connected. Hence, 𝑟 ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋). In other words, there is a 𝑐 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟.

We end this section with a discussion of path-connectivity.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a connected space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. If 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ 𝑋 and if 𝑟 is a real number that lies between 𝑓 (𝑎) and 𝑓 (𝑏), there is a 𝑐 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about generalized intermediate value theorem; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ connected},\ f:X\to\mathbb{R}\text{ continuous},\ r\in [f(a),f(b)]\Longrightarrow \exists c\in X:\ f(c)=r

nodes.title: Path-connected space
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋. A path from 𝑥 to 𝑦 is a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑥 and 𝑓 (𝑏) = 𝑦 where [𝑎, 𝑏] is a subspace of ℝ with the standard topology. We say that 𝑋 is path-connected if every pair of points of 𝑋 can be joined by a path in 𝑋.

The property of being path-connected is a topological property as it is completely described using the el- ements of 𝑋 and its open subsets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is path connected then so is 𝑌 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋. A path from 𝑥 to 𝑦 is a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑥 and 𝑓 (𝑏) = 𝑦 where [𝑎, 𝑏] is a subspace of ℝ with the standard topology. We say that 𝑋 is path-connected if every pair of points of 𝑋 can be joined by a path in 𝑋. The property of being path-connected is a topological property as it is completely described using the elements of 𝑋 and its open subsets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is path connected then so is 𝑌 .
nodes.explanation: Defines path-connected space and fixes terminology used later in connectedness, continuity, maps.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ path-connected}\iff \forall x,y\in X\ \exists f:[0,1]\to X\text{ continuous with }f(0)=x\text{ and }f(1)=y

nodes.title: Path Connectedness of the Real Line
nodes.originalText: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then ℝ is path-connected as for any two points, 𝑝, 𝑞 ∈ ℝ, there is a path from 𝑝 to 𝑞 in ℝ, e.g., the path given by 𝑓 (𝑡) = (1 − 𝑡)𝑝 + 𝑡𝑞 where 𝑡 ∈ [0, 1].
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then ℝ is path-connected as for any two points, 𝑝, 𝑞 ∈ ℝ, there is a path from 𝑝 to 𝑞 in ℝ, e.g., the path given by 𝑓 (𝑡) = (1 − 𝑡)𝑝 + 𝑡𝑞 where 𝑡 ∈ [0, 1].
nodes.explanation: Illustrates path connectedness of the real line in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Euclidean Spaces Are Path Connected
nodes.originalText:
For all 𝑛 ⩾ 2, ℝ𝑛 with the standard topology is path-connected, and so is ℝ𝑛 ⧵ {𝑝} for each 𝑝 ∈ ℝ𝑛 . For 𝑛 = 1, ℝ ⧵ {𝑝} is neither connected nor path-connected.

The next theorem states that path-connectedness implies connectedness. While the converse is not true in general, it is true that open subsets of ℝ𝑛 that are connected (where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology) are also path-connected. See [7, Proposition 12.25] for a proof of this fact.
nodes.formalStatement: For all 𝑛 ⩾ 2, ℝ𝑛 with the standard topology is path-connected, and so is ℝ𝑛 ⧵ {𝑝} for each 𝑝 ∈ ℝ𝑛 . For 𝑛 = 1, ℝ ⧵ {𝑝} is neither connected nor path-connected. The next theorem states that path-connectedness implies connectedness. While the converse is not true in general, it is true that open subsets of ℝ𝑛 that are connected (where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology) are also path-connected. See [7, Proposition 12.25] for a proof of this fact.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates euclidean spaces are path connected in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Path-connectedness implies connectedness
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a path-connected space. Then 𝑋 is connected.

Proof. Assume that 𝑋 is path-connected but that 𝑋 is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of 𝑋. Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑋. Let 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 to 𝑦 ∈ 𝑉 . Then 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) are disjoint non-empty open subsets of [𝑎, 𝑏] whose union is equal to [𝑎, 𝑏]. Thus they are a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏]. This is a contradiction to the fact that [𝑎, 𝑏] is a connected space.

50 6.2. Hausdorff spaces
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a path-connected space. Then 𝑋 is connected.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about path-connectedness implies connectedness; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ path-connected}\Longrightarrow X\text{ connected}

nodes.originalText:

6.2 Hausdorff spaces A common feature that we typically want a topological space to have is the ability to separate the individual points. This is commonly referred to as separation axioms. We will in this section focus on the most common separation axiom.

nodes.title: Hausdorff Spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. We say that 𝑋 is Hausdorff if for each pair of points 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋, with 𝑥 ≠ 𝑦, there are disjoint neighborhoods 𝑈 and 𝑉 of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively. In other words, for each pair of distinct points 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋 there are open subsets 𝑈 and 𝑉 of 𝑋 with 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 and 𝑦 ∈ 𝑉 where 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅.

The property to be a Hausdorff space is completely described using the elements of 𝑋 and its open subsets, and so, it is a topological property. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is Hausdorff then so is 𝑌 . To see this let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space and let 𝑦1 and 𝑦2 be distinct points in 𝑌 . If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a homeomorphism then 𝑓 −1 (𝑦1 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑦2 ) are distinct points in 𝑋. Since 𝑋 is Hausdorff this means that there are disjoint neighborhoods 𝑈1 and 𝑈2 of 𝑓 −1 (𝑦1 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑦2 ), respectively. Thus 𝑓 (𝑈1 ) and 𝑓 (𝑈2 ) are disjoint neighborhoods of 𝑦1 and 𝑦2 , respectively. Hence, 𝑌 is Hausdorff.
nodes.formalStatement: A topological space $X$ is Hausdorff if for every pair of distinct points $x,y\in X$ there exist disjoint neighborhoods $U$ of $x$ and $V$ of $y$.
nodes.explanation: Hausdorff spaces are spaces in which distinct points can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ Hausdorff}\iff \forall x\ne y\in X\ \exists U,V\in\mathcal{T}:x\in U,\ y\in V,\ U\cap V=\emptyset

nodes.title: Finite Discrete Spaces Are Hausdorff
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}. If we equip 𝑋 with the discrete topology then 𝑋 is Hausdorff since for all pairs of distinct points 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋, the open subsets {𝑥} and {𝑦} are neighborhoods of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively, and {𝑥} ∩ {𝑦} = ∅. However, if we equip 𝑋 with the topology {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏}, 𝑋} it is not Hausdorff; the only neighborhood of 𝑐 is 𝑈 = 𝑋, and no neighborhood 𝑉 of either 𝑎 or 𝑏 can be disjoint from 𝑋.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 51
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}. If we equip 𝑋 with the discrete topology then 𝑋 is Hausdorff since for all pairs of distinct points 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋, the open subsets {𝑥} and {𝑦} are neighborhoods of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively, and {𝑥} ∩ {𝑦} = ∅. However, if we equip 𝑋 with the topology {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏}, 𝑋} it is not Hausdorff; the only neighborhood of 𝑐 is 𝑈 = 𝑋, and no neighborhood 𝑉 of either 𝑎 or 𝑏 can be disjoint from 𝑋.
