nodes.title: Hausdorffness and Finer Topologies
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 1 and 2 be two topologies on 𝑋. If 2 is finer than 1 and 𝑋 equipped with 1 as its topology is Hausdorff then 𝑋 equipped with 2 as its topology is also Hausdorff.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 1 and 2 be two topologies on 𝑋. If 2 is finer than 1 and 𝑋 equipped with 1 as its topology is Hausdorff then 𝑋 equipped with 2 as its topology is also Hausdorff.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates hausdorffness and finer topologies in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Metric Spaces Are Hausdorff
nodes.originalText:
Every metric space is Hausdorff.

Proof. Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space, and let 𝑥 and 𝑦 be two distinct points in 𝑋. We must show that there are disjoint neighborhoods 𝑈 and 𝑉 of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively. Let 𝛿 = 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦). Then 𝛿 > 0. Let 𝑈 = 𝖡(𝑥; 𝛿∕2) and 𝑉 = 𝖡(𝑦; 𝛿∕2) be neighborhoods of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively. Then by M3, 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅.
nodes.formalStatement: Every metric space is Hausdorff.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about metric spaces are hausdorff; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: (X,d)\text{ metric}\Longrightarrow X\text{ Hausdorff}

nodes.title: Euclidean Spaces Are Hausdorff
nodes.originalText:
For all integers 𝑛 ⩾ 1, the metric space (ℝ𝑛 , 𝑑) is Hausdorff. In particular, ℝ𝑛 with the standard topology is Hausdorff.

All Hausdorff spaces share the property that finite subsets are closed which is an immediate con- sequence of the following theorem.
nodes.formalStatement: For all integers 𝑛 ⩾ 1, the metric space (ℝ𝑛 , 𝑑) is Hausdorff. In particular, ℝ𝑛 with the standard topology is Hausdorff. All Hausdorff spaces share the property that finite subsets are closed which is an immediate consequence of
nodes.explanation: Illustrates euclidean spaces are hausdorff in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Singletons Are Closed in Hausdorff Spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space. Then for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the subset {𝑥} of 𝑋 is closed in 𝑋.

Proof. Let 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋 with 𝑥 ≠ 𝑦. Since 𝑋 is Hausdorff, we have neighborhoods 𝑈 and 𝑉 of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively, such that 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅. Then 𝑥 ∉ 𝑉 . In other words, 𝑥 ∈ 𝑉 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑉 . Since 𝑉 is open, 𝑉 𝑐 is closed in 𝑋. Thus {𝑥} ⊆ 𝑉 𝑐 , and hence, 𝑦 ∉ {𝑥}. Hence, {𝑥} = {𝑥}. Thus {𝑥} is closed in 𝑋.

There are examples of topological spaces who are not Hausdorff but have the property that finite subsets are closed. One such example is the set of real numbers equipped with the cofinite topology. We have seen that the (finite) product of connected spaces is connected, cf. Theorem 6.11. The same statement holds for Hausdorff spaces, i.e., the (finite) product of Hausdorff spaces is Hausdorff.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space. Then for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the subset {𝑥} of 𝑋 is closed in 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about singletons are closed in hausdorff spaces; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ Hausdorff},\ x\in X\Longrightarrow \{x\}\text{ is closed in }X

nodes.title: Finite Products of Hausdorff Spaces Are Hausdorff
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be Hausdorff spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is Hausdorff.

Proof. We prove the statement for two Hausdorff spaces. The general result then follows from an induction argument. Let (𝑥1 , 𝑦1 ) and (𝑥2 , 𝑦2 ) be two distinct points in 𝑋 × 𝑌 , i.e., 𝑥1 ≠ 𝑥2 or 𝑦1 ≠ 𝑦2 . If 𝑥1 ≠ 𝑥2 there must be neighborhoods 𝑈𝑋 and 𝑉𝑋 in 𝑋 of 𝑥1 and 𝑥2 , respectively, such that 𝑈𝑋 ∩ 𝑉𝑋 = ∅ as 𝑋 is assumed to be Hausdorff. Then 𝑈𝑋 × 𝑌 and 𝑉𝑋 × 𝑌 are neighborhoods of (𝑥1 , 𝑦1 ) and (𝑥2 , 𝑦2 ), respectively, where (𝑈𝑋 ×𝑌 )∩(𝑉𝑋 ×𝑌 ) = ∅. Similarly, if 𝑦1 ≠ 𝑦2 there must be neighborhoods 𝑈𝑌 and 𝑉𝑌 in 𝑌 of 𝑦1 and 𝑦2 , respectively, such that 𝑈𝑌 ∩𝑉𝑌 = ∅ as 𝑌 is assumed to be Hausdorff. Then 𝑋 ×𝑈𝑌 and 𝑋 × 𝑉𝑌 are neighborhoods of (𝑥1 , 𝑦1 ) and (𝑥2 , 𝑦2 ), respectively, where (𝑋 × 𝑈𝑌 ) ∩ (𝑋 × 𝑉𝑌 ) = ∅. Hence, 𝑋 × 𝑌 is Hausdorff.

∏ The theorem be extended to hold for arbitrary products, 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 , if we equip the product with either the product topology or the box topology.

52 6.3. Compact spaces

We end this section with a result that helps us decide whether or not a topological space is Haus- dorff.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be Hausdorff spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is Hausdorff.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about finite products of hausdorff spaces are hausdorff; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X_1,\dots,X_n\text{ Hausdorff}\Longrightarrow X_1\times\cdots\times X_n\text{ Hausdorff}

nodes.title: Hausdorff Spaces via the Diagonal
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is Hausdorff if and only if the diagonal Δ = {(𝑥, 𝑥) ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋} is closed in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑋.

Proof. Assume that 𝑋 is a Hausdorff space. Then for any two distinct points 𝑥 and 𝑦 in 𝑋, i.e., (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ Δ𝑐 = (𝑋 × 𝑋) ⧵ Δ, there are disjoint neighborhoods 𝑈 and 𝑉 of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively. Thus 𝑈 × 𝑉 is open in 𝑋 × 𝑋 and (𝑈 × 𝑉 ) ∩ Δ = ∅, i.e., 𝑈 × 𝑉 ⊆ Δ𝑐 . Hence, by Theorem 3.10, it follows that Δ𝑐 is open in 𝑋 × 𝑋, and so, Δ is closed in 𝑋 × 𝑋. Now assume that Δ is closed in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑋. Then for any point (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑋 with 𝑥 ≠ 𝑦, i.e., (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ Δ𝑐 , there is a basis element 𝑈 × 𝑉 for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑋 such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑈 × 𝑉 ⊆ Δ𝑐 . Since 𝑈 × 𝑉 ⊆ Δ𝑐 , we have 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅. Thus 𝑈 and 𝑉 are neighborhoods of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively, such that 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅. Hence, 𝑋 is Hausdorff.

6.3 Compact spaces In Section 6.1 we saw how the intermediate value theorem might be generalized to connected spaces. In this section we will see how the extreme value theorem may be generalized to compact spaces. The extreme value theorem says that for a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → ℝ there are points 𝑚, 𝑀 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] such that 𝑓 (𝑚) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑥) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑀) for all 𝑥 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏]. See Figure 6.3.

𝑦

𝑓 (𝑀)

𝑦 = 𝑓 (𝑥)

𝑓 (𝑚) 𝑥 𝑎 𝑀 𝑚 𝑏

Figure 6.3: The extreme value theorem.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is Hausdorff if and only if the diagonal Δ = {(𝑥, 𝑥) ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋} is closed in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about hausdorff spaces via the diagonal; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ Hausdorff}\iff \Delta_X=\{(x,x)\mid x\in X\}\text{ is closed in }X\times X

nodes.title: Cover of a space
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a collection ⋃ of subsets of 𝑋. We say that  is a cover of 𝑋, or covering of 𝑋 if 𝑋 = 𝐴∈ 𝐴. If 𝐴 is also open in 𝑋 for each 𝐴 ∈ , we say that  is an open cover of 𝑋, or open covering of 𝑋. We say that ′ is a subcover of  if ′ is another cover of 𝑋 that satisfies ′ ⊆ .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a collection ⋃ of subsets of 𝑋. We say that  is a cover of 𝑋, or covering of 𝑋 if 𝑋 = 𝐴∈ 𝐴. If 𝐴 is also open in 𝑋 for each 𝐴 ∈ , we say that  is an open cover of 𝑋, or open covering of 𝑋. We say that ′ is a subcover of  if ′ is another cover of 𝑋 that satisfies ′ ⊆ .
nodes.explanation: Defines cover of a space and fixes terminology used later in covering, open-sets, sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{A}\text{ covers }X\iff X=\bigcup_{A\in\mathcal{A}}A,\qquad \mathcal{A}'\text{ is a subcover}\iff \mathcal{A}'\subseteq\mathcal{A}\text{ and }X=\bigcup_{A\in\mathcal{A}'}A

nodes.title: Hausdorffness Tested on Basis Elements
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. Then  is an open cover of 𝑋. Similarly, if  is a subbasis for the topology on 𝑋, then  is an open cover of 𝑋.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 53
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. Then  is an open cover of 𝑋. Similarly, if  is a subbasis for the topology on 𝑋, then  is an open cover of 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates hausdorffness tested on basis elements in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Compact spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. We say that 𝑋 is compact if every open cover  of 𝑋 contains a finite subcover.

The property of being compact is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of the collection of open sets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is compact then so is 𝑌 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. We say that 𝑋 is compact if every open cover  of 𝑋 contains a finite subcover. The property of being compact is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of the collection of open sets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is compact then so is 𝑌 .
nodes.explanation: Defines compact spaces and fixes terminology used later in compactness, covering, homeomorphism.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ compact}\iff \forall\text{ open covers }\mathcal{A}\text{ of }X,\ \exists A_1,\ldotsldots,A_n\in\mathcal{A}\text{ such that }X=\bigcup_{k=1}^n A_k

nodes.title: Finite Spaces Are Compact
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a finite topological space. Then 𝑋 is compact as there are only finitely many different open subsets 𝐴 of 𝑋, and so, any collection covering 𝑋 must necessarily be finite.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a finite topological space. Then 𝑋 is compact as there are only finitely many different open subsets 𝐴 of 𝑋, and so, any collection covering 𝑋 must necessarily be finite.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates finite spaces are compact in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Indiscrete Spaces Are Compact
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete space. Then 𝑋 is compact as the only open covers are the collections {𝑋} and {∅, 𝑋} which are finite.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete space. Then 𝑋 is compact as the only open covers are the collections {𝑋} and {∅, 𝑋} which are finite.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates indiscrete spaces are compact in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Noncompactness of the Real Line
nodes.originalText: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Since the open cover  = {(𝑛 − 1, 𝑛 + 1) ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ}, does not admit a finite subcover, ℝ is not compact.
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Since the open cover  = {(𝑛 − 1, 𝑛 + 1) ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ}, does not admit a finite subcover, ℝ is not compact.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates noncompactness of the real line in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Compact subspaces
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is compact if 𝐴 is compact in the subspace topology.

If 𝐴 is a subspace of 𝑋, a collection  of subsets of 𝑋 is a cover of 𝐴 if the union of elements of  contains 𝐴.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is compact if 𝐴 is compact in the subspace topology. If 𝐴 is a subspace of 𝑋, a collection  of subsets of 𝑋 is a cover of 𝐴 if the union of elements of  contains 𝐴.
nodes.explanation: Defines compact subspaces and fixes terminology used later in compactness, covering, sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: A\subseteq X\text{ compact}\iff A\text{ is compact with the subspace topology}\iff \forall\text{ open covers }\mathcal{U}\text{ of }A,\ \exists U_1,\ldotsldots,U_n\in\mathcal{U}:A\subseteq\bigcup_{k=1}^n U_k

nodes.title: Compactness of Subspaces via Open Covers
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 is compact if and only if every cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋 contains a finite subcollection that covers 𝐴.

Proof. Assume that 𝐴 is compact. Let  be a cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋. Then the collection

 ′ = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 ∈ }

is an open cover of 𝐴. Since 𝐴 is compact there must be a finite subcover {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈1 , 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈2 , … , 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈𝑛 } of  ′ . Hence, {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 } is a finite subcollection of  that covers 𝐴. Now assume that every cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋 contains a finite subcollection that covers 𝐴. Let  = {𝑉𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝐴. Hence, by definition of the subspace topology, cf. Definition 5.1, we have for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ that 𝑉𝜆 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈𝜆 where 𝑈𝜆 is an open subset of 𝑋. Thus the collection  ′ = {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ is a cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋. Then, by assumption, there must be a finite subcollection {𝑈𝜆1 , 𝑈𝜆2 , … , 𝑈𝜆𝑛 } that covers 𝐴. Hence, {𝑉𝜆1 , 𝑉𝜆2 , … , 𝑉𝜆𝑛 } is a finite subcover of . Thus every cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝐴 has a finite subcover, and so, 𝐴 is compact.

The following two theorems indicates that being compact and being closed are closely related properties.

54 6.3. Compact spaces
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 is compact if and only if every cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋 contains a finite subcollection that covers 𝐴.
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about compactness of subspaces via open covers that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Closed Subspaces of Compact Spaces Are Compact
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝐴 be a closed subset of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 is compact.

⋃ Proof. Let  be a cover of 𝐴, i.e., 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐶∈ 𝐶, by open subsets of 𝑋. Since 𝐴 is closed in 𝑋, 𝐴𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝐴 is open in 𝑋. Thus  =  ∪ {𝐴𝑐 } is an open cover of 𝑋. Since 𝑋 is compact there must be a finite subcover ′ ⊆  of 𝑋. If ′ contains 𝐴𝑐 , let ′′ = ′ ⧵ {𝐴𝑐 }. Then ′′ is a finite subcover of  that covers 𝐴. If ′ does not contain 𝐴𝑐 then ′ is a finite subcover of  that covers 𝐴. Either way there is a finite subcover of  that covers 𝐴. Thus 𝐴 is compact.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝐴 be a closed subset of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 is compact. ⋃
nodes.explanation: States a main result about closed subspaces of compact spaces are compact; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ compact},\ A\subseteq X\text{ closed}\Longrightarrow A\text{ compact}

nodes.title: Compact Subsets of Hausdorff Spaces Are Closed
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space, and let 𝐾 be a subset of 𝑋 which is compact. Then 𝐾 is closed in 𝑋.

Proof. We show that 𝐾 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝐾 is open in 𝑋. Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝐾 𝑐 . Then for each 𝑦 ∈ 𝐾 there are neighborhoods 𝑈𝑦 and 𝑉𝑦 of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively, such that 𝑈𝑦 ∩ 𝑉𝑦 = ∅, since 𝑋 is assumed to be Hausdorff and 𝑥 ≠ 𝑦. The collection  = {𝑉𝑦 ∣ 𝑦 ∈ 𝐾} of open subsets of 𝑋 covers 𝐾 since ⋃ 𝐾⊆ 𝑉𝑦 . 𝑦∈𝐾

Since 𝐾 is assumed to be compact there must be a finite subcollection {𝑉𝑦1 , 𝑉𝑦2 , … , 𝑉𝑦𝑛 } that covers 𝐾. Let 𝑉 = 𝑉𝑦1 ∪ 𝑉𝑦2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ 𝑉𝑦𝑛 , and let 𝑈 = 𝑈𝑦1 ∩ 𝑈𝑦2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑦𝑛 . Then 𝑈 is open in 𝑋, 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 and 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅. Furthermore, 𝑈 ⊆ 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑉 ⊆ 𝑋 ⧵ 𝐾 = 𝐾 𝑐 . Hence, 𝐾 𝑐 is open in 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space, and let 𝐾 be a subset of 𝑋 which is compact. Then 𝐾 is closed in 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about compact subsets of hausdorff spaces are closed; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ Hausdorff},\ K\subseteq X\text{ compact}\Longrightarrow K\text{ closed in }X

nodes.title: Continuous Images of Compact Spaces Are Compact
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a compact space, 𝑌 a topological space and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is compact.

Proof. Let  = {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be an open cover of 𝑌 . Then  = {𝑓 −1 (𝑈𝜆 )}𝜆∈Λ is an open cover of 𝑋. Since 𝑋 is compact there must be a finite subcover ′ = {𝑓 −1 (𝑈𝜆1 ), 𝑓 −1 (𝑈𝜆2 ), … , 𝑓 −1 (𝑈𝜆𝑛 )} of . Then  ′ = {𝑈𝜆1 , 𝑈𝜆2 , … , 𝑈𝜆𝑛 } is a finite subcover of . Hence, 𝑌 is compact.

The theorem can be extended to saying that the continuous image of a compact space is compact, i.e., assuming 𝑋 is a compact space and 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a continuous map then 𝑓 (𝑋) is compact.

We will prove that the (finite) product of compact spaces is compact. To prove this we need the following result.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a compact space, 𝑌 a topological space and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is compact.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about continuous images of compact spaces are compact; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ compact},\ f:X\twoheadrightarrow Y\text{ continuous}\Longrightarrow Y\text{ compact}

nodes.title: Tube lemma
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑌 be a compact space. If 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝑈 is an open set in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑌 containing {𝑥} × 𝑌 , then there is a neighborhood 𝑊 of 𝑥 in 𝑋 such that 𝑊 × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 .

The set 𝑊 × 𝑌 is often called a tube about {𝑥} × 𝑌 . See Figure 6.4.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 55

𝑌 𝑋×𝑌 𝑈

𝑊 𝑥 𝑋

Figure 6.4: A tube about {𝑥} × 𝑌 .

Proof. As 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 × 𝑌 and (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ {𝑥} × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 for all 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 , there is a basis element 𝑊𝑦 ×𝑉𝑦 ⊆ 𝑈 for the product topology on 𝑋 ×𝑌 such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑊𝑦 ×𝑉𝑦 . The collection {𝑉𝑦 }𝑦∈𝑌 is an open cover of 𝑌 . Since 𝑌 is compact there must be a finite subcover of {𝑉𝑦 }𝑦∈𝑌 , say, {𝑉𝑦1 , 𝑉𝑦2 , … , 𝑉𝑦𝑛 }. Let 𝑛 ⋂ 𝑊 = 𝑊𝑦𝑖 . 𝑖=1

Then 𝑊 is open in 𝑋, and it must contain 𝑥. Clearly, 𝑛 ( ) ⋃ 𝑊 ×𝑌 ⊆ 𝑊𝑦𝑖 × 𝑉𝑦𝑖 ⊆ 𝑈 . 𝑖=1

Thus {𝑥} × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑊 × 𝑌 and 𝑊 × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 .

The lemma is not true if we remove the assumption that 𝑌 is compact; the open set

𝑈 = {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ ℝ × ℝ ∣ |𝑥𝑦| < 1} ⊆ ℝ × ℝ

does contain {0} × ℝ but does not contain any tube 𝑊 × ℝ containing {0} × ℝ. (Here we have assumed that ℝ is given the standard topology, and hence, it is not compact, cf. Example 6.32.)
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑌 be a compact space. If 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝑈 is an open set in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑌 containing {𝑥} × 𝑌 , then there is a neighborhood 𝑊 of 𝑥 in 𝑋 such that 𝑊 × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 . The set 𝑊 × 𝑌 is often called a tube about {𝑥} × 𝑌 . See Figure 6.4.
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about tube lemma that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Finite Products of Compact Spaces Are Compact
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be compact spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is compact.

Proof. We prove the statement for the product of two compact spaces. The general result then follows by an inductive argument. Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be compact spaces. Let  be an open cover of 𝑋 × 𝑌 . We must show that there is a finite subcover ′ of . For each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, {𝑥} × 𝑌 is compact as it is homeomorphic to 𝑌 which is assumed to be compact. Thus there is a finite subcollection 𝑥 of  that covers {𝑥} × 𝑌 . Let ⋃ 𝑈𝑥 = 𝐴𝑥 ∈𝑥 𝐴𝑥 . Then 𝑈𝑥 is open in 𝑋 × 𝑌 and contains {𝑥} × 𝑌 . Thus by Lemma 6.38 for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 there is a neighborhood 𝑊𝑥 ⊆ 𝑋 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝑊𝑥 and 𝑊𝑥 × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈𝑥 . Furthermore, 𝑥 covers 𝑊𝑥 × 𝑌 . Now let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 vary. The collection {𝑊𝑥 }𝑥∈𝑋 is then an open cover of 𝑋. Since 𝑋 is compact, there must be a finite subcover {𝑊𝑥1 , 𝑊𝑥2 , … , 𝑊𝑥𝑛 } of {𝑊𝑥 }𝑥∈𝑋 . For each 1 ⩽ 𝑖 ⩽ 𝑛 the subspace

56 6.3. Compact spaces

𝑊𝑥𝑖 × 𝑌 is covered by the finite subcollection 𝑥𝑖 of . Hence,

𝑛 ⋃ 𝑋×𝑌 = 𝑊𝑥 𝑖 × 𝑌 𝑖=1 ⋃𝑛 is covered by the subcollection ′ = 𝑖=1 𝑥𝑖 of . Thus 𝑋 × 𝑌 is compact.

The theorem can be extended to hold for arbitrary products of compact spaces if we equip the product with the product topology. This is known as Tychonoff’s theorem. It is a deep result whose proof uses several original ideas. If we equip the product with the box topology the statement is no longer true. See, e.g., [4, §37] for a discussion and proof of Tychonoff’s theorem.

We have already seen that the real line (with the standard topology) is not compact, cf. Exam- ple 6.32. The next theorem states that all closed intervals of the real line are compact.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be compact spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is compact.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about finite products of compact spaces are compact; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X_1,\dots,X_n\text{ compact}\Longrightarrow X_1\times\cdots\times X_n\text{ compact}

nodes.title: Compact Subsets of the Real Line
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then every closed interval [𝑎, 𝑏] in ℝ is compact.

Proof. Let  = {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a cover of [𝑎, 𝑏] by open subsets of ℝ, and let

𝑆 = {𝑥 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] ∣ [𝑎, 𝑥] is covered by a finite subcollection of }.

Note that 𝑆 is bounded above by 𝑏. Since 𝑎 ∈ 𝑈𝜆 for some 𝜆 ∈ Λ, the singleton {𝑈𝜆 } is a finite subcollection of  that covers [𝑎, 𝑎] = {𝑎}. Hence, 𝑆 is non-empty and it is bounded above. Thus 𝑆 has a least upper bound; 𝑐 = sup 𝑆. Clearly, 𝑎 ⩽ 𝑐 ⩽ 𝑏. We will show that 𝑐 ∈ 𝑆. The result follows if we can also show that 𝑐 = 𝑏. Choose 𝜆′ ∈ Λ with 𝑐 ∈ 𝑈𝜆′ . Since 𝑈𝜆′ is open in ℝ there is a real number 𝜖 > 0 such that (𝑐 − 𝜖, 𝑐 + 𝜖) ⊆ 𝑈𝜆′ . Moreover, there is an 𝑥 ∈ 𝑆 such that 𝑐 − 𝜖 < 𝑥, and so, by definition of 𝑆 there is a finite subcollection ⋃ {𝑈𝜆1 , 𝑈𝜆2 , … , 𝑈𝜆𝑛 } of  such that [𝑎, 𝑥] ⊆ 𝑛𝑖=1 𝑈𝜆𝑖 . Furthermore, [𝑥, 𝑐] ⊆ 𝑈𝜆′ . Thus [𝑎, 𝑐] = [𝑎, 𝑥] ∪ [𝑥, 𝑐] is covered by the finite subcollection {𝑈𝜆1 , 𝑈𝜆2 , … , 𝑈𝜆𝑛 , 𝑈𝜆′ } of . Hence, 𝑐 ∈ 𝑆. We now show that 𝑐 = 𝑏. Assume that 𝑐 < 𝑏. Then there must be a 𝑦 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] such that 𝑐 < 𝑦 < 𝑐 + 𝜖. Thus [𝑎, 𝑦] is covered by the subcollection {𝑈𝜆1 , 𝑈𝜆2 , … , 𝑈𝜆𝑛 , 𝑈𝜆′ } of  such that 𝑦 ∈ 𝑆. This is a contradiction of the fact that 𝑐 is an upper bound. Hence, 𝑐 = 𝑏.

In order to state and prove the Heine–Borel theorem we need the following definition.
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then every closed interval [𝑎, 𝑏] in ℝ is compact.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about compact subsets of the real line; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: [a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}\text{ is compact}

nodes.title: Bounded subsets
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is bounded if there is an 𝑀 ∈ ℝ such that 𝑑(𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ) ⩽ 𝑀 for all 𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ∈ 𝐴.

Equivalently, we may say that a subset 𝐴 of a metric space (𝑋, 𝑑) is bounded if there is a 𝐾 ∈ ℝ and 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑑(𝑎, 𝑥) ⩽ 𝐾 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴. In particular, this means that a subset of ℝ𝑛 equipped with the Euclidean metric is bounded if it is contained in some open ball of finite radius centered at the origin.
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is bounded if there is an 𝑀 ∈ ℝ such that 𝑑(𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ) ⩽ 𝑀 for all 𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ∈ 𝐴. Equivalently, we may say that a subset 𝐴 of a metric space (𝑋, 𝑑) is bounded if there is a 𝐾 ∈ ℝ and 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑑(𝑎, 𝑥) ⩽ 𝐾 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴. In particular, this means that a subset of ℝ𝑛 equipped with the Euclidean metric is bounded if it is contained in some open ball of finite radius centered at the origin.
nodes.explanation: Defines bounded subsets and fixes terminology used later in metric, sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: A\subseteq X\text{ bounded}\iff \exists M\in\mathbb{R}\ \forall a_1,a_2\in A,\ d(a_1,a_2)\le M\iff \exists x\in X\ \exists K\in\mathbb{R}\ \forall a\in A,\ d(a,x)\le K

nodes.title: Heine–Borel
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ𝑛 be given the (Euclidean) metric topology and the Eu- clidean metric. A subset 𝐴 of ℝ𝑛 is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 57

Proof. Assume that 𝐴 is compact. By Theorem 6.22, ℝ𝑛 is Hausdorff, and so, by Theorem 6.36 𝐴 is closed in ℝ𝑛 . We must show that 𝐴 is also bounded. Let  = {𝖡(0; 𝑛) ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ+ }, i.e., a collection of open balls centered at the origin in ℝ𝑛 . Then  is a cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of ℝ𝑛 . Since 𝐴 is compact there must be a finite subcollection of  that covers 𝐴. Thus there is an 𝑁 ∈ ℤ+ such that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝖡(0; 𝑁). Hence, for all 𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ∈ 𝐴 we have 𝑑(𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ) < 2𝑁. Thus 𝐴 is bounded. Now assume that 𝐴 is bounded and closed in ℝ𝑛 . Let 𝑝 = (𝑝1 , 𝑝2 , … , 𝑝𝑛 ) ∈ 𝐴. Since 𝐴 is bounded there is an 𝑀 ∈ ℝ such that 𝑑(𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ) ⩽ 𝑀 for all 𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ∈ 𝐴. Then 𝐴 is contained in the product space 𝑃 = [𝑝1 − 𝑀, 𝑝1 + 𝑀] × [𝑝2 − 𝑀, 𝑝2 + 𝑀] × ⋯ × [𝑝𝑛 − 𝑀, 𝑝𝑛 + 𝑀]

which by Theorem 6.40 and Theorem 6.39 is compact. Since 𝐴 is closed in ℝ𝑛 and a subset of 𝑃 , it follows by extending Theorem 6.35 to our setting that 𝐴 is compact. Specifically, let  be a cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of ℝ𝑛 . Since 𝐴 is closed in ℝ𝑛 , 𝐴𝑐 = ℝ𝑛 ⧵ 𝐴 is open in ℝ𝑛 . Thus

 =  ∪ {𝐴𝑐 }

is an open cover of ℝ𝑛 , and thus it is also an open cover of 𝑃 in the subspace topology. Since 𝑃 is compact there must be a finite subcover of  that covers 𝑃 . This implies that there is a finite subcover of  that covers 𝐴. Thus 𝐴 is compact.

An immediate consequence of Theorem 6.42 is that 𝑆 𝑛 considered as a subspace of ℝ𝑛+1 is com- pact, and hence, that the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 is compact. We end this section with a proof of the generalized extreme value theorem.
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ𝑛 be given the (Euclidean) metric topology and the Euclidean metric. A subset 𝐴 of ℝ𝑛 is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about heine–borel; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: A\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\text{ compact}\iff A\text{ is closed and bounded}

nodes.title: Generalized extreme value theorem
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. Then there are 𝑚, 𝑀 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑚) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑥) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑀) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋.

Proof. By Theorem 6.37, 𝑓 (𝑋) is compact. We must show that 𝑓 (𝑋) contains its supremum and its infimum. If it does, then by setting 𝑓 (𝑚) = inf 𝑓 (𝑋) and 𝑓 (𝑀) = sup 𝑓 (𝑋) the theorem follows. We prove that 𝑓 (𝑋) contains its supremum. The proof for the infimum is similar. Since 𝑓 (𝑋) is compact, it is closed and bounded by Theorem 6.42. In particular, 𝑓 (𝑋) is bounded above. Hence, the set 𝑓 (𝑋) has a least upper bound; 𝑠 = sup 𝑓 (𝑋). Thus 𝑝 ⩽ 𝑠 for all 𝑝 ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋). We must show that 𝑠 ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋). Assume that 𝑠 ∉ 𝑓 (𝑋). Since 𝑓 (𝑋) is closed, i.e., 𝑓 (𝑋)𝑐 = ℝ⧵𝑓 (𝑋) is open, it follows that there is a real number 𝜖 > 0 such that (𝑠 − 𝜖, 𝑠 + 𝜖) ⊆ 𝑓 (𝑋)𝑐 , and so,

(𝑠 − 𝜖, 𝑠 + 𝜖) ∩ 𝑓 (𝑋) = ∅.

Hence, there is a real number 𝑦 such that 𝑦 is an upper bound of 𝑓 (𝑋) and 𝑠 − 𝜖 < 𝑦 < 𝑠. This is a contradiction to the fact that 𝑠 is the least upper bound of 𝑓 (𝑋). Hence, 𝑠 = sup 𝑓 (𝑋) ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋).

58 6.4. Exercises

6.4 Exercises

Exercise 6.1 Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Show that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is surjective continuous map and 𝑋 is path-connected then 𝑌 is also path-connected.

We say that a subset 𝐴 of a topological space 𝑋 is path-connected if 𝐴 is path-connected in the subspace topology. Hence, the previous exercise may be extended to saying that the continuous image of a path-connected space is path-connected.

Exercise 6.2 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴 be subspaces of 𝑋.

(a) Show that if 𝐴 is connected then so is 𝐵.

(b) Show that [𝑎, 𝑏), (𝑎, 𝑏], [𝑎, 𝑏], (−∞, 𝑏] and [𝑎, ∞) are all connected spaces when considered as subspaces of ℝ with the standard topology.

Exercise 6.3 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and consider 𝐼 = [0, 1] as a subspace of ℝ where ℝ is given the standard topology. Furthermore, let the cone on 𝑋 be quotient space 𝐶𝑋 = 𝑋×𝐼∕∼, where ∼ is the equivalence relation on the product space 𝑋 × 𝐼 given by (𝑥, 0) ∼ (𝑥′ , 0) for all 𝑥, 𝑥′ ∈ 𝑋. Show that 𝐶𝑋 is path-connected.

Exercise 6.4 Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Show that 𝐴 is Hausdorff.

Exercise 6.5 Let 𝑋 be an infinite set with the cofinite topology.

(a) Show that 𝑋 is compact.

(b) Show that any subset of 𝑋 is compact.

Exercise 6.6 Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝑌 be a Hausdorff space. Furthermore, let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a continuous map.

(a) Show that 𝑓 is a closed map.

(b) Show that if 𝑓 is a surjective continuous map, then 𝑓 is a quotient map.

(c) Show that if 𝑓 a bijective continuous map, then 𝑓 is a homeomorphism.

(d) Show that 𝑓 is proper, i.e., for each subset 𝐾 of 𝑌 that is compact the preimage 𝑓 −1 (𝐾) is compact.

Exercise 6.7 Show that the surface of the cube centered at the origin,

𝐶 = {(𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑧) ∈ ℝ3 ∣ max{|𝑥|, |𝑦|, |𝑧|} = 1},

and the 2-sphere, { } |√ 𝑆 2 = (𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑧) ∈ ℝ3 | 𝑥2 + 𝑦2 + 𝑧2 = 1 , |

Chapter 6. Topological properties 59

are homeomorphic where they are both considered to be subspaces of ℝ3 with the standard topology.

Exercise 6.8 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴1 .𝐴2 , … , 𝐴𝑛 be subspaces of 𝑋 each of which is compact. Show that 𝑛 ⋃ 𝐴𝑖 𝑖=1

is compact.

7. The fundamental group

7.1 Homotopy of paths The topological properties we have discussed so far do not help us to distinguish between, e.g., the 2-sphere 𝑆 2 and the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 . In order to prove that 𝑆 2 and 𝑇 2 are not topologically equivalent, i.e., 𝑆 2 ≇ 𝑇 2 , we need new properties and new techniques. A such property is that of simple connectedness. A path-connected space 𝑋 is, roughly speaking, simply connected if every closed curve in 𝑋 can be shrunk to a point in 𝑋. The 2-sphere 𝑆 2 is simply connected as any closed curve on 𝑆 2 can be shrunk to a point in 𝑆 2 . On the other hand, the torus 𝑇 2 is not simply connected as there are closed curves which cannot be shrunk to a point in 𝑇 2 . See Figure 7.1. To be simply connected is a topological property. Hence, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and one of them is simply connected then so is the other. We can express that a path connected space is simply connected by saying that its fundamental group is trivial. In particular, if 𝑋 ≅ 𝑌 then their fundamental groups are isomorphic. We will show that the fundamental group of 𝑆 2 is not isomorphic to the fundamental group of 𝑇 2 . Hence, 𝑆 2 ≇ 𝑇 2 . Computing fundamental groups provides us with more information than just that of being simply connected.

Figure 7.1: The 2-sphere 𝑆 2 is simply connected while the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 ×𝑆 1 is not simply connected.

In order to define the fundamental group of a topological space, we will need the concept of ho- motopy (of paths).
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. Then there are 𝑚, 𝑀 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑚) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑥) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑀) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about generalized extreme value theorem; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ compact},\ f:X\to\mathbb{R}\text{ continuous}\Longrightarrow \exists m,M\in X:\ \forall x\in X,\ f(m)\le f(x)\le f(M)

nodes.title: Homotopy
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be two continuous maps. Furthermore, let ℝ be the set of real numbers with the standard topology, 𝐼 = [0, 1] be a subspace of ℝ, and let 𝑋 × 𝐼 be given the product topology. We say that 𝑓0 is homotopic to 𝑓1 , written 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 , if there is a continuous map

𝐻∶ 𝑋 ×𝐼 → 𝑌

such that 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑓0 (𝑥) and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) = 𝑓1 (𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. The map 𝐻 is called a homotopy between 𝑓0 and 𝑓1 . If 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 and 𝑓1 is a constant map, we say that 𝑓0 is nullhomotopic.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 61

We can think of a homotopy as a continuous one-parameter family {𝐻|𝑋×{𝑡} }𝑡∈𝐼 of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 . See Figure 7.2.

𝑓1 𝐼 𝑌 𝑡 𝐻

𝑋

𝑓0

Figure 7.2: A homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓1 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be two continuous maps. Furthermore, let ℝ be the set of real numbers with the standard topology, 𝐼 = [0, 1] be a subspace of ℝ, and let 𝑋 × 𝐼 be given the product topology. We say that 𝑓0 is homotopic to 𝑓1 , written 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 , if there is a continuous map 𝐻∶ 𝑋 ×𝐼 → 𝑌
nodes.explanation: Defines homotopy and fixes terminology used later in continuity, homotopy, maps.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f_0\simeq f_1\iff \exists H:X\times I\to Y\text{ continuous with }H(x,0)=f_0(x)\text{ and }H(x,1)=f_1(x)

nodes.title: Straight-line homotopy
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then any two continu- ous maps 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ𝑛 , where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology, are homotopic. The map 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → ℝ𝑛 given by

𝐻(𝑥, 𝑡) = (1 − 𝑡)𝑓0 (𝑥) + 𝑡𝑓1 (𝑥).

for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼 = [0, 1] is a homotopy between 𝑓0 and 𝑓1 . We call it the straight-line homotopy as it deforms for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the point 𝑓0 (𝑥) to the point 𝑓1 (𝑥) along the straight-line segment between them. In particular, any continuous map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ𝑛 is homotopic to the constant map 𝑐 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ𝑛 given by 𝑐(𝑥) = 0 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Hence, any continuous map into ℝ𝑛 is nullhomotopic.

We will need the following lemma to prove that ≃ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps between a topological space 𝑋 and a topological space 𝑌 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then any two continuous maps 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ𝑛 , where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology, are homotopic. The map 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → ℝ𝑛 given by 𝐻(𝑥, 𝑡) = (1 − 𝑡)𝑓0 (𝑥) + 𝑡𝑓1 (𝑥).
nodes.explanation: Illustrates straight-line homotopy in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Pasting lemma
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 = 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵 be a topological space, where 𝐴 and 𝐵 are closed in 𝑋. Furthermore, let 𝑌 be a topological space, and assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐵 → 𝑌 are continuous maps. If 𝑓 (𝑥) = 𝑔(𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵, then the map ℎ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 given by { 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ℎ(𝑥) = 𝑔(𝑥) 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵 is continuous.

Proof. We will prove that the preimage of a closed subset of 𝑌 under ℎ is closed in 𝑋. Let 𝐶 be a closed subset of 𝑌 . Then ℎ−1 (𝐶) = 𝑓 −1 (𝐶) ∪ 𝑔 −1 (𝐶). Since 𝑓 is continuous, it follows that 𝑓 −1 (𝐶) is closed in 𝐴, and so, it is closed in 𝑋. Similarly, 𝑔 −1 (𝐶) is closed in 𝐵, and so, it is closed in 𝑋. Hence, ℎ−1 (𝐶) = 𝑓 −1 (𝐶) ∪ 𝑔 −1 (𝐶)

62 7.1. Homotopy of paths

is closed in 𝑋 as it is the union of two closed subsets of 𝑋. Thus ℎ is continuous.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 = 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵 be a topological space, where 𝐴 and 𝐵 are closed in 𝑋. Furthermore, let 𝑌 be a topological space, and assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐵 → 𝑌 are continuous maps. If 𝑓 (𝑥) = 𝑔(𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵, then the map ℎ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 given by { 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ℎ(𝑥) = 𝑔(𝑥) 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵 is continuous.

nodes.title: Homotopy Relative Endpoints Is an Equivalence Relation
nodes.originalText:
The relation ≃ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps from a topological space 𝑋 to a topological space 𝑌 .

Proof. We must show that ≃ is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. For any continuous map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 there is a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 given by 𝐻(𝑥, 𝑡) = 𝑓 (𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. Then 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑥) and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) = 𝑓 (𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Hence, 𝑓 ≃ 𝑓 . Thus ≃ is reflexive. Let 𝑓0 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be two continuous maps, and let 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a homotopy between them. Then 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 given by 𝐻(𝑥, 𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑥, 1 − 𝑡) is a homotopy between 𝑓1 and 𝑓0 . Hence, 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 implies 𝑓1 ≃ 𝑓0 . Thus ≃ is symmetric. To show that ≃ is transitive, we must show that if 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 and 𝑓1 ≃ 𝑓2 , then 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓2 . Let 𝐻1 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓1 , and let 𝐻2 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a homotopy from 𝑓1 to 𝑓2 . Then there is a map 𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 given by { 𝐻1 (𝑥, 2𝑡) 0 ⩽ 𝑡 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑥, 𝑡) = 𝐻2 (𝑥, 2𝑡 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑡 ⩽ 1. It is continuous for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼 = [0, 1] by Lemma 7.3 since 𝑋 × [0, 1∕2] and 𝑋 × [1∕2, 1] are closed subsets of 𝑋 × 𝐼 whose union is equal to 𝑋 × 𝐼, and 𝐻1 (𝑥, 1) = 𝐻2 (𝑥, 0) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Hence, 𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 is a homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓2 . Thus ≃ is transitive.
nodes.formalStatement: The relation ≃ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps from a topological space 𝑋 to a topological space 𝑌 .

nodes.mathematicalFormula: \simeq\text{ is an equivalence relation on }C(X,Y)

nodes.title: Homotopy classes
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) be the set of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 . The homotopy classes in 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) are the equivalence classes under the relation ≃. We write [𝑓 ] for the homotopy class of 𝑓 ∈ 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ), i.e.,

[𝑓 ] = {𝑔 ∈ 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) ∣ 𝑓 ≃ 𝑔},

and we write [𝑋, 𝑌 ] for the set of homotopy classes of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 , i.e., [𝑋, 𝑌 ] = 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 )∕≃.

If we can connect two points 𝑥 and 𝑦 in a topological space 𝑋 by a path, i.e., a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑥 and 𝑓 (𝑏) = 𝑦, cf. Definition 6.14, we say that they lie in the same path component. Specifically, the relation ∼𝑝 given by 𝑥 ∼𝑝 𝑦 if and only if there is a path from 𝑥 to 𝑦 in 𝑋, is an equivalence relation on 𝑋. We say that an equivalence class in 𝑋 under ∼𝑝 is a path component. We write 𝜋0 (𝑋) for the set of path components of 𝑋. Note that 𝜋0 (𝑋) consists of only one element if and only if 𝑋 is path-connected.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) be the set of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 . The homotopy classes in 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) are the equivalence classes under the relation ≃. We write [𝑓 ] for the homotopy class of 𝑓 ∈ 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ), i.e., [𝑓 ] = {𝑔 ∈ 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) ∣ 𝑓 ≃ 𝑔},
nodes.explanation: Defines homotopy classes and fixes terminology used later in continuity, homotopy, maps.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: [f]=\{g\in C(X,Y)\mid f\simeq g\}

nodes.title: Constant Paths Under Continuous Maps
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 = {𝑥0 } and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Then the continuous maps 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 correspond to the points 𝑓 (𝑥0 ) = 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 . Two continuous maps 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 are homotopic if and only if there is a path from 𝑦0 = 𝑓0 (𝑥0 ) to 𝑦1 = 𝑓1 (𝑥0 ). Hence, the homotopy classes of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 correspond to the path components of 𝑌 , and [𝑋, 𝑌 ] corresponds to 𝜋0 (𝑌 ).

We are particularly interested in the case where we have homotopies between paths in a topological space 𝑋. For simplicity, let all paths be continuous maps from 𝐼 = [0, 1] to 𝑋.

nodes.title: Path homotopy
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. We say that two paths 𝑓 , 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 in 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 are path homotopic, written 𝑓 ≃𝑝 𝑔, if there is a continuous map 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 such that

𝐻(𝑠, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑠) and 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = 𝑔(𝑠)

for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, and

𝐻(0, 𝑡) = 𝑥0 and 𝐻(1, 𝑡) = 𝑥1

for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. We call 𝐻 a path homotopy from 𝑓 to 𝑔.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. We say that two paths 𝑓 , 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 in 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 are path homotopic, written 𝑓 ≃𝑝 𝑔, if there is a continuous map 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 such that 𝐻(𝑠, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑠) and 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = 𝑔(𝑠)

nodes.title: Path Connectedness Is an Equivalence Relation
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. Then the relation ≃𝑝 is an equivalence relation on the set of all paths from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 in 𝑋.

Proof. The result follows by proving that the homotopies constructed in the proof of Theorem 7.4 when applied to paths are path homotopies. Let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 . Then the constant homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 given by

𝐻(𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝑓 (𝑠)

is path homotopy from 𝑓 to 𝑓 : 𝐻(𝑠, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑠) and 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = 𝑓 (𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, and 𝐻(0, 𝑡) = 𝑓 (0) = 𝑥0 and 𝐻(1, 𝑡) = 𝑓 (1) = 𝑥1 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. Assume that 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path homotopy between paths 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 . Then the homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 given by

𝐻(𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑠, 1 − 𝑡)

is a path homotopy from 𝑔 to 𝑓 : 𝐻(𝑠, 0) = 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = 𝑔(𝑠) and 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = 𝐻(𝑠, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, and 𝐻(0, 𝑡) = 𝐻(0, 1 − 𝑡) = 𝑥0 and 𝐻(1, 𝑡) = 𝐻(1, 1 − 𝑡) = 𝑥1 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. Finally, assume that 𝑓0 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋, 𝑓1 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 and 𝑓2 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 are paths from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , and that 𝐻1 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓1 and that 𝐻2 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path homotopy from 𝑓1 to 𝑓2 . Then the homotopy 𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 given by { 𝐻1 (𝑠, 2𝑡) 0 ⩽ 𝑡 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝐻2 (𝑠, 2𝑡 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑡 ⩽ 1

is a path homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓2 : (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 0) = 𝐻1 (𝑠, 0) = 𝑓0 (𝑠) and (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 1) = 𝐻2 (𝑠, 1) = 𝑓2 (𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, and (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(0, 𝑡) = 𝑥0 and (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(1, 𝑡) = 𝑥1 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼.

nodes.title: Path homotopy classes
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , we write [𝑓 ] for its path homotopy class, i.e.,

[𝑓 ] = {𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 ∣ 𝑔 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 and 𝑓 ≃𝑝 𝑔}.

The fundamental group of a topological space 𝑋 with basepoint 𝑥0 is, as a set, the set of path homotopy classes of paths from 𝑥0 to 𝑥0 , i.e., the set of path homotopy classes of loops based at 𝑥0 . The group structure of the fundamental group is derived from the following product.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , we write [𝑓 ] for its path homotopy class, i.e., [𝑓 ] = {𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 ∣ 𝑔 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 and 𝑓 ≃𝑝 𝑔}.

nodes.mathematicalFormula: [f]=\{g:I\to X\mid g\text{ is a path from }x_0\text{ to }x_1\text{ and }f\simeq_p g\}

nodes.title: Product of paths
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 , 𝑥2 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 , we define the product of 𝑓 and 𝑔 as the path 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥2 given by { 𝑓 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝑓 ∗ 𝑔)(𝑠) = 𝑔(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1.

Note that the map 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 is well-defined and continuous by Lemma 7.3. Furthermore, as (𝑓 ∗ 𝑔)(0) = 𝑓 (0) = 𝑥0 and (𝑓 ∗ 𝑔)(1) = 𝑔(1) = 𝑥2 , 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥2 . See Figure 7.5.

nodes.title: Associativity of Path Products up to Homotopy
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 , 𝑥2 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 , then the product 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 induces a well-defined operation on path homotopy classes given by

[𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔] = [𝑓 ∗ 𝑔].

Proof. Let 𝑓 ′ ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , and let 𝑔 ′ ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 . Fur- thermore, let 𝐻1 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path homotopy from 𝑓 to 𝑓 ′ , and let 𝐻2 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path homotopy from 𝑔 to 𝑔 ′ . Then the map 𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 given by { 𝐻1 (2𝑠, 𝑡) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝐻2 (2𝑠 − 1, 𝑡) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1

66 7.1. Homotopy of paths

is a path homotopy from 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 to 𝑓 ′ ∗ 𝑔 ′ : it is well-defined since 𝐻1 (1, 𝑡) = 𝑥1 = 𝐻2 (0, 𝑡) for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼 and it is continuous by Lemma 7.3, and

(𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 0) = (𝑓 ∗ 𝑔)(𝑠) and (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 1) = (𝑓 ′ ∗ 𝑔 ′ )(𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(0, 𝑡) = 𝐻1 (0, 𝑡) = 𝑥0 and (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(1, 𝑡) = 𝐻2 (1, 𝑡) = 𝑥2 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼.

Hence, 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 induces a well-defined operation on path homotopy classes given by [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔] = [𝑓 ∗ 𝑔].
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 , 𝑥2 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 , then the product 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 induces a well-defined operation on path homotopy classes given by [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔] = [𝑓 ∗ 𝑔].
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about associativity of path products up to homotopy that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Path Products Have Two-Sided Homotopy Inverses
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then the product of paths, ∗, has the following properties on the set of path homotopy classes in 𝑋.

Associativity Let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 , 𝑥2 and 𝑥3 be points in 𝑋. If 𝑓0 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 , and 𝑓2 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥2 to 𝑥3 , then

([𝑓0 ] ∗ [𝑓1 ]) ∗ [𝑓2 ] = [𝑓0 ] ∗ ([𝑓1 ] ∗ [𝑓2 ]).

Left and right units For 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, let 𝑐𝑥 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 denote the constant path at 𝑥, given by 𝑐𝑥 (𝑠) = 𝑥 for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 then

[𝑐𝑥0 ] ∗ [𝑓 ] = [𝑓 ] = [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑐𝑥1 ].

Inverse If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be the reverse path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥0 , given by 𝑓 (𝑠) = 𝑓 (1 − 𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼. Then

[𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑓 ] = [𝑐𝑥0 ] and [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑓 ] = [𝑐𝑥1 ].

and { 𝑓0 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝑓0 ∗ (𝑓1 ∗ 𝑓2 ))(𝑠) = (𝑓1 ∗ 𝑓2 )(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 ⎧𝑓0 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ⎪ = ⎨𝑓1 (4𝑠 − 2) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 3∕4 (7.2) ⎪𝑓 (4𝑠 − 3) 3∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1. ⎩ 2

We prove that the constant path is a left unit under ∗ for path homotopy classes. The proof for the fact that the constant path is a right unit under ∗ is similar. 70 7.2. Definition and elementary properties of the fundamental group

7.2 Definition and elementary properties of the fundamental group The set of path homotopy classes of paths in a topological space 𝑋 does not form a group under the product of paths as the product of two path homotopy classes is not always defined. However, such a product is defined if we restrict to paths that begin and end at some fixed point 𝑥0 . This is the fundamental group of 𝑋 with basepoint 𝑥0 . We say that the ordered pair (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is a based space (sometimes also called a pointed space).

nodes.title: The fundamental group
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) be a based space. A path 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥0 is called a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 . Let

𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) = {[𝑓 ] ∣ 𝑓 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 }

be the set of path homotopy classes of loops in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 . We say that 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is the fundamental group of 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 .

From the definition it is clear that 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) depends only on the path component of 𝑋 that contains 𝑥0 . The fundamental group does not provide us with any information about the rest of 𝑋. As the notation suggests, the fundamental group is sometimes also referred to as the first homotopy group of 𝑋. In fact, there are groups 𝜋𝑛 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) for all 𝑛 ∈ ℤ+ . These groups are part of a subject called homotopy theory.

If we restrict Theorem 7.14 to the case where all paths are loops in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , we get the following result.
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) be a based space. A path 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥0 is called a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 . Let 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) = {[𝑓 ] ∣ 𝑓 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 }
nodes.explanation: Defines the fundamental group and fixes terminology used later in fundamental-group, groups, homotopy.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \pi_1(X,x_0)=\{[f]\mid f:I\to X\text{ is a loop based at }x_0\}

nodes.title: The Fundamental Group Is a Group
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) be a based space. Then the fundamental group 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) of 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 is, in fact, a group with product of paths, ∗, as its binary operation. The identity element 𝑒 is equal to the path homotopy class of the constant path at 𝑥0 , 𝑒 = [𝑐𝑥0 ], and the inverse of [𝑓 ] is [𝑓 ]−1 = [𝑓 ] where 𝑓 is the reverse path of 𝑓 .

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 71

The identity element 𝑒 is equal to the path homotopy class of the constant path at 𝑥0 , 𝑒 = [𝑐𝑥0 ], and the inverse of [𝑓 ] is [𝑓 ]−1 = [𝑓 ] where 𝑓 is the reverse path of 𝑓 .

nodes.title: Fundamental Group of the Real Line
nodes.originalText: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → ℝ is a loop in ℝ based at 0, by Example 7.2 we know that 𝑓 is path homotopic to the constant path at 0. Thus 𝜋1 (ℝ, 0) is the trivial group, i.e., 𝜋1 (ℝ, 0) = {𝑒}. In general, 𝜋1 (ℝ𝑛 , 0) is the trivial group, where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology. We will often denote the trivial group as simply 0.
nodes.formalStatement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → ℝ is a loop in ℝ based at 0, by Example 7.2 we know that 𝑓 is path homotopic to the constant path at 0. Thus 𝜋1 (ℝ, 0) is the trivial group, i.e., 𝜋1 (ℝ, 0) = {𝑒}. In general, 𝜋1 (ℝ𝑛 , 0) is the trivial group, where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology. We will often denote the trivial group as simply 0.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates fundamental group of the real line in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Fundamental Group of a Discrete Space
nodes.originalText: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) be a based space where 𝑋 is a discrete space. Then 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) = 0 for all 𝑥0 ∈ 𝑋 as the path component containing 𝑥0 consists of only one element, namely 𝑥0 .
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) be a based space where 𝑋 is a discrete space. Then 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) = 0 for all 𝑥0 ∈ 𝑋 as the path component containing 𝑥0 consists of only one element, namely 𝑥0 .

nodes.title: Fundamental Groups of Path-Connected Spaces Are Isomorphic
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a path-connected space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. Then 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is isomorphic to 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ).

Proof. Let 𝛼 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 . If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a loop in 𝑋 based 𝑥0 , then 𝛼 ∗ 𝑓 ∗ 𝛼 ̂ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) by is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥1 . We define the map 𝛼

̂([𝑓 ]) = [𝛼]−1 ∗ [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝛼] 𝛼

where we have used the fact that [ 𝛼 ] = [𝛼]−1 . It is a well-defined map as ∗ is well-defined, and moreover, it only depends on the path homotopy class of 𝛼. We will show that 𝛼 ̂ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) is an isomorphism. We will first show that 𝛼 ̂ is a homomorphism, and then show that it is bijective. Since

̂([𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔]) = [𝛼]−1 ∗ [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔] ∗ [𝛼] 𝛼 = ([𝛼]−1 ∗ [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝛼]) ∗ ([𝛼]−1 ∗ [𝑔] ∗ [𝛼]) =𝛼 ̂([𝑓 ]) ∗ 𝛼 ̂([𝑔]),

for all [𝑓 ], [𝑔] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ), it follows that 𝛼 ̂ is a homomorphism. To show that 𝛼 ̂ is bijective, and hence, an isomorphism, we show that 𝛼 ̂ has an inverse. Let 𝛽 = 𝛼, i.e., 𝛽 is the reverse path of 𝛼. Since ̂ 𝛽([ℎ]) = [𝛽]−1 ∗ [ℎ] ∗ [𝛽] = [𝛼] ∗ [ℎ] ∗ [𝛼]−1 𝛽([ℎ])) = [𝛼]−1 ∗ ([𝛽]−1 ∗ [ℎ] ∗ [𝛽]) ∗ [𝛼] = [ℎ] ̂( ̂ 𝛼

for all [ℎ] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ), and

𝛼 )−1 . for all [𝑓 ] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ), it follows that 𝛽̂ = (̂

In light of Theorem 7.19 it might be tempting to simply speak of the fundamental group of a path-connected space 𝑋, and thus leave out any mention of the basepoint. However, while 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) ≅ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) for any two points 𝑥0 and 𝑥1 in 𝑋, the isomorphism may depend on the path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 . It can be shown that the isomorphism of 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) with 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) for a path-connected space 𝑋 is independent of path if and only if the fundamental group is abelian.

72 7.2. Definition and elementary properties of the fundamental group
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a path-connected space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. Then 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is isomorphic to 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ).
nodes.explanation: States a main result about fundamental groups of path-connected spaces are isomorphic; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ path-connected}\Longrightarrow \pi_1(X,x_0)\cong \pi_1(X,x_1)

nodes.title: Simply connected spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a path-connected space. We say that 𝑋 is simply connected if 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is the trivial group for some 𝑥0 ∈ 𝑋, and hence, for all 𝑥0 ∈ 𝑋.

By Example 7.17, we get that ℝ𝑛 is simply connected where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology. We shall later see that the circle 𝑆 1 and the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 are two examples of non-simply connected spaces.
nodes.formalStatement: A path-connected space $X$ is simply connected if $\pi_1(X,x_0)$ is the trivial group for some, equivalently every, basepoint $x_0\in X$.
nodes.explanation: Simply connected spaces are path-connected spaces with no nontrivial loops up to path homotopy.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ simply connected}\iff X\text{ is path-connected and }\pi_1(X,x_0)\cong\{e\}

nodes.title: Based maps
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces. A based map ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) is a continuous map ℎ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 such that ℎ(𝑥0 ) = 𝑦0 .

Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be a based map. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , the composition ℎ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a loop in 𝑌 based at 𝑦0 . This leads to a map from 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) to 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ).
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces. A based map ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) is a continuous map ℎ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 such that ℎ(𝑥0 ) = 𝑦0 . Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be a based map. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , the composition ℎ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a loop in 𝑌 based at 𝑦0 . This leads to a map from 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) to 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ).
nodes.explanation: Defines based maps and fixes terminology used later in continuity, maps.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: h:(X,x_0)\to(Y,y_0)\text{ is based}\iff h:X\to Y\text{ is continuous and }h(x_0)=y_0

nodes.title: Homomorphisms induced by based maps
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be a based map. The map

ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 )

given by ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [ℎ◦𝑓 ] is called the homomorphism induced by ℎ.
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be a based map. The map ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 )
nodes.explanation: Defines homomorphisms induced by based maps and fixes terminology used later in maps.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: h_*:\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(Y,y_0),\qquad h_*([f])=[h\circ f]

nodes.title: Based Maps Induce Homomorphisms on Fundamental Groups
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be a based map. The map ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) given by ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [ℎ◦𝑓 ] is a homomorphism.

Proof. We need to show that for two loops 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , the identity

ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔]) = ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ]) ∗ ℎ∗ ([𝑔])

holds in 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ). The left-hand side is the path homotopy class of ℎ◦(𝑓 ∗ 𝑔) and the right-hand side is the path homotopy class of (ℎ◦𝑓 ) ∗ (ℎ◦𝑔). By definition, we have { (ℎ◦𝑓 )(2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ((ℎ◦𝑓 ) ∗ (ℎ◦𝑔))(𝑠) = (ℎ◦𝑔)(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1

and so { ℎ(𝑓 (2𝑠)) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (ℎ◦(𝑓 ∗ 𝑔))(𝑠) = ((ℎ◦𝑓 ) ∗ (ℎ◦𝑔))(𝑠) = ℎ(𝑔(2𝑠 − 1)) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1. Hence, ℎ◦(𝑓 ∗ 𝑔) = (ℎ◦𝑓 ) ∗ (ℎ◦𝑔). Thus ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔]) = ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ]) ∗ ℎ∗ ([𝑔]) holds in 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ).

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 73
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be a based map. The map ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) given by ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [ℎ◦𝑓 ] is a homomorphism.
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about based maps induce homomorphisms on fundamental groups that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Functoriality
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ), (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and (𝑍, 𝑧0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ1 ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and ℎ2 ∶ (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) → (𝑍, 𝑧0 ) be based maps. Then

(ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )∗ = (ℎ2 )∗ ◦(ℎ1 )∗ .

If id𝑋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is the identity map, then (id𝑋 )∗ is the identity automorphism of 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ).

The rule sending a based space (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) to its fundamental group 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and a based map ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) to its induced homomorphism ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) is a functor from the category of based spaces and based maps to the category of groups and (group) homomorphisms.

Proof. Assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 . Then

(ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [(ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )◦𝑓 ]

and ( ) (ℎ2 )∗ (ℎ1 )∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [ℎ2 ◦(ℎ1 ◦𝑓 )].

Since composition of continuous maps is associative, i.e., (ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )◦𝑓 = ℎ2 ◦(ℎ1 ◦𝑓 ), these path homo- topy classes must be equal. Hence, (ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )∗ = (ℎ2 )∗ ◦(ℎ1 )∗ . If id𝑋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is the identity map, then id𝑋 ◦𝑓 = 𝑓 , and so, (id𝑋 )∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [id𝑋 ◦𝑓 ] = [𝑓 ]. Hence, (id𝑋 )∗ is the identity automorphism of 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ).

The following corollary of Theorem 7.24 tells us that the fundamental group is a topological in- variant of based spaces.
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ), (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and (𝑍, 𝑧0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ1 ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and ℎ2 ∶ (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) → (𝑍, 𝑧0 ) be based maps. Then (ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )∗ = (ℎ2 )∗ ◦(ℎ1 )∗ .
nodes.explanation: States a main result about functoriality; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.

nodes.title: Based Homeomorphisms Induce Isomorphisms on Fundamental Groups
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces. If ℎ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a homeomorphism such that ℎ(𝑥0 ) = 𝑦0 , then ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) is an isomorphism.

The fundamental group thus allows us to distinguish between two based spaces provided that their funda- mental groups are not isomorphic. By Example 7.17, we know that 𝜋1 (ℝ, 0) = 0 and 𝜋1 (ℝ2 , 0) = 0. However, by Example 6.16, we know that ℝ and ℝ2 are not homeomorphic. Hence, it is possible for two non-homeomorphic based spaces to have isomorphic fundamental groups.

Proof. We must show that ℎ∗ is bijective. Let 𝑘 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 be the inverse homemorphism of ℎ such that 𝑘(𝑦0 ) = 𝑥0 . Then, by Theorem 7.24, 𝑘∗ ◦ℎ∗ = (𝑘◦ℎ)∗ = (id𝑋 )∗ , and ℎ∗ ◦𝑘∗ = (ℎ◦𝑘)∗ = (id𝑌 )∗ . Hence, 𝑘∗ = (ℎ∗ )−1 .

To compute the fundamental group of a product space, we may use the following theorem.
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces. If ℎ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a homeomorphism such that ℎ(𝑥0 ) = 𝑦0 , then ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) is an isomorphism. The fundamental group thus allows us to distinguish between two based spaces provided that their fundamental groups are not isomorphic.
nodes.explanation: Gives a consequence of earlier results concerning based homeomorphisms induce isomorphisms on fundamental groups.

nodes.title: Fundamental Group of a Product Space
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces. Then 𝜋1 (𝑋×𝑌 , (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 )) is isomorphic to the direct product 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) × 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ).

74 7.3. Homotopy type

Proof. Let pr 1 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝑌 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝑌 → 𝑌 be the projection maps onto the first and second factor, respectively. Define the map 𝜑 ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋 × 𝑌 , (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 )) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) × 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) by ( ) ( ) 𝜑([ℎ]) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) = [pr 1 ◦ℎ], [pr 2 ◦ℎ] .

Note that 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) × 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦𝑜 ) is a group with binary operation given by

([𝑓 ], [𝑔]) ∗ ([𝑓 ′ ], [𝑔 ′ ]) = ([𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑓 ′ ], [𝑔] ∗ [𝑔 ′ ])

for [𝑓 ], [𝑓 ′ ] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and [𝑔], [𝑔 ′ ] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ). We will show that 𝜑 is an isomorphism. It is a homomorphism since (pr 1 )∗ and (pr 2 )∗ are homo- morphisms: ( ) 𝜑([ℎ] ∗ [𝑘]) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ] ∗ [𝑘]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ] ∗ [𝑘]) ( ) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]) ∗ (pr 1 )∗ ([𝑘]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) ∗ (pr 2 )∗ ([𝑘]) ( ) ( ) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) ∗ (pr 1 )∗ ([𝑘]), (pr 2 )∗ ([𝑘]) = 𝜑([ℎ]) ∗ 𝜑([𝑘])

for [ℎ], [𝑘] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋 × 𝑌 , (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 )). Let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , and let 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a loop in 𝑌 based at 𝑦0 . Define the map ℎ ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 by

ℎ(𝑠) = (𝑓 (𝑠), 𝑔(𝑠)) .

Then ℎ is a loop in 𝑋 × 𝑌 based at (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 ), and ( ) ( ) 𝜑([ℎ]) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) = [pr 1 ◦ℎ], [pr 2 ◦ℎ] = ([𝑓 ], [𝑔]).

Hence, 𝜑 is surjective. To show that 𝜑 is injective, assume that 𝜑([ℎ]) = 𝜑([𝑘]) for [ℎ], [𝑘] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋 × 𝑌 , (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 )). Then there is a path homotopy 𝐻1 ∶ 𝐼 ×𝐼 → 𝑋 from pr 1 ◦ℎ to pr 1 ◦𝑘 and a path homotopy 𝐻2 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 from pr 2 ◦ℎ to pr 2 ◦𝑘. Define the map 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 by ( ) 𝐻(𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝐻1 (𝑠, 𝑡), 𝐻2 (𝑠, 𝑡) .

Then 𝐻 is a path homotopy from ℎ = (pr 1 ◦ℎ, pr 2 ◦ℎ) to 𝑘 = (pr 1 ◦𝑘, pr 2 ◦𝑘). Hence, [ℎ] = [𝑘], and so, 𝜑 is injective. Thus 𝜑 is an isomorphism.

7.3 Homotopy type We will see in this section how we may obtain information about the fundamental group by way of homotopy types. This allows us to compute the fundamental group of a topological space by computing the fundamental group of some other topological space that is hopefully easier to work with.
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces. Then 𝜋1 (𝑋×𝑌 , (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 )) is isomorphic to the direct product 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) × 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ).
nodes.explanation: States a main result about fundamental group of a product space; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \pi_1(X\times Y,(x_0,y_0))\cong \pi_1(X,x_0)\times\pi_1(Y,y_0)

nodes.title: Based Homotopic Maps Induce the Same Homomorphism
nodes.originalText:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and 𝑘 ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based maps. If there is a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 from ℎ to 𝑘 such that 𝐻(𝑥0 , 𝑡) = 𝑦0 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼, then the homomorphisms ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and 𝑘∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) induced by ℎ and 𝑘, respectively, are equal.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 75

Proof. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , then 𝐻◦(𝑓 × id) ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a homotopy from ℎ◦𝑓 to 𝑘◦𝑓 where id ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐼 is the identity map of 𝐼. Since (𝐻◦(𝑓 × id))(𝑠, 0) = (ℎ◦𝑓 )(𝑠) and (𝐻◦(𝑓 × id))(𝑠, 1) = (𝑘◦𝑓 )(𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, (𝐻◦(𝑓 × id))(0, 𝑡) = 𝑦0 and (𝐻◦(𝑓 × id))(1, 𝑡) = 𝑦0 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼, 𝐻◦(𝑓 × id) is a path homotopy from ℎ◦𝑓 to 𝑘◦𝑓 . Hence, ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [ℎ◦𝑓 ] = [𝑘◦𝑓 ] = 𝑘∗ ([𝑓 ]). Thus ℎ∗ = 𝑘∗ .
nodes.formalStatement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and 𝑘 ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based maps. If there is a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 from ℎ to 𝑘 such that 𝐻(𝑥0 , 𝑡) = 𝑦0 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼, then the homomorphisms ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and 𝑘∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) induced by ℎ and 𝑘, respectively, are equal.
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about based homotopic maps induce the same homomorphism that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Retractions
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. We say that a continuous map 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝑟(𝑎) = 𝑎 for each 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴. If there is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴, we say that 𝐴 is a retract of 𝑋.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. We say that a continuous map 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝑟(𝑎) = 𝑎 for each 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴. If there is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴, we say that 𝐴 is a retract of 𝑋.
nodes.explanation: Defines retractions and fixes terminology used later in continuity, maps, subspace.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: r:X\to A\text{ is a retraction}\iff r\text{ is continuous and }\forall a\in A,\ r(a)=a

nodes.title: The Circle Is a Retract of the Punctured Plane
nodes.originalText:
The circle, 𝑆 1 , is a retract of ℝ2 ⧵ {0} where ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Specifically, the continuous map 𝑟 ∶ ℝ2 ⧵ {0} → 𝑆 1 given by 𝑥 𝑟(𝑥) = ‖𝑥‖ √ where ‖𝑥‖ = 𝑥21 + 𝑥22 , is a retraction of ℝ2 ⧵ {0} onto 𝑆 1 .

If 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map, then we can express that 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 by the following commutative diagram. 𝑖 𝐴 𝑋 𝑟 id𝐴 𝐴

Hence, for a retraction 𝑟, we have 𝑟◦𝑖 = id𝐴 where id𝐴 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝐴 is the identity map of 𝐴.

Specifically, the continuous map 𝑟 ∶ ℝ2 ⧵ {0} → 𝑆 1 given by 𝑥 𝑟(𝑥) = ‖𝑥‖ √ where ‖𝑥‖ = 𝑥21 + 𝑥22 , is a retraction of ℝ2 ⧵ {0} onto 𝑆 1 . If 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map, then we can express that 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 by the following commutative diagram. 𝑖 𝐴 𝑋 𝑟 id𝐴 𝐴

nodes.title: Retracts Induce Injective Fundamental Group Homomorphisms
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. If 𝑥0 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝐴 is a retract of 𝑋, then the homomorphism 𝑖∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) induced by the inclusion map 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is a monomorphism.

Proof. Let 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴. Then, by Theorem 7.24 and the definition of retraction, we have the following commutative diagram 𝑖∗ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 ) 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) 𝑟∗ (id𝐴 )∗ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 )

where id𝐴 is the identity map of 𝐴. By Theorem 7.24, we know that (id𝐴 )∗ is an isomorphism. Thus 𝑟∗ ◦𝑖∗ is an isomorphism, and hence, also bijective. This implies that 𝑖∗ is injective. Hence, 𝑖∗ is a monomorphism.

The homomorphism induced by the inclusion of 𝐴 into 𝑋 is an isomorphism if 𝐴 is a deformation retract.

76 7.3. Homotopy type
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. If 𝑥0 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝐴 is a retract of 𝑋, then the homomorphism 𝑖∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) induced by the inclusion map 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is a monomorphism.
nodes.explanation: Records an auxiliary result about retracts induce injective fundamental group homomorphisms that supports later statements or proofs.

nodes.title: Deformation retracts
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. A homotopy 𝐻∶ 𝑋 ×𝐼 → 𝑋 is called a deformation retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑥 and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) ∈ 𝐴 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, and 𝐻(𝑎, 𝑡) = 𝑎 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴 and all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. We say that 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋.

The map 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 defined by 𝑟(𝑥) = 𝐻(𝑥, 1) is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴. Thus 𝐻 is a homotopy between the identity map of 𝑋 and the map 𝑖◦𝑟 where 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map. Note that some authors call what we have defined as a deformation retract, a strong deformation retract. In this terminology a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a deformation retract of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑥 and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) ∈ 𝐴 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, and 𝐻(𝑎, 1) = 𝑎 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴.

Every deformation retract is also a retract but the converse is not true in general.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. A homotopy 𝐻∶ 𝑋 ×𝐼 → 𝑋 is called a deformation retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑥 and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) ∈ 𝐴 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, and 𝐻(𝑎, 𝑡) = 𝑎 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴 and all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. We say that 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋. The map 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 defined by 𝑟(𝑥) = 𝐻(𝑥, 1) is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴. Thus 𝐻 is a homotopy between the identity map of 𝑋 and the map 𝑖◦𝑟 where 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map. Note that some authors call what we have defined as a deformation retract, a strong deformation retract. In this terminology a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a deformation retract of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑥 and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) ∈ 𝐴 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, and 𝐻(𝑎, 1) = 𝑎 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴.
nodes.explanation: Defines deformation retracts and fixes terminology used later in homotopy, subspace, topology.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: H:X\times I\to X\text{ is a deformation retraction onto }A\iff H(x,0)=x,\ H(x,1)\in A,\ H(a,t)=a

nodes.title: Singleton Retracts and Path Connectedness Obstruction
nodes.originalText: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑎 ∈ 𝑋. Then 𝐴 = {𝑎} is retract of 𝑋 with retraction 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 given by 𝑟(𝑥) = 𝑎. If 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋, then 𝑋 must be path-connected: the deformation retraction gives that there is a path from each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 to 𝑎. However, there are path-connected spaces that do not deformation retract onto a point.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑎 ∈ 𝑋. Then 𝐴 = {𝑎} is retract of 𝑋 with retraction 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 given by 𝑟(𝑥) = 𝑎. If 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋, then 𝑋 must be path-connected: the deformation retraction gives that there is a path from each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 to 𝑎. However, there are path-connected spaces that do not deformation retract onto a point.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates singleton retracts and path connectedness obstruction in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Deformation Retracts Induce Fundamental Group Isomorphisms
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. If 𝑥0 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋, then the homomorphism 𝑖∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) induced by the inclusion map 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is an isomorphism.

Proof. Let 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a deformation retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴, and let a retraction 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 be given by 𝑟(𝑥) = 𝐻(𝑥, 1). By Lemma 7.27 and Theorem 7.24, we have 𝑖∗ ◦𝑟∗ = (id𝑋 )∗ where id𝑋 is the identity map of 𝑋. Since 𝑟◦𝑖 = id𝐴 , we have 𝑟∗ ◦𝑖∗ = (id𝐴 )∗ where id𝐴 is the identity map of 𝐴. Thus both 𝑟∗ ◦𝑖∗ and 𝑖∗ ◦𝑟∗ are isomorphisms. Hence, 𝑖∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is an isomorphism with 𝑟∗ as its inverse.

We may generalize the notion of deformation retraction. This leads us to the notion of homotopy equivalence.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. If 𝑥0 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋, then the homomorphism 𝑖∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) induced by the inclusion map 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is an isomorphism.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about deformation retracts induce fundamental group isomorphisms; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: A\text{ deformation retract of }X\Longrightarrow i_*:\pi_1(A,x_0)\xrightarrow{\cong}\pi_1(X,x_0)

nodes.title: Homotopy equivalences
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 are continuous maps such that 𝑔◦𝑓 is homotopic to the identity map of 𝑋, id𝑋 , and 𝑓 ◦𝑔 is homotopic to the identity map of 𝑌 , id𝑌 , we say that 𝑓 and 𝑔 are homotopy equivalences. We say that each of 𝑓 and 𝑔 is a homotopy inverse of the other.

Clearly, every homeomorphism is also a homotopy equivalence. The converse is not true in general.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 are continuous maps such that 𝑔◦𝑓 is homotopic to the identity map of 𝑋, id𝑋 , and 𝑓 ◦𝑔 is homotopic to the identity map of 𝑌 , id𝑌 , we say that 𝑓 and 𝑔 are homotopy equivalences. We say that each of 𝑓 and 𝑔 is a homotopy inverse of the other. Clearly, every homeomorphism is also a homotopy equivalence. The converse is not true in general.
nodes.explanation: Defines homotopy equivalences and fixes terminology used later in continuity, homotopy, maps.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\simeq Y\iff \exists f:X\to Y,\ g:Y\to X\text{ continuous with }g\circ f\simeq \operatorname{id}_X\text{ and }f\circ g\simeq \operatorname{id}_Y

nodes.title: The Real Line Is Homotopy Equivalent to a Point
nodes.originalText:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then for any 𝑝 ∈ ℝ, the map 𝑓 ∶ ℝ → {𝑝} given by 𝑓 (𝑥) = 𝑝 is a homotopy equivalence with homotopy inverse 𝑔 ∶ {𝑝} → ℝ given by 𝑔(𝑝) = 𝑝. It is clear that 𝑓 is not a homeomorphism. In fact, there are no homeomorphisms between ℝ and {𝑝}.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 77

Then for any 𝑝 ∈ ℝ, the map 𝑓 ∶ ℝ → {𝑝} given by 𝑓 (𝑥) = 𝑝 is a homotopy equivalence with homotopy inverse 𝑔 ∶ {𝑝} → ℝ given by 𝑔(𝑝) = 𝑝. It is clear that 𝑓 is not a homeomorphism. In fact, there are no homeomorphisms between ℝ and {𝑝}.

nodes.title: Homotopy types
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. We say that 𝑋 and 𝑌 have the same homotopy type if there is a homotopy equivalence 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 .

It is common to also refer to topological spaces of the same homotopy type as homotopy equivalent. We end this section with a result showing that topological spaces of the same homotopy type have isomorphic fundamental groups. To prove the result we will need the following lemma.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. We say that 𝑋 and 𝑌 have the same homotopy type if there is a homotopy equivalence 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 . It is common to also refer to topological spaces of the same homotopy type as homotopy equivalent. We end this section with a result showing that topological spaces of the same homotopy type have isomorphic fundamental groups. To prove the result we will need
nodes.explanation: Defines homotopy types and fixes terminology used later in homotopy, topology.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\text{ and }Y\text{ have the same homotopy type}\iff X\simeq Y

If 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a homotopy from 𝑓 to 𝑔, there is a path 𝛼 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑌 in 𝑌 from 𝑦0 to 𝑦1 given by 𝛼(𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑥0 , 𝑡) such that 𝑔∗ = 𝛼 ̂◦𝑓∗ . 𝑓∗ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) 𝛼 ̂ 𝑔∗ 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦1 )

By Lemma 7.3, 𝐻 ′ is continuous. Since

𝐻 ′ (𝑠, 0) = 𝐻(ℎ(𝑠), 1) = 𝑔(ℎ(𝑠))

and ⎧𝛼(4𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕4 ′ ⎪ 𝐻 (𝑠, 1) = ⎨𝐻(ℎ(4𝑠 − 1), 0) 1∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ⎪𝛼(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 ⎩ ⎧𝛼(4𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕4 ⎪ = ⎨𝑓 (ℎ(4𝑠 − 1)) 1∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ⎪𝛼(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 ⎩

for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, and 𝐻 ′ (0, 𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑥0 , 1) = 𝑦1 and 𝐻 ′ (1, 𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑥0 , 1) = 𝑦1 , it follows that 𝐻 ′ is a path homotopy from 𝑔◦ℎ to 𝛼 ∗ (𝑓 ◦ℎ) ∗ 𝛼. Thus [𝑔◦ℎ] = [𝛼 ∗ (𝑓 ◦ℎ) ∗ 𝛼]. This completes the proof.

nodes.title: Homotopy Equivalences Induce Fundamental Group Isomorphisms
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a homotopy equivalence such that 𝑓 (𝑥0 ) = 𝑦0 . Then

𝑓∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 )

is an isomorphism.

Proof. Let 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 be a homotopy inverse of 𝑓 with 𝑔(𝑦0 ) = 𝑥1 , and let 𝑓 (𝑥1 ) = 𝑦1 . By assump- tion, we have 𝑔◦𝑓 ≃ id𝑋 and 𝑓 ◦𝑔 ≃ id𝑌 where id𝑋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 and id𝑌 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑌 are the identity maps of 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively. Thus, by Lemma 7.37, there is a path 𝛼 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 in 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 and a path 𝛽 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑌 from 𝑦0 to 𝑦1 such that

(𝑔◦(𝑓𝑥0 ))∗ = 𝑔∗ ◦(𝑓𝑥0 )∗ = 𝛼 ̂◦(id𝑋 )∗ = 𝛼 ̂

and

̂ 𝑌 )∗ = 𝛽̂ ((𝑓𝑥1 )◦𝑔)∗ = (𝑓𝑥1 )∗ ◦𝑔∗ = 𝛽◦(id

where we have specified the basepoint for the homomorphisms induced by 𝑓 . In other words, the following two diagrams commute. (id𝑋 )∗ 𝑔∗ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) 𝛼 ̂ (𝑓𝑥1 )∗ 𝑔∗ ◦(𝑓𝑥0 )∗ 𝛽̂ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦1 )

Since 𝛼̂ and 𝛽̂ are both isomorphisms, it follows by commutativity of the diagram on the left that 𝑔∗ is an epimorphism, and by commutativity of the diagram on the right that 𝑔∗ is a monomorphism. Hence, 𝑔∗ is an isomorphism. Since (𝑓𝑥0 )∗ = (𝑔∗ )−1 ◦̂ 𝛼 it follows that (𝑓𝑥0 )∗ is an isomorphism.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 79

We can use Theorem 7.38 to help us determine the fundamental group of certain topological spaces, and as a tool to determine that certain topological spaces are not of the same homotopy type, and hence, not homeo- morphic.

7.4 Exercises

Exercise 7.1 Let 𝑋, 𝑌 and 𝑍 be topological spaces. Show that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is homotopic to 𝑓 ′ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑍 is homotopic to 𝑔 ′ ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑍, then 𝑔◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑍 is homotopic to 𝑔 ′ ◦𝑓 ′ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑍.

Exercise 7.2 Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Show that 𝑋 is path-connected if and only if every two constant maps 𝑐1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 and 𝑐2 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 are homotopic.

Exercise 7.3 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 and 𝑥2 be points in 𝑋. Show that if 𝛼 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path in 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , 𝛽 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path in 𝑋 from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 and 𝛾 = 𝛼 ∗ 𝛽, ̂ 𝛼. then 𝛾̂ = 𝛽◦̂

Exercise 7.4 Let 𝑆 2 be the 2-sphere considered to be a subspace of ℝ3 with the standard topology. Show that 𝜋1 (𝑆 2 ⧵ {(0, 0, 1)}, 𝑠) is the trivial group where 𝑠 = (0, 0, −1).

Exercise 7.5 Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Show that if 𝐴 is a retract of 𝑋, then 𝐴 is a closed subset of 𝑋.

Exercise 7.6 Show that the relation of homotopy equivalence is an equivalence relation on any set of topological spaces.

A topological space 𝑋 is said to be contractible if the identity map of 𝑋, id𝑋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋, is nullho- motopic.

Exercise 7.7 Show that a topological space 𝑋 is contractible if and only if 𝑋 has the homotopy type of a point, i.e., a topological space consisting of one point.

Exercise 7.8 Let 𝑋 be a topological space and let 𝑆 1 be the unit circle considered as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Show that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑆 1 is a continuous map that is not surjective, then 𝑓 is nullhomotopic.

8. The fundamental group of the circle

8.1 Covering spaces The notion of a covering space is a helpful tool for determining fundamental groups. Specifically, we will compute the fundamental group of the circle by first establishing that the map 𝑝 ∶ ℝ → 𝑆 1 , given by 𝑝(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)),

is a covering map.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a homotopy equivalence such that 𝑓 (𝑥0 ) = 𝑦0 . Then 𝑓∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 )
nodes.explanation: States a main result about homotopy equivalences induce fundamental group isomorphisms; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y\text{ homotopy equivalence}\Longrightarrow f_*:\pi_1(X,x_0)\xrightarrow{\cong}\pi_1(Y,f(x_0))

nodes.title: Covering spaces
nodes.sectionTitle: The fundamental group of the circle
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces. We say that a surjective continuous map 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map if for each point 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵 there is a neighborhood 𝑈 such that 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) is a disjoint union of open subsets 𝑉𝜆 of 𝐸 where 𝜆 ∈ Λ, ⨆ 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑉𝜆 , 𝜆∈Λ

and 𝑝|𝑉𝜆 ∶ 𝑉𝜆 → 𝑈 is a homeomorphism for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. We refer to 𝐸 as a covering space of 𝐵.

A covering map 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is sometimes also referred to as a covering projection. Note that 𝐵 is often referred to as the base space of the covering map and that 𝐸 is often referred to as the total space of the covering map. An open subset 𝑈 of 𝐵 satisfying the condition that ⨆ 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑉𝜆 , where 𝑉𝜆 is an open subset of 𝐸, 𝜆∈Λ

such that 𝑝|𝑉𝜆 ∶ 𝑉𝜆 → 𝑈 is a homeomorphism for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ is said to be evenly covered by 𝑝. See Figure 8.1. For each 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵, we often refer to the preimage 𝑝−1 (𝑏) of {𝑏} under 𝑝 as the fiber over 𝑏. Note also that the fiber over 𝑏 is a discrete subspace of 𝐸; each 𝑉𝜆 is an open subset of 𝐸 and intersects the fiber over 𝑏 in a single point, and so, this point is open in the fiber over 𝑏. Finally, we note that a covering map is a special case of a fiber bundle with discrete fibers.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces. We say that a surjective continuous map 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map if for each point 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵 there is a neighborhood 𝑈 such that 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) is a disjoint union of open subsets 𝑉𝜆 of 𝐸 where 𝜆 ∈ Λ, ⨆ 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑉𝜆 , 𝜆∈Λ and 𝑝|𝑉𝜆 ∶ 𝑉𝜆 → 𝑈 is a homeomorphism for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. We refer to 𝐸 as a covering space of 𝐵.
nodes.explanation: Defines covering spaces and fixes terminology used later in continuity, covering, maps.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: p:E\to B\text{ is a covering map}\iff p\text{ is surjective, continuous, and }\forall b\in B\ \exists U\ni b:\ p^{-1}(U)=\bigsqcup_{\lambda\in\Lambda}V_\lambda,\ p|_{V_\lambda}:V_\lambda\to U\text{ is a homeomorphism}

nodes.title: Homeomorphisms Are Covering Maps
nodes.sectionTitle: The fundamental group of the circle
nodes.originalText: Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces. If 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a homeomorphism, then 𝑝 is also a covering map.
nodes.formalStatement: Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces. If 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a homeomorphism, then 𝑝 is also a covering map.

nodes.title: Projection onto a Factor with Discrete Fiber Is a Covering Map
nodes.sectionTitle: The fundamental group of the circle
nodes.originalText:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐷 be a discrete space. Then the projection map onto the first factor, pr 1 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐷 → 𝑋, where 𝑋 × 𝐷 is given the product topology, is a covering map. In particular, 𝑋 is evenly covered by pr 1 .
